<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an algorithm. List and explain any four essential characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An algorithm is a finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous instructions that takes some input, processes it, and produces the desired output in a finite amount of time.

> **Four characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Input:** It accepts zero or more well-specified inputs.
> 2. **Output:** It produces at least one output that is a function of the input.
> 3. **Definiteness:** Every instruction is clear and unambiguous — only one interpretation is possible.
> 4. **Finiteness:** The algorithm terminates after a finite number of steps; it cannot run indefinitely.
> 5. **Effectiveness:** Each step is simple enough to be carried out exactly and in finite time.

*(Any four correct ones will be awarded full 2 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between an algorithm and a flowchart. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Algorithm | Flowchart |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Step-by-step written procedure in natural language or pseudocode | Pictorial/diagrammatic representation using standard symbols |
| **Ease of use** | Easy to write for complex logic | Easier to visualize control flow |
| **Branching** | Shown using IF-ELSE / WHILE keywords | Shown using diamond decision symbols and arrows |
| **Standardization** | No fixed symbols; pseudocode keywords vary | Standard symbols (oval, rectangle, diamond, parallelogram) |
| **Conversion to code** | Directly convertible | Needs translation into text first |
| **Best for** | Implementation | Documentation and teaching |

*(Any 3 correct differences = 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split typically as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is provided within the module.*

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the stepwise procedure for developing an algorithm. Illustrate your answer with a suitable example.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Analysis (1.5 marks):**
Identify the inputs, expected outputs, and the underlying computational rule. The goal is to fully *understand* the problem before any solution attempt.

**Step 2 — Sample Instance (1 mark):**
Choose concrete test cases (including edge cases) and manually compute the expected outputs. These serve as *ground truth* for verification.

**Step 3 — Strategy Selection (1 mark):**
Decide the algorithmic approach — iterative, recursive, divide-and-conquer, greedy, etc.

**Step 4 — Stepwise Refinement (1.5 marks):**
Decompose the problem from a high-level outline into progressively finer sub-steps until each step is a *primitive instruction* (i.e., a step the executor can perform directly).

**Step 5 — Representation (1 mark):**
Express the algorithm in pseudocode / flowchart with standard notation.

**Step 6 — Verification and Analysis (1 mark):**
Dry-run the algorithm on the sample inputs and analyze its time/space complexity using Big-O notation.

**Example Illustration (1 mark included above):**
Consider computing the *factorial* of $N$, i.e., $N! = 1 \times 2 \times 3 \times \dots \times N$.

**Pseudocode:**

```text
ALGORITHM: Factorial
INPUT: N
OUTPUT: N!

STEP 1: START
STEP 2: READ N
STEP 3: SET fact = 1
STEP 4: SET i = 1
STEP 5: WHILE i ≤ N DO
           fact = fact * i
           i    = i + 1
        END WHILE
STEP 6: PRINT fact
STEP 7: END
```

**Dry run for $N = 4$:** $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 24$ ✓
**Complexity:** $T(n) = O(n)$ (single loop of $N$ iterations).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an algorithm to find the GCD of two numbers using Euclid's algorithm. Write its pseudocode and explain with a dry-run table.

**Model Solution:**

**Strategy (1 mark):** Euclid's algorithm uses the identity $\gcd(A, B) = \gcd(B, A \bmod B)$ with base case $\gcd(A, 0) = A$.

**Pseudocode (2 marks):**

```text
ALGORITHM: Euclidean_GCD
INPUT : Two positive integers A, B
OUTPUT: GCD of A and B

STEP 1: START
STEP 2: READ A, B
STEP 3: WHILE B ≠ 0 DO
           TEMP = B
           B    = A mod B
           A    = TEMP
        END WHILE
STEP 4: PRINT A
STEP 5: END
```

**Dry Run Table for $A = 48$, $B = 18$ (3 marks):**

| Iteration | $A$ (in) | $B$ (in) | $A \bmod B$ | $A$ (out) | $B$ (out) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 48 | 18 | 12 | 18 | 12 |
| 2 | 18 | 12 | 6 | 12 | 6 |
| 3 | 12 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 |
| 4 | Loop exits (since $B = 0$) | — | — | — | — |

**Final Output: $\gcd(48, 18) = 6$** ✓

**Complexity (1 mark):** $T(A, B) = O(\log \min(A, B))$ — logarithmic in the smaller operand.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the various ways to represent an algorithm? Compare pseudocode and flowchart representation with an example.

**Model Solution:**

**Three representations (1.5 marks):**

1. **Natural language** — plain English description.
2. **Flowchart** — geometric diagram using standardized symbols.
3. **Pseudocode** — structured English resembling a programming language.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Pseudocode | Flowchart |
|---|---|---|
| Notation | Textual keywords (READ, IF, WHILE) | Geometric shapes (rectangle, diamond) |
| Readability | Excellent for programmers | Excellent for visual learners |
| Implementation | Direct translation to code | Needs text conversion first |
| Maintenance | Easy to edit | Tedious to redraw |
| Loop representation | `WHILE ... DO` block | Arrow loop-back pattern |
| Decision representation | `IF condition THEN ... ELSE ...` | Diamond shape with two outgoing arrows |

**Example (3.5 marks) — Algorithm to check whether a number is even or odd:**

**Pseudocode:**
```text
ALGORITHM: Even_Odd_Check
INPUT: A number N
OUTPUT: "Even" or "Odd"

STEP 1: START
STEP 2: READ N
STEP 3: IF N mod 2 = 0 THEN
           PRINT "Even"
        ELSE
           PRINT "Odd"
        END IF
STEP 4: END
```

**Flowchart representation:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 (the same control-flow pattern applies — a decision diamond with two branches).

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ — a single modulo operation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Develop an algorithm to find the largest among $N$ numbers stored in a list. Write the pseudocode, give a dry-run trace, and state its time complexity.

**Model Solution:**

**Problem Analysis (1 mark):** Input — $N$ numbers. Output — the maximum value $M$ in the list.

**Strategy (1 mark):** Single linear scan maintaining a running maximum.

**Pseudocode (2 marks):**

```text
ALGORITHM: Find_Maximum
INPUT : N and N numbers x_1, x_2, ..., x_N
OUTPUT: The largest value M

STEP 1: START
STEP 2: READ N
STEP 3: READ first number into M
STEP 4: SET count = 1
STEP 5: WHILE count < N DO
           READ next number x
           IF x > M THEN
               M = x
           END IF
           count = count + 1
        END WHILE
STEP 6: PRINT M
STEP 7: END
```

**Dry Run for $N = 5$, numbers = $\{12, 45, 7, 89, 23\}$ (2 marks):**

| Step | $x$ read | $M$ before | $x > M$? | $M$ after | count |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | 12 | — | — | 12 | 1 |
| 1 | 45 | 12 | Yes | 45 | 2 |
| 2 | 7 | 45 | No | 45 | 3 |
| 3 | 89 | 45 | Yes | 89 | 4 |
| 4 | 23 | 89 | No | 89 | 5 |

**Final Output: $M = 89$** ✓

**Time Complexity (1 mark):** The loop runs $N - 1$ times doing $O(1)$ work per iteration, so

$$
T(n) = 1 + (n - 1) = n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T(n) = O(n)
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Frequently Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Algorithm with Program** — An algorithm is *language-independent*; a program is its *language-specific implementation*. Writing Python code when the question asks for an *algorithm* loses you the pseudocode/flowchart marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Start" and "End" terminators** — Every KTU-board algorithm and flowchart must begin with `START` and end with `END`. Examiners explicitly allocate 0.5–1 mark for this convention.
> 3. **Skipping the Dry Run / Verification step** — A beautiful algorithm with no verification trace is worth only **partial marks**. Always include a sample-input trace table.
> 4. **Incorrect indentation in pseudocode** — KTU expects nested blocks (inside IF/WHILE) to be visually indented. A flat linear block loses presentation marks.
> 5. **Mis-stating Complexity** — Writing “time complexity is $n$” instead of the *Big-O form* $O(n)$. Examiners insist on proper asymptotic notation.
> 6. **Ignoring Edge Cases** — What happens if $N = 0$? What if all numbers are negative? Mentioning edge-case handling fetches you the *extra mile* marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **algorithm** is a *finite*, *unambiguous*, *effective* sequence of steps that transforms input into output.
- ✅ The **5 essential properties** (Knuth) are: **Input, Output, Definiteness, Finiteness, Effectiveness** — memorize as **"I-O-D-F-E"**.
- ✅ Algorithm development is a **6-step iterative process**: Analyze → Sample → Strategy → Refine → Represent → Verify.
- ✅ **Three representations:** Natural language, Flowchart, Pseudocode. Pseudocode is the most KTU-board-friendly.
- ✅ **Standard flowchart symbols:** Oval (Start/End), Parallelogram (I/O), Rectangle (Process), Diamond (Decision), Arrows (Flow).
- ✅ **Stepwise refinement** = top-down decomposition until every step is a *primitive instruction*.
- ✅ **Euclid's GCD algorithm** uses the identity $\gcd(A, B) = \gcd(B, A \bmod B)$ with complexity $O(\log n)$.
- ✅ **Linear-scan algorithms** (sum, max, search) have complexity $O(n)$.
- ✅ Always **dry-run** your algorithm on a sample input and **tabulate** the trace — this single step can earn 2–3 bonus marks.
- ✅ Always state **time complexity** using **Big-O notation** $O(\cdot)$.
- ✅ Always begin pseudocode with `START` and end with `END` — non-negotiable KTU convention.
- ✅ An algorithm is **not a program** — it is a *blueprint*; the program is the building constructed from it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Writing the Program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Writing the Program

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (UCEST105 – Algorithmic Thinking with Python)**, the topic **"Writing the Program"** refers to the practical translation of a well-defined algorithm into a syntactically correct, logically sound, and executable sequence of instructions using the **Python programming language**. It is the bridge that connects *algorithmic logic* (the "what to do") with *machine execution* (the "how to do it").

A **program** in Python is a plain text file (typically with the `.py` extension) containing a finite, ordered collection of **statements** and **expressions** that the **Python Interpreter** reads, parses, and executes line-by-line (or rather, statement-by-statement).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1)**
> "Writing the Program" is the third stage of the **Program Development Life Cycle (PDLC)**. It follows *Problem Analysis* and *Algorithm Design*, and precedes *Testing & Debugging*. At this stage, the student must convert the abstract algorithm into concrete Python code, choosing appropriate data types, control structures, and I/O mechanisms.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a **robot chef** to make a cup of tea. The robot knows *nothing* about the world — it cannot guess, assume, or improvise.

- **Algorithm** = The recipe written in plain English on paper: *"Boil water. Add tea leaves. Wait 3 minutes. Strain into cup. Add sugar."*
- **Writing the Program** = Teaching that same recipe to the robot in its **native language** (Python), using exact grammar, exact tools, and exact commands it understands.

The "robot" here is the **Python Interpreter**. The "recipe" is the **Source Code**. The moment you press "Run," the chef robot executes your instructions *literally* — not what you *meant*, but what you *wrote*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this stage matters:** A brilliant algorithm written incorrectly in code will *fail silently or loudly*. KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate the ability to **write clean, syntactically valid Python code** that directly implements a given algorithm. Marks are heavily allocated to **code correctness, indentation discipline, and proper use of `print()` / `input()`**.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Source Code:** The human-readable text file containing Python instructions (extension: **`.py`**).
- **Bytecode:** The intermediate, low-level representation (extension: **`.pyc`**) automatically generated by the interpreter.
- **Statement:** A single executable unit in Python (terminated by a newline, not a semicolon).
- **Indentation Unit:** Standardized to **4 spaces** per logical level in Python.
- **Reserved Words (Keywords):** Currently **35 keywords** in Python 3.11+ (cannot be used as variable names).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Program Development Life Cycle (PDLC) — Where "Writing the Program" Fits

The act of writing a program is **not** the first step. It is the third step in a structured engineering process:

1. **Problem Definition** — Understand *what* must be solved.
2. **Algorithm Design** — Write the step-by-step logic (pseudocode / flowchart).
3. **➤ Writing the Program** — Translate the algorithm into Python source code.
4. **Testing & Debugging** — Run the program, fix errors (syntax / logical / runtime).
5. **Documentation** — Add comments and external docs.
6. **Maintenance** — Update for changing requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks "Explain the steps in writing a program," examiners expect you to mention the **PDLC context** first, then drill into the mechanics of step 3.

---

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Minimal Python Program

A Python program is composed of the following building blocks, written in this typical order:

1. **Shebang / Encoding line** (optional): `#!/usr/bin/env python3`
2. **Module Docstring** (optional but recommended): A triple-quoted string describing the program.
3. **Import statements** (if external libraries are needed).
4. **Constant / Global variable declarations**.
5. **Function definitions** (for modular code).
6. **Main program logic** (statements, I/O, calculations).
7. **Entry point check**: `if __name__ == "__main__":`

The simplest valid Python program is just **one line**:
```python
print("Hello, KTU!")
```

---

### 2.3 The Two Execution Modes in Python

| Mode | Command | Use Case | KTU Relevance |
|------|---------|----------|---------------|
| **Interactive Mode** | Type `python` in terminal | Testing snippets, quick calculations | Rarely tested directly |
| **Script Mode** | `python filename.py` | Writing full programs (saved in `.py` file) | **The default KTU expectation** |

---

### 2.4 Core Components You Must Master to "Write the Program"

#### A. Keywords
Reserved words with predefined meanings. **Cannot** be used as identifiers.

Examples: `if`, `else`, `elif`, `for`, `while`, `def`, `return`, `import`, `True`, `False`, `None`, `and`, `or`, `not`, `in`, `is`, `pass`, `break`, `continue`.

#### B. Identifiers
Names you give to variables, functions, classes. Rules:
- Must start with a **letter (A–Z, a–z)** or **underscore (`_`)**.
- Can contain letters, digits, and underscores.
- **Case-sensitive** (`Age` ≠ `age`).
- Cannot be a keyword.

#### C. Variables
A named reference to a value stored in memory. Python is **dynamically typed** — you don't declare the type explicitly.

```python
marks = 95           # int
name = "Anand"       # str
pi = 3.14159         # float
is_pass = True       # bool
```

#### D. Comments
- **Single-line:** Start with `#`
- **Multi-line:** Enclosed in triple quotes `"""` or `'''` (technically a string, but used as a docstring/comment at module level).

#### E. Statements vs Expressions
- **Expression:** Produces a value → `2 + 3`, `x * y`
- **Statement:** Performs an action → `x = 5`, `print("Hi")`, `if x > 0:`

#### F. Basic Input / Output
- **Output:** `print(value1, value2, ..., sep=' ', end='\n')`
- **Input:** `variable = input("Prompt: ")` — **always returns a string**. Type conversion via `int()`, `float()`, etc., is required for numeric input.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax | Example | Notes |
|-----------|--------|---------|-------|
| Print | `print(args)` | `print("Sum =", x+y)` | Default `sep=' '`, `end='\n'` |
| Input | `var = input(prompt)` | `n = int(input("Enter n: "))` | Returns **str** → cast explicitly |
| Assignment | `var = value` | `count = 0` | Single `=` |
| Multiple Assignment | `a, b = 1, 2` | `x, y = y, x` (swap) | Tuple unpacking |
| Augmented Assignment | `var op= value` | `total += 5` | Same as `total = total + 5` |
| Comment | `# text` | `# This is a comment` | Single line |
| Docstring | `"""text"""` | `"""Program to add two numbers"""` | Triple-quoted, multi-line |
| Indentation | 4 spaces per level | See below | **Mandatory**, not optional |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ability to **write a clean, executable program** is the foundational skill for:
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Writing microcontroller scripts.
- **Data Science:** Prototyping algorithms before production deployment.
- **Automation:** Replacing repetitive manual tasks in industry.
- **Scientific Computing:** Simulating physical systems.
- **Web Backends:** Building server logic (Django, Flask).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme lab examinations, "Writing the Program" constitutes **60–70% of the marks** for any given problem statement. The remaining marks are for *output verification, viva, and documentation*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Converting an Algorithm into a Python Program

**Problem Statement (Algorithm given):**
> *Algorithm: Read two integers from the user, compute their sum, and display the result.*

This is the **canonical KTU Module 1 problem**. Let us now perform an exhaustive, step-by-step translation.

---

#### Step 1: Analyze the Algorithm Components

From the algorithm text, we can identify:
- **Inputs needed:** Two integers (call them `a` and `b`).
- **Process:** Arithmetic addition: $S = a + b$.
- **Output needed:** The value of $S$.

---

#### Step 2: Map Each Algorithm Step to a Python Construct

| Algorithm Step | Python Construct | Reasoning |
|----------------|------------------|-----------|
| Read integer `a` | `a = int(input("Enter first number: "))` | `input()` returns string → `int()` cast needed |
| Read integer `b` | `b = int(input("Enter second number: "))` | Same as above |
| Compute $S = a + b$ | `s = a + b` | Direct arithmetic operator |
| Display $S$ | `print("Sum =", s)` | `print()` with comma-separated args |

---

#### Step 3: Assemble the Complete Python Program

```python
# Program: add_two_numbers.py
# Author: KTU Student
# Date: Module 1 Demonstration
# Description: Reads two integers and prints their sum.

# Step 1: Get input from the user
a = int(input("Enter first number: "))
b = int(input("Enter second number: "))

# Step 2: Compute the sum
s = a + b

# Step 3: Display the result
print("Sum =", s)
```

---

#### Step 4: Trace Through the Execution (Dry Run)

Assume the user enters `7` and `8`.

| Line # | Statement Executed | State of Memory | Output |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|--------|
| 7 | `a = int(input("Enter first number: "))` | `a = 7` (int) | Prompts: `Enter first number: ` |
| 8 | `b = int(input("Enter second number: "))` | `b = 8` (int) | Prompts: `Enter second number: ` |
| 11 | `s = a + b` | `s = 15` (int) | — (no output) |
| 14 | `print("Sum =", s)` | unchanged | `Sum = 15` |

The mathematical operation performed is:
$$
S = a + b = 7 + 8 = 15
$$

---

#### Step 5: Verification of Correctness

The output `Sum = 15` matches the expected result of $7 + 8 = 15$. The program is **logically and syntactically correct**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Module 1 Translations

| Algorithm Phrase | Python Equivalent | Key Pitfall |
|------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| "Read a number" | `x = int(input())` | Forgetting `int()` cast |
| "Read a name" | `name = input()` | No cast needed (string is default) |
| "Display the value" | `print(x)` | — |
| "Display with a label" | `print("Result =", x)` | Mixing commas vs `+` |
| "Swap two values" | `a, b = b, a` | Using a temp variable is also valid |
| "Initialize to zero" | `count = 0` | Cannot use `0` as a variable name |
| "Wait for nothing" | `pass` or `...` | Used in empty code blocks |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Writing a Program with a Conditional

**Algorithm:** *Read a number. If it is even, print "Even"; otherwise, print "Odd".*

#### Python Implementation

```python
# Program: even_odd.py
# Description: Determines whether a number is even or odd.

n = int(input("Enter an integer: "))

if n % 2 == 0:
    print("Even")
else:
    print("Odd")
```

#### Mathematical Logic Behind the Modulo Operation

A number $n$ is **even** if and only if $n$ leaves a remainder of $0$ when divided by $2$. This can be expressed as:

$$
\text{IsEven}(n) = 
\begin{cases} 
\text{True} & \text{if } n \bmod 2 = 0 \\
\text{False} & \text{if } n \bmod 2 \neq 0 
\end{cases}
$$

In Python, the `%` operator returns the remainder. Thus, `n % 2 == 0` evaluates to `True` for even $n$.

#### Dry Run

| Input (n) | n % 2 | n % 2 == 0 | Branch Taken | Output |
|-----------|-------|-------------|----------------|--------|
| 10 | 0 | True | `if` branch | `Even` |
| 7 | 1 | False | `else` branch | `Odd` |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Writing a Program with a Loop

**Algorithm:** *Print the numbers from 1 to N, where N is given by the user.*

```python
# Program: print_1_to_n.py

n = int(input("Enter N: "))

for i in range(1, n + 1):
    print(i)
```

**Key Insight:** `range(1, n + 1)` generates the sequence $1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$ because the stop value in `range()` is **exclusive**. Using `range(1, n)` would stop at $n-1$, which is a common KTU error.

$$
\text{Range}(start, stop) = \{ x \in \mathbb{Z} \mid start \leq x < stop \}
$$

For `range(1, n+1)` with $n = 5$:
$$
\{ x \in \mathbb{Z} \mid 1 \leq x < 6 \} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Program Development Life Cycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition] --> B[Algorithm Design]
    B --> C[Writing the Program]
    C --> D[Testing and Debugging]
    D --> E{All Tests Pass?}
    E -->|No| F[Debug and Fix]
    F --> C
    E -->|Yes| G[Documentation]
    G --> H[Maintenance]
    H --> I[Software in Production]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Python Program Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Python Source Code .py] --> lex[Lexer]
    lex --> tokens[Token Stream]
    tokens --> parse[Parser]
    parse --> ast[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    ast --> comp[Compiler to Bytecode]
    comp --> bc[Bytecode .pyc]
    bc --> vm[Python Virtual Machine PVM]
    vm --> out[Program Output]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Anatomy of a Python Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Python Program File .py] --> S1[1 Shebang Line optional]
    P --> S2[2 Module Docstring]
    P --> S3[3 Import Statements]
    P --> S4[4 Global Variable Declarations]
    P --> S5[5 Function Definitions def blocks]
    P --> S6[6 Main Logic Statements I/O, conditions, loops]
    P --> S7[7 Entry Point Check if name equals main]
```

---

### 4.4 Flowchart: Translating an Algorithm into a Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> readAlg[Read the Algorithm Text]
    readAlg --> idIO[Identify Inputs and Outputs]
    idIO --> mapPy[Map Each Step to Python Construct]
    mapPy --> code[Write Source Code in .py File]
    code --> indent[Apply Correct Indentation 4 spaces]
    indent --> syntax[Syntax Check]
    syntax --> run[Run via python filename.py]
    run --> result{Output Correct?}
    result -->|No| debug[Debug and Edit Source]
    debug --> code
    result -->|Yes| done([Program Complete])
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define a program. List any four characteristics of a good program.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **program** is a finite, ordered sequence of well-defined instructions written in a programming language (such as Python) that, when executed by a computer, performs a specific task or solves a given problem.

**Four characteristics of a good program:**

1. **Correctness** — It produces the right output for all valid inputs.
2. **Readability** — It uses meaningful identifiers, proper indentation, and comments so that others (and the original author) can understand it later.
3. **Efficiency** — It uses minimal memory and CPU time to achieve the result.
4. **Maintainability** — It is modular (broken into functions) and documented, so future modifications are easy.

*(Any 4 valid characteristics = full 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between Script Mode and Interactive Mode of Python execution.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Interactive Mode | Script Mode |
|--------|------------------|-------------|
| **Invocation** | Type `python` in the terminal | Save code in a `.py` file and run `python file.py` |
| **Execution** | One statement at a time; result shown immediately | All statements executed together |
| **Use Case** | Testing small snippets, debugging, learning | Writing full programs for submission |
| **Persistence** | Code is lost when the session ends | Code is saved permanently in a file |
| **KTU Preference** | Rarely accepted in exams | **The standard mode for KTU lab and theory exams** |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the different steps involved in writing a Python program. Illustrate with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to read the radius of a circle from the user and print its area and circumference. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for (a):

The steps involved in writing a Python program are:

1. **Understand the Problem:** Identify inputs, required outputs, and the relationship between them.
2. **Design the Algorithm:** Write a step-by-step logic in plain English or pseudocode.
3. **Identify the Required Constructs:** Decide which Python features (variables, `input()`, `print()`, `if`, `for`, etc.) are needed.
4. **Write the Source Code:** Open a text editor (or IDE like IDLE / PyCharm) and write the code in a file with a `.py` extension.
5. **Apply Proper Indentation:** Use **4 spaces per level** — Python does not use braces `{}` to define blocks.
6. **Add Comments and Docstrings:** Use `#` for single-line and `""" """` for multi-line comments.
7. **Save and Execute:** Run the program using `python filename.py` in the terminal.
8. **Verify Output and Debug:** Compare actual output with expected output. Fix any errors.

**Example Illustration:**

```python
# Greet the user
name = input("Enter your name: ")
print("Hello,", name, "! Welcome to KTU.")
```

Here, the algorithm *"ask the user for their name and greet them"* is implemented in three lines.

---

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Algorithm:**
- **Input:** Radius $r$ of the circle (float).
- **Process:** Compute area $A = \pi r^2$ and circumference $C = 2 \pi r$.
- **Output:** Display $A$ and $C$.

**Python Program:**

```python
# Program: circle_calc.py
# Description: Computes area and circumference of a circle.

import math  # Provides the value of pi

# Step 1: Read the radius
r = float(input("Enter the radius of the circle: "))

# Step 2: Compute area and circumference
area = math.pi * r * r
circumference = 2 * math.pi * r

# Step 3: Display the results
print("Area of the circle =", area)
print("Circumference of the circle =", circumference)
```

**Sample Run:**

For input $r = 5$:
$$
A = \pi \times 5^2 = 25\pi \approx 78.54
$$
$$
C = 2 \times \pi \times 5 = 10\pi \approx 31.42
$$

**Output:**
```
Area of the circle = 78.53981633974483
Circumference of the circle = 31.41592653589793
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the role of variables, keywords, and comments in writing a Python program with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to read three numbers from the user and print the largest among them. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Variables:**
Variables are named memory locations that store data values. In Python, variables are created the moment a value is assigned to them — no explicit declaration is required. Python is **dynamically typed**, so the type is inferred at runtime.

```python
age = 20           # int
name = "Rahul"     # str
height = 5.8       # float
```

**Rules for variable names (identifiers):**
- Must begin with a letter or underscore.
- Cannot be a Python keyword.
- Are case-sensitive.

**2. Keywords:**
Keywords are **reserved words** that have special meaning in Python's syntax. They **cannot** be used as variable names.

```python
# Valid
count = 10
_class = "KTU"

# INVALID — SyntaxError
if = 5       # 'if' is a keyword
class = "A"  # 'class' is a keyword
```

Common keywords: `if`, `else`, `for`, `while`, `def`, `return`, `True`, `False`, `None`, `import`, `in`, `not`, `and`, `or`.

**3. Comments:**
Comments are non-executable lines that explain the code. They improve readability and are ignored by the interpreter.

- **Single-line comment:** Uses `#`.
- **Multi-line comment / docstring:** Uses triple quotes `"""` or `'''`.

```python
# This is a single-line comment
"""
This is a multi-line
comment or docstring.
"""
```

---

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Algorithm:**
- Read three numbers $a$, $b$, $c$ from the user.
- Compare them to find the maximum.
- Print the result.

**Method 1: Using `if-elif-else`**

```python
# Program: largest_of_three.py

a = int(input("Enter first number: "))
b = int(input("Enter second number: "))
c = int(input("Enter third number: "))

if a >= b and a >= c:
    largest = a
elif b >= a and b >= c:
    largest = b
else:
    largest = c

print("The largest number is =", largest)
```

**Method 2: Using the built-in `max()` function** *(more Pythonic)*

```python
a = int(input("Enter first number: "))
b = int(input("Enter second number: "))
c = int(input("Enter third number: "))

largest = max(a, b, c)
print("The largest number is =", largest)
```

**Sample Run:**

For inputs $a = 12$, $b = 45$, $c = 7$:
$$
\max(12, 45, 7) = 45
$$

**Output:**
```
The largest number is = 45
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `int()` or `float()` cast on `input()`** → `TypeError` at runtime. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Inconsistent indentation** → `IndentationError`. Python is whitespace-sensitive. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Using a keyword as a variable name** (e.g., `if = 5`) → `SyntaxError`. (Lose 0.5–1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing `print(a + b)` with `print("a + b")`** → The former prints the sum; the latter prints the literal string. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Missing import for `math.pi`** → `NameError: name 'pi' is not defined`. Always use `math.pi` or define `pi = 3.14159`. (Lose 0.5 mark)
> 6. **Not including a sample input/output in the answer** → KTU expects a **dry run / trace** for full marks on code questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Program** = a set of instructions written in a programming language and executed by a computer.
- ✅ The **PDLC** order is: *Problem → Algorithm → **Write Program** → Test → Document → Maintain*.
- ✅ Python files use the **`.py`** extension and are run via `python filename.py`.
- ✅ **`input()` always returns a string** — cast with `int()`, `float()`, etc., for numeric data.
- ✅ **`print()`** supports multiple arguments separated by commas, with default `sep=' '` and `end='\n'`.
- ✅ **Keywords** are reserved — 35 of them in Python 3.11+; never use as identifiers.
- ✅ **Identifiers** are case-sensitive and must start with a letter or underscore.
- ✅ **Indentation** is **mandatory** in Python — use **4 spaces per logical level** (no tabs, no braces).
- ✅ **Comments** use `#` (single-line) or `""" """` (multi-line/docstring).
- ✅ A Python program can run in **Interactive Mode** (terminal-based) or **Script Mode** (saved file). KTU prefers Script Mode.
- ✅ **Dynamic typing** in Python means you do **not** declare variable types explicitly.
- ✅ Always provide a **sample input/output trace** when writing a program in the exam.
- ✅ The `range(start, stop)` function is **exclusive of `stop`** — `range(1, 5)` gives $1, 2, 3, 4$, not $5$.
- ✅ For `math.pi`, you must write `import math` at the top of the file.
- ✅ The `if __name__ == "__main__":` guard is the **Pythonic entry point** for standalone programs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing the Program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Testing the Program # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Program Testing** is the systematic process of executing a program with the intent of finding errors (bugs), verifying that the program behaves as intended, and validating that it meets the specified requirements. According to the IEEE Standard 829, testing is *"the process of exercising or evaluating a system or system component by manual or automated means to verify that it satisfies specified requirements or to identify differences between expected and actual results."* In the context of **Algorithmic Thinking with Python**, program testing is the disciplined activity of feeding **deliberately chosen inputs (test cases)** to a Python program and **comparing the actual output** with the **expected output** to confirm correctness. ## 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Formal Definition | |---|---| | **Bug** | An error, flaw, or fault in a program that causes incorrect behavior. | | **Debugging** | The process of locating, analyzing, and removing bugs from a program. | | **Test Case** | A set of conditions (inputs, execution preconditions, expected results) under which a tester determines whether the program satisfies its specification. | | **Test Suite** | A collection of test cases grouped together for systematic execution. | | **Validation** | Are we building the *right* program? (Checks if the program meets user needs). | | **Verification** | Are we building the program *right*? (Checks if the program meets its specification). | | **Assertion** | A boolean expression that must evaluate to **True** at a specific point in the program; otherwise an `AssertionError` is raised. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Taste Test" > [!NOTE] > **Imagine you are teaching a friend to cook a recipe for the first time.** You wouldn't just trust that the written recipe is correct — you'd watch them *actually cook it*, taste the dish, and check if it matches the expected flavor. If it tastes too salty, the recipe has a "bug." You might then ask: Was too much salt added? Was the measurement unit wrong (tablespoon vs. teaspoon)? Was the cooking time incorrect? > > **Program testing is exactly this process, but for software.** The "recipe" is your Python code, the "tasting" is running the program, and the "expected flavor" is the documented specification. Even *very experienced chefs* (programmers) make mistakes, so testing is **non-negotiable** for trustworthy software. ## 1.4 The Fundamental Truth About Testing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dijkstra's Famous Principle (EWD 268, 1969):** *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence."* This means exhaustive testing of all possible inputs is impossible for any non-trivial program. Therefore, our goal is to design a **smart, finite set of test cases** that maximizes the probability of catching defects. ## 1.5 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Scheme) - Understand the **types of testing** appropriate for algorithmic code. - Learn to design **effective test cases** covering boundary, normal, and error conditions. - Master Python's built-in testing tools: **`assert` statements**, **`unittest` module**, and **`doctest` module**. - Differentiate between **Syntax Errors, Runtime Errors, and Logic (Semantic) Errors**. - Apply the **Defensive Programming** mindset while writing algorithms. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Program Errors (Errors Taxonomy) Every Python program can fail in one of **three fundamentally different ways**. KTU examiners *frequently* test this classification. ### 2.1.1 Syntax Errors (Compile-Time / Parse Errors) - **Detected by:** The Python interpreter **before** the program runs. - **Cause:** Violation of Python's grammatical rules (e.g., missing colon `:`, mismatched parentheses, misspelled keywords). - **Behavior:** Program does not execute at all. The interpreter prints a `SyntaxError` and the line number. - **Example:** ```python if x == 5 # SyntaxError: expected ':' print("Five") ``` ### 2.1.2 Runtime Errors (Exceptions) - **Detected by:** The interpreter **during** execution. - **Cause:** Operation that is syntactically legal but semantically impossible (e.g., division by zero, accessing a non-existent list index, type mismatch). - **Behavior:** Program starts running, then halts abruptly with a traceback. - **Common Examples:** `ZeroDivisionError`, `IndexError`, `TypeError`, `KeyError`, `ValueError`, `FileNotFoundError`. ### 2.1.3 Logic Errors (Semantic / Design Errors) - **Detected by:** **No one — except the careful tester.** The program runs to completion but produces incorrect results. - **Cause:** The programmer's algorithm itself is wrong (e.g., using `>` instead of `>=`, off-by-one error in a loop). - **Behavior:** No crash, no error message — just **wrong output**. This is the most dangerous category. - **Example:** A function intended to compute the area of a rectangle writes `length + breadth` instead of `length * breadth`. > [!NOTE] > **Why testing matters most:** Logic errors cannot be caught by the interpreter. Only **deliberate human-designed test cases** can expose them. This is the entire reason testing exists as a discipline. ## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Testing Strategies ### Strategy A: Black-Box Testing (Functional / Behavioral) - The tester **treats the program as an opaque box** — only the **inputs and outputs** are observed. - The internal code structure is **not** examined. - Test cases are derived from the **specification** (what the program is supposed to do). - **Strengths:** Detects missing functionalities, specification mismatches, and is unbiased by the implementation. - **Weaknesses:** Cannot detect code paths that are never triggered; may have redundant tests. ### Strategy B: White-Box Testing (Structural / Glass-Box) - The tester **examines the internal logic, branches, and paths** of the code. - Test cases are designed to exercise **every branch, loop boundary, and conditional path** at least once. - **Key Metrics used:** - **Statement Coverage:** Every executable line runs at least once. - **Branch Coverage:** Every `if/else` arm executes at least once. - **Path Coverage:** Every independent route through the code is exercised. - **Strengths:** Catches hidden logic errors, dead code, and untested paths. - **Weaknesses:** Misses specification errors; can be expensive for large systems. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Test-Case Design Categories > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners love asking: *"Design test cases for the function X."* A complete answer **must** cover all four categories below. | # | Category | What it Tests | Example for `sqrt(x)` | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Normal / Typical Cases** | Inputs that fall comfortably inside the valid range. | `x = 25` → expect `5` | | 2 | **Boundary / Edge Cases** | Inputs at the very edges of the valid range. | `x = 0`, `x = 1` | | 3 | **Extreme / Large Cases** | Inputs at the limits of the data type. | `x = 10**18` | | 4 | **Invalid / Error Cases** | Inputs that violate the function's preconditions. | `x = -4`, `x = "abc"` | ### The Boundary Value Principle > [!NOTE] > **Principle (KTU favourite):** *"Bugs love to hide at the boundaries."* If a function is valid for `0 ≤ x ≤ 100`, the boundaries are `x = 0` and `x = 100`. Always test the value **just below** (`-1`, `99`), **at** (`0`, `100`), and **just above** (`1`, `101`) every boundary. ## 2.4 Levels of Testing (The Testing Pyramid) From the smallest unit of code to the entire deployed system, testing is performed at four hierarchical levels. 1. **Unit Testing** — Tests the **smallest testable component** (typically a single function) in isolation. *Performed by the developer.* This is the **foundation** of the testing pyramid in Python courses. 2. **Integration Testing** — Tests the **interfaces between modules** to ensure they work together correctly. 3. **System Testing** — Tests the **complete, integrated system** against its full specification. 4. **Acceptance Testing** — Performed by the **end user / client** to determine if the system meets business needs and is ready for delivery (the famous "UAT" — User Acceptance Test). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Table | # | Concept | Symbolic / Practical Form | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of branches in a code with `n` independent `if` statements | $\text{Branches} = 2n$ | Target for branch coverage testing. | | 2 | Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe) | $M = E - N + 2P$ | Number of independent test paths. | | 3 | Statement Coverage | $\dfrac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\%$ | White-box metric (target: **100%**). | | 4 | Branch Coverage | $\dfrac{\text{Branches Tested}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%$ | White-box metric (target: **100%**). | | 5 | Test Effectiveness | $\dfrac{\text{Defects Found by Tests}}{\text{Total Defects Present}} \times 100\%$ | Quality measure of a test suite. | | 6 | Python `assert` statement | `assert , ` | Triggers `AssertionError` if condition is `False`. | | 7 | Python `unittest` discovery | `python -m unittest discover` | Runs all `test_*.py` files. | > **Notation used:** $E$ = edges of control flow graph, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (typically $P=1$ for a single function). ## 2.6 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > In **real-world software engineering**, testing is not a luxury — it is a **legal, financial, and ethical obligation**. Failures in production can cost millions (e.g., the Ariane 5 rocket crash in 1996 was due to an unhandled integer overflow — a missed runtime error). For KTU algorithms, rigorous testing ensures your submitted lab programs and exam code are **correct, robust, and trustworthy**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Walk-Through: McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity Cyclomatic complexity measures the **minimum number of independent test paths** required to traverse every edge of a program's control flow graph at least once. ### 3.1.1 The Formula Derivation The independent paths are calculated using the formula: $$ M = E - N + 2P $$ where: - $M$ = Cyclomatic complexity (number of independent paths) - $E$ = Number of edges in the control flow graph - $N$ = Number of nodes in the control flow graph - $P$ = Number of connected components (for a single function, $P = 1$) **Equivalently**, for code containing only decision points (`if`, `while`, `for`, `and`, `or`, `case`), it can be simplified to: $$ M = \pi + 1 $$ where $\pi$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes. ### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Step by Step **Python code under test:** ```python def grade(score): if score >= 90: return "A" elif score >= 75: return "B" else: return "C" ``` **Step 1 — Identify the decision points.** There are two `if` statements: `score >= 90` and `score >= 75`. So $\pi = 2$. **Step 2 — Apply the simplified formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} M &= \pi + 1 \\ &= 2 + 1 \\ &= 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret the result.** We need a **minimum of 3 independent test cases** to cover all paths. **Step 4 — Design the 3 independent test cases.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path 1 (Branch: \texttt{score} >= 90):} \quad & \text{Input } score = 95 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Expected } "A" \\ \text{Path 2 (Branch: 75 <= score < 90):} \quad & \text{Input } score = 80 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Expected } "B" \\ \text{Path 3 (Branch: score < 75):} \quad & \text{Input } score = 50 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Expected } "C" \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verify branch coverage.** $$ \text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{3 \text{ branches tested}}{3 \text{ total branches}} \times 100\% = 100\% $$ **Conclusion:** Three test cases give us 100% branch coverage. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Walk-Through: `assert` Statement Semantics ### 3.2.1 Syntax and Behavior The Python `assert` statement is a built-in debugging aid with the formal form: $$ \text{assert}\ \langle \text{expression} \rangle \ \ [\text{,}\ \langle \text{message} \rangle] $$ It is logically equivalent to: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{if } \mathbf{not}\ \text{condition:} \\ \quad \text{raise AssertionError}(\text{message}) \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Evaluation of an `assert` Block **Code:** ```python def divide(a, b): assert b != 0, "Denominator cannot be zero." return a / b print(divide(10, 2)) # Call 1 print(divide(10, 0)) # Call 2 ``` **Call 1 Analysis (`divide(10, 2)`):** - **Step 1:** Evaluate the condition `b != 0` → `2 != 0` → `True`. - **Step 2:** Since condition is `True`, the assertion passes silently. - **Step 3:** Execute the next line → `return 10 / 2` → `5.0`. - **Output:** `5.0` **Call 2 Analysis (`divide(10, 0)`):** - **Step 1:** Evaluate the condition `b != 0` → `0 != 0` → `False`. - **Step 2:** Since condition is `False`, raise `AssertionError("Denominator cannot be zero.")`. - **Step 3:** Program execution halts with a traceback. **Output Traceback (excerpt):** ``` AssertionError: Denominator cannot be zero. ``` > [!NOTE] > **Important:** Running Python with the `-O` flag (optimize) **disables all `assert` statements**. They are designed for development-time checks, not for production input validation. For production code, use explicit `raise ValueError(...)` statements instead. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Test Level Use this decision matrix when an exam question asks *"which type of testing is most appropriate for scenario X?"* | Scenario Described in Question | Correct Testing Type | Reason | |---|---|---| | Checking if a single `factorial(n)` function returns correct output for several `n`. | **Unit Testing** | Smallest testable component is the function. | | Verifying that Module A (login) and Module B (database) communicate correctly. | **Integration Testing** | Interface between two modules. | | Confirming the entire e-commerce website works end-to-end. | **System Testing** | Tests the whole integrated system. | | Final client sign-off before a product launch. | **Acceptance Testing** | End user validates against business needs. | | Designing test cases from the requirement specification without seeing code. | **Black-Box Testing** | Spec-driven, implementation-agnostic. | | Tracing every `if/else` branch to ensure each is executed. | **White-Box Testing** | Code-structure-driven. | ## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Comprehensive Test-Case Design **Function under test:** ```python def classify_number(n): """Return 'positive', 'negative', or 'zero'.""" assert isinstance(n, (int, float)), "Input must be numeric" if n > 0: return "positive" elif n < 0: return "negative" else: return "zero" ``` **Step 1 — Identify the function's contract.** - Accepts: `int` or `float`. - Returns: a string `"positive"`, `"negative"`, or `"zero"`. **Step 2 — Enumerate the equivalence partitions.** - Partition P1: $n > 0$ (positive numbers). - Partition P2: $n < 0$ (negative numbers). - Partition P3: $n = 0$ (zero). - Partition P4 (Invalid): $n$ is a non-numeric type. **Step 3 — Choose boundary values.** For real numbers, test values arbitrarily close to the boundary, e.g., `0.0001` and `-0.0001`. **Step 4 — Construct the test case table.** $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|l|} \hline \textbf{Test ID} & \textbf{Input } (n) & \textbf{Expected Output} & \textbf{Category} \\ \hline \text{T1} & 10 & \text{"positive"} & \text{Normal} \\ \text{T2} & -7 & \text{"negative"} & \text{Normal} \\ \text{T3} & 0 & \text{"zero"} & \text{Boundary} \\ \text{T4} & 0.0001 & \text{"positive"} & \text{Boundary} \\ \text{T5} & -0.0001 & \text{"negative"} & \text{Boundary} \\ \text{T6} & \text{"hello"} & \text{AssertionError} & \text{Invalid} \\ \text{T7} & 10^{18} & \text{"positive"} & \text{Extreme} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Step 5 — Compute statement and branch coverage achieved.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Statement Coverage} &= \frac{\text{All 4 code lines executed}}{\text{4 total lines}} \times 100\% = 100\% \\ \text{Branch Coverage} &= \frac{\text{All 3 branches exercised}}{\text{3 total branches}} \times 100\% = 100\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The above test suite (T1–T7) is **complete**: it covers normal, boundary, extreme, and invalid cases. In a KTU exam, you would earn full marks (7/7) for this kind of systematic design. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Testing Pyramid (Levels of Testing) ```mermaid graph TD A1[Acceptance Testing
Performed by End User]:::top A2[System Testing
Complete Integrated System]:::upper A3[Integration Testing
Modules Working Together]:::middle A4[Unit Testing
Smallest Function or Module]:::bottom A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 classDef top fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef upper fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef middle fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bottom fill:#69db7c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **widest layer (Unit Testing)** has the most tests, and the **narrowest layer (Acceptance Testing)** has the fewest. This is the "ice-cream cone" anti-pattern in reverse — a healthy project has many cheap unit tests and few expensive acceptance tests. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Program-Testing Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Write Python Algorithm] --> reviewA[Self-Review the Code] reviewA --> identA{Identify Test Requirements} identA --> unitA[Unit Test Individual Functions] identA --> integA[Integration Test Modules] unitA --> execA[Execute Test Cases] integA --> execA execA --> compA{Output matches Expected?} compA -- Yes --> passA[Mark Test as Passed] compA -- No --> failA[Log Failure and Bug] failA --> debugA[Debug the Code] debugA --> fixA[Apply Fix] fixA --> startA passA --> allA{All Tests Pass?} allA -- No --> execA allA -- Yes --> doneA[Program Validated and Shipped] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Classification of Errors in Python ```mermaid graph TD errRoot[Program Errors]:::root syn[Syntax Errors
Detected Before Execution]:::syn run[Runtime Errors
Detected During Execution]:::run log[Logic Errors
Detected Only by Testing]:::log synEx[Missing colon
Misspelled keyword
Mismatched brackets]:::leaf runEx[ZeroDivisionError
IndexError
KeyError
TypeError]:::leaf logEx[Wrong operator
Off-by-one error
Incorrect formula]:::leaf errRoot --> syn errRoot --> run errRoot --> log syn --> synEx run --> runEx log --> logEx classDef root fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef syn fill:#ff8787,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef run fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef log fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#e9ecef,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Test-Case Design Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD p1[Given a Python Function] --> p2[List the Specification Requirements] p2 --> p3[Identify Equivalence Partitions] p3 --> p4[Add Boundary Values at Each Edge] p4 --> p5[Add Invalid Input Cases] p5 --> p6{Is Branch Coverage 100%?} p6 -- No --> p7[Add More Tests to Cover Missing Branches] p7 --> p6 p6 -- Yes --> p8[Finalize the Test Suite] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Syntax Errors, Runtime Errors, and Logic Errors in a Python program. Give one example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Error Type | When Detected | Example (Code) | Resulting Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | **Syntax Error** | Before execution (at parse time). | `if x == 5`
(missing colon) | `SyntaxError` is raised; program never runs. | | **Runtime Error** | During execution. | `print(10 / 0)` | `ZeroDivisionError` traceback; program halts. | | **Logic Error** | Never by the interpreter — only by testing. | Area of rectangle: `l + b` instead of `l * b` | Program runs and prints a value, **but the value is wrong**. | *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for examples)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the purpose of the `assert` statement in Python? Explain with an example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `assert` statement is a **debugging aid** that tests whether a condition is `True` at a specific point in a program. If the condition is `False`, an `AssertionError` is raised, halting the program. It is used to enforce **preconditions and invariants** during development. **Example:** ```python def calculate_discount(price, discount_percent): assert price >= 0, "Price cannot be negative" assert 0 <= discount_percent <= 100, "Discount must be between 0 and 100" return price * (discount_percent / 100) ``` - *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for explanation of behavior.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **different levels of software testing** with a suitable diagram. Describe **Unit Testing** in detail with a Python example using the `unittest` module. **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **complete set of test cases** for the following Python function. Justify why you chose each test case and compute the **branch coverage** achieved by your test suite. ```python def absolute_value(n): if n >= 0: return n else: return -n ``` #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Levels of Testing [7 Marks] The four levels of software testing, arranged from smallest to largest scope, are: 1. **Unit Testing [1 mark]** — Tests the **smallest testable component** of the software, typically a single function or method, in **isolation** from the rest of the system. It is performed by developers and forms the **base of the testing pyramid**. *Python example using `unittest`:* ```python import unittest def add(a, b): return a + b class TestAddFunction(unittest.TestCase): def test_positive_numbers(self): self.assertEqual(add(2, 3), 5) def test_negative_numbers(self): self.assertEqual(add(-2, -3), -5) def test_zero(self): self.assertEqual(add(0, 0), 0) if __name__ == "__main__": unittest.main() ``` Run with: `python -m unittest test_add.py` 2. **Integration Testing [1.5 marks]** — Tests the **interaction between multiple modules** to expose defects in their interfaces. Example: verifying that the `login()` module and the `database` module communicate correctly. 3. **System Testing [1.5 marks]** — Tests the **complete, integrated system** against its specified requirements. Both functional and non-functional requirements (performance, security, usability) are validated. 4. **Acceptance Testing [1.5 marks]** — The **final test** before delivery, performed by the **end user or client** to determine if the system meets business needs. Includes: - **Alpha Testing:** Performed at the developer's site. - **Beta Testing:** Performed by a limited set of real users in their own environment. 5. **Diagram [1.5 marks]** — A pyramid with Unit Testing at the broad base, then Integration, then System, and Acceptance at the peak. ### Part (b) — Test Case Design for `absolute_value` [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Identify branches [1 mark]:** There are **2 branches**: (i) `n >= 0`, (ii) `else` (i.e., `n < 0`). **Step 2 — Identify boundary values [1 mark]:** The boundary is at $n = 0$. **Step 3 — Design test cases [3 marks]:** | Test ID | Input (n) | Expected Output | Branch Tested | Category | |---|---|---|---|---| | T1 | `5` | `5` | `n >= 0` (True) | Normal | | T2 | `-3` | `3` | `n < 0` (Else) | Normal | | T3 | `0` | `0` | `n >= 0` (boundary) | Boundary | | T4 | `-0.0001` | `0.0001` | `n < 0` (boundary) | Boundary | | T5 | `1e9` | `1e9` | `n >= 0` (extreme) | Extreme | | T6 | `"abc"` | TypeError | Invalid input | Invalid | **Step 4 — Compute branch coverage [2 marks]:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Branches Tested} &= 2 \quad (\text{T1, T2, T3, T4, T5 cover `n >= 0`; T2 and T4 cover `else`}) \\ \text{Branch Coverage} &= \frac{2}{2} \times 100\% = 100\% \end{aligned} $$ **Justification [Bonus, ensures full marks]:** T3 is essential because $n = 0$ is the critical boundary where the condition flips. T4 ensures negative infinitesimals are handled. T5 checks the extreme input range. T6 ensures the function is robust to type errors. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Black-Box Testing** and **White-Box Testing** in detail. Compare them in a table and state one scenario where each is most useful. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following Python function. Write a **`unittest` TestCase** class to thoroughly test it, including **at least five** test methods covering normal, boundary, and error conditions. ```python def divide_numbers(a, b): if b == 0: raise ValueError("Denominator cannot be zero") return a / b ``` #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Black-Box vs White-Box Testing [7 Marks] **Black-Box Testing [2.5 marks]:** - Also called **functional** or **behavioral** testing. - The tester views the program as an **opaque box**; only **inputs and outputs** are examined. - Test cases are derived from the **requirements specification**. - The tester does **not** need to know the code. - **Best scenario:** Acceptance testing for a third-party library where the source code is unavailable. - **Techniques:** Equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, decision tables. **White-Box Testing [2.5 marks]:** - Also called **structural** or **glass-box** testing. - The tester **examines the internal logic**, branches, and code paths. - Test cases are designed to ensure **100% statement/branch/path coverage**. - **Best scenario:** Unit testing a critical safety function in an embedded system. - **Techniques:** Statement coverage, branch coverage, cyclomatic complexity, path testing. **Comparison Table [2 marks]:** | Parameter | Black-Box Testing | White-Box Testing | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | Inputs and outputs | Internal code structure | | **Knowledge of code** | Not required | Required | | **Driven by** | Specification | Source code | | **Test case design** | Requirements / use cases | Control flow / data flow | | **Catches** | Missing or wrong features | Dead code, hidden branches | | **Performed by** | Testers, end users | Developers | | **Coverage metric** | Function-level | Statement / branch / path | ### Part (b) — `unittest` TestCase for `divide_numbers` [7 Marks] ```python import unittest def divide_numbers(a, b): if b == 0: raise ValueError("Denominator cannot be zero") return a / b class TestDivideNumbers(unittest.TestCase): def test_normal_positive(self): """Normal: 10 / 2 = 5.0""" self.assertEqual(divide_numbers(10, 2), 5.0) def test_normal_negative(self): """Normal: -10 / 2 = -5.0""" self.assertEqual(divide_numbers(-10, 2), -5.0) def test_boundary_one(self): """Boundary: numerator equals denominator → 1.0""" self.assertEqual(divide_numbers(7, 7), 1.0) def test_boundary_zero_numerator(self): """Boundary: 0 / 5 = 0.0""" self.assertEqual(divide_numbers(0, 5), 0.0) def test_fractional_result(self): """Boundary: 1 / 4 = 0.25""" self.assertEqual(divide_numbers(1, 4), 0.25) def test_error_zero_denominator(self): """Error: denominator = 0 must raise ValueError""" with self.assertRaises(ValueError): divide_numbers(10, 0) def test_type_error(self): """Error: string input must raise TypeError""" with self.assertRaises(TypeError): divide_numbers("10", 2) if __name__ == "__main__": unittest.main() ``` **Mark Breakdown [7 marks]:** - Correct class structure inheriting `unittest.TestCase`: **1 mark** - `test_normal_positive` and `test_normal_negative`: **1 mark** - Boundary tests (`boundary_one`, `boundary_zero_numerator`, `fractional_result`): **2 marks** - Error tests using `assertRaises`: **2 marks** - Proper `if __name__ == "__main__":` block: **0.5 mark** - Comments and code readability: **0.5 mark** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Validation with Verification** — Validation = "right product?" Verification = "product right?" Mixing them up costs full marks on the 3-mark definitional question. > 2. **Writing only "Happy Path" tests** — Examiners *always* look for **boundary, invalid, and extreme cases**. Designing only `test_normal` methods will lose 2–3 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the `if __name__ == "__main__":` block** in `unittest` answers — this is a syntax-level requirement; omitting it may fail to run and lose 0.5 mark. > 4. **Using `assert == ` for floats** without `assertAlmostEqual` — floating-point comparisons can fail due to rounding. Prefer `self.assertAlmostEqual(divide_numbers(1, 3), 0.333, places=3)`. > 5. **Confusing `assertRaises` (context manager) with `assertRaises` (method form)** — both are valid in `unittest` but must be applied correctly; misuse is a common pitfall. > 6. **Listing test cases without categorization** — always label each test as Normal, Boundary, Extreme, or Invalid. Unlabelled tables lose organization marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read this last before entering the exam hall.** - **Testing** = executing a program to find bugs; **debugging** = removing them. - **Dijkstra's principle:** Testing can show the *presence* of bugs, never their *absence*. - **Three error classes:** - **Syntax** → caught before execution (e.g., missing `:`). - **Runtime** → caught during execution (e.g., `ZeroDivisionError`). - **Logic** → never caught by the interpreter (e.g., wrong operator). - **Two testing strategies:** - **Black-Box** → spec-driven, no code knowledge. - **White-Box** → code-structure-driven, uses coverage metrics. - **Four levels of testing (pyramid, base to peak):** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance. - **Four test-case categories:** Normal, Boundary, Extreme, Invalid. **Always include all four.** - **Boundary Principle:** Test the value just below, at, and just above every edge. - **Python `assert` statement:** `assert , `; raises `AssertionError` if `False`; disabled by `python -O`. - **`unittest` module:** Test classes inherit from `unittest.TestCase`; use `assertEqual`, `assertTrue`, `assertRaises`; run with `python -m unittest`. - **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $M = E - N + 2P$ or $M = \pi + 1$ (decision points + 1). - **Coverage formulas:** Statement Coverage = (Executed / Total) × 100%; Branch Coverage = (Tested branches / Total branches) × 100%. - **Golden Rule:** A good test suite has **100% branch coverage** and covers **all four categories** of test cases. - **Validation vs. Verification:** Validation = right *product*; Verification = product *right*. - **Defensive programming:** Use `assert` and explicit `raise` statements to make algorithms robust against invalid inputs.

topics.title: Evaluating the Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluating the Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *Algorithmic Thinking with Python*, **Evaluating the Solution** is defined as the systematic process of **verifying correctness** and **measuring efficiency** of a proposed algorithm (or Python implementation) with respect to a given problem specification. The evaluation has three formal pillars:

1. **Correctness Verification** – Proving (or empirically demonstrating) that the algorithm produces the *right output* for *all valid inputs* within the problem's domain.
2. **Efficiency Analysis** – Quantifying the **resource consumption** of the algorithm, primarily **Time Complexity** and **Space Complexity**, usually expressed via **Asymptotic Notation (Big-O, $O(\cdot)$)**.
3. **Validation** – Confirming that the *right problem* has been solved, i.e., the output satisfies the *user's actual need*, not merely the literal specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In Module 1, evaluation is treated as a *qualitative + quantitative* exercise. You must be able to (a) hand-trace an algorithm on sample inputs, (b) count elementary operations, (c) express the cost as a function of input size $n$, and (d) reduce it to its asymptotic class.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you have built a **water purifier** for a village:

- **Correctness** = Does the output water pass the lab test for purity? (Does it solve the problem?)
- **Efficiency** = How many litres per hour does it produce, and how much electricity does it use? (Time & space cost.)
- **Validation** = Do the villagers actually like the *taste* of the water, or is it technically pure but undrinkable? (Did we solve the *right* problem?)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** An algorithm can be **correct but inefficient** (a brute-force search that takes 100 years), or **fast but incorrect** (a buggy shortcut). Evaluation ensures we get **both** right.

## 1.3 Evaluation Metrics — The Three Standard Measures

| Metric | Symbol | What it Measures | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity | $T(n)$ | Number of elementary operations | Steps / Operations |
| Space Complexity | $S(n)$ | Extra memory used beyond input | Memory units (words/bytes) |
| Correctness | $\forall x \in D$ | Output matches expected for every valid input $x$ in domain $D$ | Boolean (True/False) |

where $n$ is the **input size** (length of list, number of nodes, digits in a number, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evaluation Pipeline (Structured Logic Steps)

A KTU-grade evaluation always follows this sequence:

1. **Understand the specification** → Identify the input domain, output range, and the input–output relationship $f: I \rightarrow O$.
2. **Hand-trace on small inputs** → Use a trace table to verify logical flow without running code.
3. **Identify elementary operations** → Comparisons, assignments, arithmetic, I/O — each counts as 1 unit.
4. **Express cost as a function** $T(n)$ of the input size $n$.
5. **Reduce to asymptotic form** → Keep only the **dominant term** and drop constants to get $O(g(n))$.
6. **Classify into a complexity class** → $O(1), O(\log n), O(n), O(n \log n), O(n^2), O(2^n), O(n!)$.
7. **Compare with alternatives** → A solution is "good" only relative to the problem's constraints.
8. **Empirical benchmarking (optional)** → Time the actual Python execution using `time` or `timeit` modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Order Matters:** KTU examiners often award marks for *the process*. Even if the final Big-O is wrong, a clearly written trace table and a well-formed $T(n)$ function earn partial credit.

## 2.2 Asymptotic Notation — The Three Flavours

For a given function $T(n)$, the formal definitions are:

- **Big-O** ($O$): Upper bound. $T(n) = O(g(n))$ if $\exists c, n_0 > 0$ such that $T(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.
- **Big-Omega** ($\Omega$): Lower bound. $T(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ if $\exists c, n_0 > 0$ such that $T(n) \ge c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$.
- **Big-Theta** ($\Theta$): Tight bound. $T(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ iff $T(n) = O(g(n))$ **and** $T(n) = \Omega(g(n))$.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, **Big-O is the default** unless the question explicitly asks for $\Omega$ or $\Theta$.

## 2.3 Best, Worst, and Average Case

For any non-deterministic algorithm (e.g., Linear Search):

- **Best Case** — $T_{\text{best}}(n)$: Cost on the *most favourable* input (e.g., element found at index 0). $\rightarrow$ **$O(1)$** for Linear Search.
- **Worst Case** — $T_{\text{worst}}(n)$: Cost on the *least favourable* input (e.g., element at last index or absent). $\rightarrow$ **$O(n)$** for Linear Search.
- **Average Case** — $T_{\text{avg}}(n)$: Expected cost over the *uniform distribution* of inputs. $\rightarrow$ **$O(n)$** for Linear Search.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` is **forbidden** inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value / divisibility notation.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time complexity of a simple loop | $T(n) = c_1 + c_2 \cdot n$ | Linear scan |
| 2 | Time complexity of nested loops | $T(n) = c_1 n + c_2 n^2$ | Bubble Sort, Selection Sort |
| 3 | Time complexity of divide-and-conquer | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | Recurrences (Master Theorem base) |
| 4 | Master Theorem (informal) | If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | Merge Sort, Binary Search |
| 5 | Geometric series | $1 + 2 + 4 + \ldots + 2^{k-1} = 2^k - 1$ | Recursive doubling |
| 6 | Arithmetic series | $1 + 2 + \ldots + n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | Nested loops |
| 7 | Harmonic series | $1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \ldots + \dfrac{1}{n} = \Theta(\log n)$ | Analysis of some random algorithms |
| 8 | Logarithmic loop | $T(n) = T(n/2) + c \Rightarrow T(n) = O(\log n)$ | Binary Search |
| 9 | Big-O drop-rule | $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \rightarrow O(n^2)$ | Asymptotic simplification |
| 10 | Space of recursion | $S(n) = O(\text{depth of call stack})$ | Recursive algorithms |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evaluating solutions is the **backbone of every software engineering decision**:

- **Google Search** uses $O(1)$ hash-table lookups for billions of queries.
- **GPS Navigation** runs Dijkstra's algorithm ($O((V+E)\log V)$) to find shortest paths in real time.
- **Database indexing** relies on B-Trees ($O(\log n)$) to retrieve records from terabytes of data.

A **1-unit improvement** in Big-O can mean the difference between an app that runs in **0.1 seconds** and one that takes **3 hours** for $n = 10^6$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Deriving $T(n)$ for Linear Search

**Algorithm (Python pseudocode):**
```
def linear_search(arr, target):
    for i in range(len(arr)):       # Loop
        if arr[i] == target:        # Comparison
            return i                # Return
    return -1                       # Not found
```

**Step-by-step derivation of $T_{\text{worst}}(n)$:**

Let $n = \text{len}(arr)$. Let $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4$ be the costs of loop init, comparison, return, and the final return respectively.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{worst}}(n) &= \underbrace{c_1}_{\text{loop init}} + \underbrace{n \cdot c_2}_{n \text{ comparisons}} + \underbrace{c_3}_{\text{final return}} \\
&= c_1 + c_2 n + c_3 \\
&= a \cdot n + b \quad \text{(where } a = c_2,\ b = c_1 + c_3\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic reduction:**

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = a n + b \;\xrightarrow{\text{drop constant, keep dominant term}}\; O(n)
$$

> **Reasoning:** As $n \to \infty$, the additive constant $b$ and even the multiplicative constant $a$ become negligible compared to the growth rate $n$. Hence we keep only the *order of growth*.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Nested Loops — Bubble Sort

**Code structure:**
```
for i in range(n):              # Outer: n iterations
    for j in range(n - i - 1):  # Inner: (n - i - 1) iterations
        if arr[j] > arr[j+1]:   # Comparison
            swap(arr, j, j+1)    # Swap
```

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-i-2} 1 \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (n - i - 1) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{n} k \quad \text{(letting } k = n - i\text{)} \\
&= \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n^2 + n}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic form:**

$$
T(n) = \frac{n^2 + n}{2} \;\rightarrow\; O(n^2)
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Logarithmic — Binary Search

**Code structure:**
```
def binary_search(arr, target, low, high):
    if low > high: return -1
    mid = (low + high) // 2
    if arr[mid] == target: return mid
    elif arr[mid] < target:
        return binary_search(arr, target, mid+1, high)
    else:
        return binary_search(arr, target, low, mid-1)
```

**Recurrence:**

$$
T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c
$$

**Solving by unrolling:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + c \\
&= T(n/4) + 2c \\
&= T(n/8) + 3c \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(n/2^k) + kc
\end{aligned}
$$

**Termination:** Recursion stops when $n/2^k = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; k = \log_2 n$.

$$
T(n) = T(1) + c \log_2 n = O(\log n)
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Python Operations

| Python Construct | Time Complexity $O(\cdot)$ | Space Complexity $O(\cdot)$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Index access `lst[i]` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | List is array-backed |
| Append `lst.append(x)` | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(1)$ | Occasional resize |
| Search in list `x in lst` | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Linear scan |
| `dict[key]` lookup | $O(1)$ average | $O(n)$ overall | Hash table |
| `set` membership `x in s` | $O(1)$ average | $O(n)$ overall | Hash table |
| `for` loop over $n$ items | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Per iteration |
| Nested `for` (2 levels) | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | Bubble/Selection sort |
| Recursion depth $d$ | $O(d)$ work | $O(d)$ stack space | Each call uses a frame |
| String concatenation `s + t` | $O(\vert s \vert + \vert t \vert)$ | Creates new string | Immutable |
| List slicing `lst[a:b]` | $O(b - a)$ | $O(b - a)$ | Returns new list |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Evaluation Workflow

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end evaluation pipeline** a KTU student should follow when assessing any algorithmic solution. It traces the path from a raw solution to a fully validated and optimized implementation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Proposed Solution] --> B[Step 1: Understand Specification]
    B --> C[Step 2: Hand Trace on Small Inputs]
    C --> D{Trace matches expected output?}
    D -- No --> E[Debug and Refine Logic]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 3: Identify Elementary Operations]
    F --> G[Step 4: Express T of n]
    G --> H[Step 5: Apply Asymptotic Reduction]
    H --> I[Step 6: Derive Big O Class]
    I --> J{Meets Problem Constraints?}
    J -- No --> K[Step 7: Optimize Algorithm]
    K --> C
    J -- Yes --> L[Step 8: Empirical Benchmarking]
    L --> M[Step 9: Validation with Real Users]
    M --> N[End: Evaluated and Approved Solution]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Complexity Class

This block diagram helps a student decide which Big-O class a given code structure belongs to — a frequently tested KTU skill.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Code Structure] --> Q{Number of nested loops?}
    Q -- Zero loops, constant work --> R1[Class: O of 1]
    Q -- One loop over n --> R2[Class: O of n]
    Q -- Two nested loops over n --> R3[Class: O of n squared]
    Q -- Loop where i doubles or halves --> R4[Class: O of log n]
    Q -- Loop with n nested recursive calls --> R5[Class: O of 2 power n]

    P --> S{Recursive structure?}
    S -- Single call, n reduces by half --> T1[Class: O of log n]
    S -- Two calls, each on n minus 1 --> T2[Class: O of 2 power n]
    S -- Two calls, each on n by 2 --> T3[Class: O of n log n]
```

## 4.3 Verification vs Validation — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Verification
        V1[Are we building the product RIGHT?] --> V2[Correctness, Logic, Trace Tables]
    end
    subgraph Validation
        W1[Are we building the RIGHT product?] --> W2[User Needs, Real World Use]
    end
    Verification --> Final[Evaluated Solution]
    Validation --> Final
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions are short-answer / definition type. They appear in **KTU Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and the **first section of the End Semester Exam (ESE)**.

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between *Time Complexity* and *Space Complexity*. Why is Big-O notation preferred over exact operation counts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Measures the number of elementary operations performed | Measures the extra memory used by the algorithm |
| Depends on | CPU instructions, loop iterations, recursion depth | Variables, data structures, call stack |
| Example | Bubble Sort $\rightarrow O(n^2)$ time | Recursive Fibonacci $\rightarrow O(n)$ space |

**Why Big-O is preferred (1 mark):** Exact operation counts depend on hardware, compiler, and constant factors, making them non-portable. Big-O captures only the *order of growth* as $n \to \infty$, which is hardware-independent and reveals the algorithm's **scalability** — the most important property for large inputs.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by *Best Case*, *Worst Case*, and *Average Case* complexity? Illustrate with Linear Search.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Best Case** ($T_{\text{best}}$): The minimum time an algorithm takes on the *most favourable* input. For Linear Search, this is $O(1)$ — element found at the first index.
- **Worst Case** ($T_{\text{worst}}$): The maximum time on the *least favourable* input. For Linear Search, this is $O(n)$ — element is at the last position or absent.
- **Average Case** ($T_{\text{avg}}$): The expected time averaged over all equally-likely inputs. For Linear Search with uniform distribution, $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{n+1}{2} = O(n)$.

> **Marking tip (1 mark):** Always state both the *condition* and the *Big-O class*.

---
