out-degree equals 0"]) nodeS --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeE style nodeS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style nodeE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px ``` **(b) — Flowchart for Running Sum (7 Marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA(["START"]) nodeB["Initialize: SUM equals 0"] nodeC[/"Read integer N"/] nodeD{{"Is N less than 0"}} nodeE["SUM equals SUM plus N"] nodeF[/"Print SUM"/] nodeG(["END"]) nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD -->|"NO, N is positive"| nodeE nodeD -->|"YES, N is negative"| nodeF nodeE --> nodeC nodeF --> nodeG style nodeA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style nodeG fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px ``` **Logic Explanation (7 marks):** 1. **Start symbol** initializes the algorithm *(½ mark)* 2. **Initialization** of `SUM = 0` *(1 mark)* 3. **Input** of integer `N` *(1 mark)* 4. **Decision** `N < 0 ?` — branches into two paths *(1 mark)* 5. **NO branch:** Add N to SUM and **loop back** to read next N *(1 mark)* 6. **YES branch:** Print the final SUM *(1 mark)* 7. **End symbol** terminates the algorithm — exactly **one incoming arrow** from the print block, **no outgoing arrow** *(1½ marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the End symbol** → Lose **2 marks** outright (the flowchart is "incomplete"). > 2. **Drawing Start/End as plain rectangles** → Lose **1–2 marks** (wrong shape; not per ISO 5807). > 3. **Writing code/calculations inside Start or End** → Lose **1 mark** (terminators are *markers only*). > 4. **Drawing multiple Start symbols** → Lose **1 mark** (only **one** Start is allowed). > 5. **Connecting two End symbols with an arrow** → Lose **1 mark** (End symbols never have outgoing arrows). > 6. **Missing the arrow exiting Start** → Lose **½ mark** (Start must hand over control to the next block). > 7. **Not labeling Start/End with the words "Start" / "End"** → Lose **½ mark** (labels are mandatory for clarity). --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Start and End symbols are called **Terminators** (or **Terminals**). - ✅ Both are drawn as **rounded-rectangle / pill / capsule** shapes. - ✅ They follow the **ISO 5807** international standard. - ✅ The **Start symbol** has **in-degree = 0** and **out-degree = 1**. - ✅ The **End symbol** has **in-degree ≥ 1** and **out-degree = 0**. - ✅ A valid flowchart has **exactly one Start** and **at least one End** (can have multiple Ends for different termination paths). - ✅ Terminators contain **no logic, no conditions, no processes** — they are pure *markers*. - ✅ Industry color convention: **Green** for Start, **Red** for End. - ✅ In Python, the Start corresponds to the **first executable line** and the End corresponds to **`sys.exit()`** or **program termination**. - ✅ Memorize the table in **Section 2.4** verbatim — it is the highest-yield content for KTU valuations. - ✅ Real-world usage: industrial pipelines, networking protocols, robotics, banking software, bioinformatics.

topics.title: Arithmetic Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Arithmetic Calculations* in Python refer to the systematic execution of mathematical operations on numeric operands (literals, variables, or expressions) using a well-defined set of **arithmetic operators**, **built-in functions**, and the **`math` module** utilities, governed by strict rules of **operator precedence**, **associativity**, and **type promotion**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (UCEST105), arithmetic calculations form the foundational computational layer of *Module 2 – Algorithmic Thinking*. Every subsequent control structure, loop, and function ultimately reduces to arithmetic + logic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Calculator" Metaphor

Imagine a **scientific calculator with memory cells**:

- The **display screen** is your Python `print()` output.
- The **number buttons (0–9, .)** are your numeric **literals** (`10`, `3.14`).
- The **operator buttons (+, −, ×, ÷)** are Python's arithmetic operators.
- The **memory slots (M+, MR)** are your **variables** (`x = 5`).
- The **function keys (sin, log, √)** are the **`math` module** functions.

When you press `2 + 3 × 4` on a calculator, it doesn't just read left-to-right — it knows **BODMAS/PEMDAS** rules. Python behaves *exactly* the same way through its **operator precedence table**. Forgetting this rule is the #1 reason students lose marks in KTU exams.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Python Arithmetic

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Operators:** Symbols that perform the operation (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `//`, `%`, `**`).
>
> **Pillar 2 — Built-in Functions:** Always available, no import needed (`abs()`, `round()`, `pow()`, `divmod()`, `max()`, `min()`, `sum()`).
>
> **Pillar 3 — `math` Module:** Advanced operations requiring `import math` (`math.sqrt()`, `math.log()`, `math.sin()`, `math.factorial()`).

## 1.4 Numeric Types in Arithmetic

| Type | Keyword | Example | Mutable? | Precision |
|------|---------|---------|----------|-----------|
| Integer | `int` | `42`, `-7` | Immutable | Arbitrary (unlimited) |
| Floating Point | `float` | `3.14`, `2.0` | Immutable | ~15-17 significant digits (IEEE 754) |
| Complex | `complex` | `2 + 3j` | Immutable | Two 64-bit floats |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Python's `int` type has **arbitrary precision** — no overflow error! This is a frequent exam question. (Unlike C/Java's 32-bit `int` which overflows at 2,147,483,647.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Core Arithmetic Operators

Python provides **seven** binary (and one unary) arithmetic operators. Understanding each one's *exact* behavior is critical for KTU exams.

### 2.1.1 Addition `+`
- **Numeric behavior:** Sum of two numbers.
- **Identity element:** $0$ (i.e., $a + 0 = a$).
- **Type rule:** `int + int = int`, `int + float = float` (int is promoted), `int + complex = complex`.

### 2.1.2 Subtraction `-`
- **Numeric behavior:** Difference of two numbers.
- **Identity element:** $0$ (i.e., $a - 0 = a$).
- **Unary minus:** `-a` negates the value.

### 2.1.3 Multiplication `*`
- **Numeric behavior:** Product of two numbers.
- **Identity element:** $1$ (i.e., $a \times 1 = a$).
- **Zero property:** $a \times 0 = 0$ for all numeric $a$.

### 2.1.4 True Division `/` (The "Slash")
- **Behavior:** *Always* returns a **`float`**, even if the result is mathematically a whole number.
- **Critical KTU Point:** `10 / 2` gives `5.0`, **not** `5`.

### 2.1.5 Floor Division `//` (The "Double Slash")
- **Behavior:** Returns the **mathematical floor** of the true division.
- **Formula:** $a \ // \ b = \left\lfloor \dfrac{a}{b} \right\rfloor$
- **Sign rule:** Result rounds **toward negative infinity** (not toward zero).
- **Example:** `-7 // 2 = -4` (NOT `-3`), because $-3.5$ floors to $-4$.

### 2.1.6 Modulo `%` (The "Remainder Operator")
- **Formula:** $a = (a \ // \ b) \times b + (a \ \% \ b)$
- **Sign of result:** Has the **same sign as the divisor** `b`.
- **Example:** `7 % 3 = 1`, `-7 % 3 = 2` (NOT `-1`).

### 2.1.7 Exponentiation `**`
- **Behavior:** $a ** b = a^b$
- **Right-associative:** `2 ** 3 ** 2 = 2 ** 9 = 512` (NOT `64`).
- **Fractional exponents allowed:** `4 ** 0.5 = 2.0` (returns float).

## 2.2 Operator Precedence — The Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Weightage Topic:** Operator precedence is asked almost every semester.

Precedence is evaluated **highest to lowest** (top binds tightest):

$$\text{**} \;\; > \;\; \text{unary } {-} \;\; > \;\; {*}, \ {/}, \ {//}, \ {/\%} \;\; > \;\; \text{binary } {+}, \ {-}$$

**Associativity:**
- All operators are **left-associative** *except* `**` which is **right-associative**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operator / Function | Symbol / Syntax | Operation | Result Type | Identity / Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Addition | `a + b` | Sum | `int` or promoted | $a + 0 = a$ |
| Subtraction | `a - b` | Difference | `int` or promoted | $a - 0 = a$ |
| Multiplication | `a * b` | Product | `int` or promoted | $a \times 1 = a$ |
| True Division | `a / b` | Quotient | **always `float`** | $\text{zerodivisionerror if } b=0$ |
| Floor Division | `a // b` | $\lfloor a/b \rfloor$ | `int` if both `int` | Rounds to $-\infty$ |
| Modulo | `a % b` | Remainder | `int` if both `int` | Sign matches divisor |
| Exponentiation | `a ** b` | $a^b$ | `int` if both `int` and $b \geq 0$ | Right-associative |
| Unary Negation | `-a` | Sign flip | Same as `a` | $a + (-a) = 0$ |
| Absolute Value | `abs(a)` | $\vert a \vert$ | Same as `a` | $\text{abs}(-x) = x$ |
| Power (built-in) | `pow(a, b)` | $a^b$ | `int`/`float` | Equivalent to `a ** b` |
| Power + Modulus | `pow(a, b, m)` | $a^b \ \% \ m$ | `int` | **Efficient** (3-arg form) |
| Round | `round(x, n)` | Round to $n$ decimals | `float` if $n$ given, else `int` | Banker's rounding (ties → even) |
| Divmod | `divmod(a, b)` | $(a \ // \ b, \ a \ \% \ b)$ | Tuple of `int`s | Single C-level operation |
| Max of sequence | `max(s)` | Largest element | Element type | — |
| Min of sequence | `min(s)` | Smallest element | Element type | — |
| Sum of sequence | `sum(s)` | Total | Element type | Initial value optional |
| Square root | `math.sqrt(x)` | $\sqrt{x}$ | `float` | $x \geq 0$ required |
| Logarithm | `math.log(x, base)` | $\log_{\text{base}} x$ | `float` | Default base = $e$ |
| Sine | `math.sin(x)` | $\sin(x)$ in **radians** | `float` | NOT degrees! |
| Pi constant | `math.pi` | $\pi \approx 3.14159$ | `float` | 15-digit precision |
| Factorial | `math.factorial(n)` | $n!$ | `int` | $n \geq 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Division Algorithm Identity:** For any integers $a$ and $b \neq 0$:
> $$a = (a \ // \ b) \times b + (a \ \% \ b)$$
> This is the *defining property* of floor division and modulo. Memorize it.

## 2.4 Type Promotion Rules (Mixed Arithmetic)

When operands of different numeric types meet, Python applies **automatic type promotion**:

$$\text{int} + \text{float} \rightarrow \text{float}$$

$$\text{int} + \text{complex} \rightarrow \text{complex}$$

$$\text{float} + \text{complex} \rightarrow \text{complex}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical promotion order:** $\text{int} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{complex}$ (the "wider" type always wins).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Signal Processing** | DFT/FFT: $X_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_n e^{-i 2\pi k n / N}$ |
| **Computer Graphics** | Matrix transformations, vector dot products |
| **Cryptography** | Modular exponentiation: `pow(base, exp, mod)` for RSA |
| **Physics Simulations** | Newton's law: $F = m \cdot a$; kinematics: $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ |
| **Data Science** | Mean: $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$; Std. deviation: $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}}$ |
| **Financial Computing** | Compound interest: $A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt}$ |
| **Embedded Systems** | Bit manipulation, fixed-point arithmetic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Operator Precedence Evaluation

**Problem:** Evaluate the following Python expression step by step:

$$y = 2 + 3 \times 4 ** 2 - 10 / / 3$$

### Step-by-Step Resolution

**Step 1:** Identify the **highest-precedence** operator: exponentiation `**`.

$$\text{Sub-expression: } 4 ** 2 = 16$$

**Step 2:** Replace the sub-expression in the original:

$$y = 2 + 3 \times 16 - 10 \ // \ 3$$

**Step 3:** Next highest: multiplication `*` and floor division `//` (same precedence, left-to-right associativity).

$$\text{Sub-expression 1: } 3 \times 16 = 48$$

$$\text{Sub-expression 2: } 10 \ // \ 3 = \left\lfloor \frac{10}{3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3.333... \right\rfloor = 3$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$y = 2 + 48 - 3$$

**Step 5:** Add and subtract (left to right):

$$y = 50 - 3 = 47$$

**Final Answer:** `y = 47` (type `int`).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Modulo with Negative Operands

**Problem:** Compute `(-17) % 5` and `17 % (-5)`.

### Derivation

Using the **division algorithm identity**:
$$a = (a \ // \ b) \times b + (a \ \% \ b)$$

**Case 1:** $a = -17, \ b = 5$

First, find `(-17) // 5`:
$$\frac{-17}{5} = -3.4$$
$$\text{Floor of } -3.4 = -4$$
$$\therefore (-17) \ // \ 5 = -4$$

Now apply the identity:
$$-17 = (-4) \times 5 + r$$
$$-17 = -20 + r$$
$$r = 3$$

**Therefore:** $(-17) \ \% \ 5 = 3$ (note: result is positive because divisor is positive).

**Case 2:** $a = 17, \ b = -5$

First, find `17 // (-5)`:
$$\frac{17}{-5} = -3.4$$
$$\text{Floor of } -3.4 = -4$$
$$\therefore 17 \ // \ (-5) = -4$$

Apply the identity:
$$17 = (-4) \times (-5) + r$$
$$17 = 20 + r$$
$$r = -3$$

**Therefore:** $17 \ \% \ (-5) = -3$ (sign matches divisor's negative sign).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Students frequently write `(-17) % 5 = -2` thinking modulo preserves the sign of the dividend. **It does not in Python** — the sign follows the **divisor**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Compound Interest Formula

**Engineering Application:** Calculate the maturity amount of ₹50,000 invested at 8% annual rate, compounded quarterly, for 5 years.

**Formula:**
$$A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{n \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $P = 50000$ (Principal)
- $r = 0.08$ (Annual rate)
- $n = 4$ (Compounding periods per year)
- $t = 5$ (Years)

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** Compute $\frac{r}{n} = \frac{0.08}{4} = 0.02$

**Step 2:** Compute $n \cdot t = 4 \times 5 = 20$

**Step 3:** Compute the base $\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right) = 1.02$

**Step 4:** Raise to power: $1.02^{20} = 1.48594739598...$

**Step 5:** Multiply by principal: $A = 50000 \times 1.48594739598 \approx 74297.37$

### Python Code (For Reference — Topic is Algorithmic Thinking)

```python
P = 50000
r = 0.08
n = 4
t = 5
A = P * (1 + r/n) ** (n*t)
print(f"Maturity Amount: Rs. {A:.2f}")
# Output: Maturity Amount: Rs. 74297.37
```

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Trigonometric Identity Verification

**Problem:** Verify the identity $\sin^2(\theta) + \cos^2(\theta) = 1$ for $\theta = \pi/4$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Set $\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$ radians $= 45°$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\sin(\pi/4)$:

$$\sin(\pi/4) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071067811865476$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\cos(\pi/4)$:

$$\cos(\pi/4) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071067811865476$$

**Step 4:** Square and add:

$$\sin^2(\pi/4) + \cos^2(\pi/4) = (0.70710678...)^2 + (0.70710678...)^2$$

$$= 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0$$

```python
import math
theta = math.pi / 4
result = math.sin(theta)**2 + math.cos(theta)**2
print(f"sin^2 + cos^2 = {result}")
# Output: sin^2 + cos^2 = 1.0
```

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Euclidean GCD Using `math.gcd`

**Problem:** Find the GCD of 48 and 18 using the Euclidean algorithm.

### Derivation (Euclidean Algorithm)

$$\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \ \% \ b)$$

Recursive reduction:
- $\gcd(48, 18) = \gcd(18, 48 \ \% \ 18) = \gcd(18, 12)$
- $\gcd(18, 12) = \gcd(12, 18 \ \% \ 12) = \gcd(12, 6)$
- $\gcd(12, 6) = \gcd(6, 12 \ \% \ 6) = \gcd(6, 0)$
- $\gcd(6, 0) = 6$ ← Base case

**Therefore:** $\gcd(48, 18) = 6$

```python
import math
print(math.gcd(48, 18))  # Output: 6
```

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Common Exam Snippets

| Expression | Stepwise Evaluation | Final Value | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `7 + 3 * 2` | $7 + (3 \times 2) = 7 + 6$ | `13` | `int` |
| `(7 + 3) * 2` | $10 \times 2$ | `20` | `int` |
| `2 ** 3 ** 2` | $2 ** (3 ** 2) = 2^9$ | `512` | `int` |
| `10 / 3` | True division | `3.3333...` | `float` |
| `10 // 3` | $\lfloor 3.333 \rfloor$ | `3` | `int` |
| `10 % 3` | Remainder | `1` | `int` |
| `-10 // 3` | $\lfloor -3.333 \rfloor = -4$ | `-4` | `int` |
| `-10 % 3` | $-10 = (-4) \times 3 + 2$ | `2` | `int` |
| `5 + 3.0` | Promotion: $5.0 + 3.0$ | `8.0` | `float` |
| `abs(-3.7)` | $\vert -3.7 \vert$ | `3.7` | `float` |
| `round(3.5)` | Banker's rounding → even | `4` | `int` |
| `round(2.5)` | Banker's rounding → even | `2` | `int` |
| `divmod(17, 5)` | $(17 \ // \ 5, \ 17 \ \% \ 5)$ | `(3, 1)` | `tuple` |
| `pow(2, 10)` | $2^{10}$ | `1024` | `int` |
| `pow(2, 10, 7)` | $2^{10} \ \% \ 7 = 1024 \% 7$ | `2` | `int` |
| `math.sqrt(16)` | $\sqrt{16}$ | `4.0` | `float` |
| `math.factorial(5)` | $5!$ | `120` | `int` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Operator Precedence Pyramid

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the binding strength hierarchy of Python arithmetic operators. **Higher tiers bind tighter** (evaluated first).

```mermaid
graph TD
    TIER1["TIER 1 HIGHEST PRECEDENCE EXPONENTIATION OPERATOR DOUBLE ASTERISK"]
    TIER2["TIER 2 UNARY OPERATORS NEGATION PLUS TILDE"]
    TIER3["TIER 3 MULTIPLICATIVE OPERATORS ASTERISK SLASH DOUBLE SLASH PERCENT"]
    TIER4["TIER 4 ADDITIVE OPERATORS PLUS MINUS"]
    TIER5["TIER 5 LOWEST PRECEDENCE EVALUATED LAST ASSIGNMENT IF USED"]
    TIER1 --> TIER2
    TIER2 --> TIER3
    TIER3 --> TIER4
    TIER4 --> TIER5
```

**Reading guide:** An expression is reduced top-down. All `**` operations finish first, then unary operators, then `* / // %`, finally `+ -`.

---

## 4.2 Arithmetic Operation Flow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT Numeric Operands a and b] --> B{OPERATOR TYPE}
    B -->|ADD| C[a PLUS b]
    B -->|SUB| D[a MINUS b]
    B -->|MUL| E[a TIMES b]
    B -->|DIV| F[a SLASH b]
    B -->|FDIV| G[a DOUBLE SLASH b]
    B -->|MOD| H[a PERCENT b]
    B -->|POW| I[a DOUBLE ASTERISK b]
    C --> J{TYPE CHECK}
    D --> J
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -->|int AND int| K[Result INT]
    J -->|ANY float| L[Result FLOAT]
    J -->|ANY complex| M[Result COMPLEX]
    K --> N[OUTPUT Return Result]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

---

## 4.3 Floor Division & Modulo — Division Algorithm Block

This block diagram visualizes the **mathematical guarantee** of Python's floor division and modulo:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DIVIDEND a] --> B[Compute QUOTIENT q EQUALS a DOUBLE SLASH b]
    A --> C[Compute REMAINDER r EQUALS a PERCENT b]
    B --> D[PRODUCT q TIMES b]
    C --> D
    D --> E[ADD q TIMES b PLUS r]
    E --> F[VERIFY EQUALS ORIGINAL a]
    F -->|MATCH| G[CONSISTENT RESULT]
    F -->|MISMATCH| H[INVALID RUNTIME ERROR]
```

**Identity enforced:** $a = q \cdot b + r$ where $q = \lfloor a/b \rfloor$ and $0 \leq r < \vert b \vert$.

---

## 4.4 Expression Evaluation Tree

For the expression `2 + 3 * 4 ** 2 // 3`, here is the evaluation tree:

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["ROOT EXPRESSION: 2 PLUS T1 MINUS T2"]
    T1["T1: 3 TIMES U1"]
    T2["T2: U2 DOUBLE SLASH 3"]
    U1["U1: 4 DOUBLE ASTERISK 2 EQUALS 16"]
    U2["U2: 3 TIMES 4 EQUALS 12"]
    T1 --> U1
    T2 --> U2
    ROOT --> T1
    ROOT --> T2
```

**Evaluation order (post-order traversal):**
1. Compute `U1 = 4 ** 2 = 16` (deepest, highest precedence)
2. Compute `T1 = 3 * 16 = 48`
3. Compute `U2 = 3 * 4 = 12` *(but wait — this conflicts with our example!)*

> **Correction:** For the cleanest tree, the expression `2 + 3 * 4 ** 2 - 10 // 3` evaluates as:

1. `4 ** 2 = 16`
2. `3 * 16 = 48`
3. `10 // 3 = 3`
4. `2 + 48 = 50`
5. `50 - 3 = 47`

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between the `/` (true division) and `//` (floor division) operators in Python with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `/` (True Division) | `//` (Floor Division) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operator symbol** | Single forward slash | Double forward slash |
| **Result type** | **Always `float`** | `int` if both operands are `int`; `float` if any is `float` |
| **Result value** | Exact mathematical quotient | Floor of the mathematical quotient |
| **Rounding rule** | Never rounds (full precision) | Rounds toward **negative infinity** |
| **Example** | `7 / 2 → 3.5` | `7 // 2 → 3` |
| **Negative case** | `-7 / 2 → -3.5` | `-7 // 2 → -4` (NOT `-3`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key distinguishing feature is that `/` always returns a `float` result, while `//` returns the **mathematical floor** of the quotient, which may differ from the truncation behavior seen in C/C++/Java.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the role of the `math` module in Python. List any four functions from this module with their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

The `math` module is Python's **standard library** for advanced mathematical operations. It must be imported using `import math` before use. It provides access to mathematical functions defined by the C standard.

**Four important `math` module functions:**

1. **`math.sqrt(x)`** — Returns the square root of $x$ as a `float`. Requires $x \geq 0$. Example: `math.sqrt(25) → 5.0`.
2. **`math.pow(x, y)`** — Returns $x^y$ as a `float`. Different from `**` because it always returns a `float`. Example: `math.pow(2, 3) → 8.0`.
3. **`math.factorial(n)`** — Returns $n!$ for non-negative integer $n$. Example: `math.factorial(5) → 120`.
4. **`math.log(x, base)`** — Returns $\log_{\text{base}} x$. If base is omitted, returns natural logarithm $\ln(x)$. Example: `math.log(100, 10) → 2.0`.
5. **(Bonus) `math.pi`** — Provides the constant $\pi \approx 3.141592653589793$.

> [!NOTE]
> The `math` module is **part of Python's standard library** — no external installation (like `pip`) is required.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice A** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the operator precedence and associativity rules in Python. Evaluate the following expression showing each step:

$$x = 25 \ // \ 4 \ * \ 3 + 18 \ \% \ 5 - 2 \ ** \ 3$$

**(b)** Write a Python program to calculate the **area and perimeter of a circle** and the **volume of a sphere** for a given radius. Use the `math` module and format the output to 2 decimal places.

#### **Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Operator Precedence Hierarchy (highest to lowest):**

| Precedence Level | Operators | Associativity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Highest) | `**` | Right-to-left |
| 2 | Unary `+`, Unary `-` | Right-to-left |
| 3 | `*`, `/`, `//`, `%` | Left-to-right |
| 4 (Lowest) | Binary `+`, Binary `-` | Left-to-right |

**Step-by-step evaluation of** `x = 25 // 4 * 3 + 18 % 5 - 2 ** 3`:

**Step 1:** Evaluate `**` (highest precedence)
$$2 \ ** \ 3 = 2^3 = 8$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back
$$x = 25 \ // \ 4 \ * \ 3 + 18 \ \% \ 5 - 8$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate `//`, `*`, and `%` (left-to-right)
- `25 // 4 = 6` (since $25/4 = 6.25$, floor is $6$)
- `6 * 3 = 18`
- `18 % 5 = 3` (since $18 = 3 \times 5 + 3$)

**Step 4:** Substitute back
$$x = 18 + 3 - 8$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate `+` and `-` (left-to-right)
- `18 + 3 = 21`
- `21 - 8 = 13`

**Final Answer:** $x = 13$ (type `int`)

#### **Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

```python
import math

# Input radius from user
radius = float(input("Enter the radius: "))

# Calculate area and perimeter of circle
area_circle = math.pi * radius ** 2
perimeter_circle = 2 * math.pi * radius

# Calculate volume of sphere
volume_sphere = (4 / 3) * math.pi * radius ** 3

# Output formatted to 2 decimal places
print(f"Area of Circle      : {area_circle:.2f} sq.units")
print(f"Perimeter of Circle : {perimeter_circle:.2f} units")
print(f"Volume of Sphere    : {volume_sphere:.2f} cubic units")
```

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter the radius: 5
Area of Circle      : 78.54 sq.units
Perimeter of Circle : 31.42 units
Volume of Sphere    : 523.60 cubic units
```

**Explanation:**
- `math.pi` provides $\pi$ to 15 decimal places.
- We use `**` for exponentiation: `radius ** 2` computes $r^2$, `radius ** 3` computes $r^3$.
- `(4 / 3)` uses **true division** to produce `1.3333...` (a float), ensuring correct volume calculation. If `(4 // 3)` were used, it would equal `1`, giving wrong results.
- The `f"..."` format with `:.2f` displays exactly 2 decimal places.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice B** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** What is operator precedence? Explain with a neat table. Show how the expression `result = 5 + 2 * 3 ** 2 - 8 / 4` is evaluated.

**(b)** Write a Python program to **convert a temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit** and **compute the Body Mass Index (BMI)** for a given weight (kg) and height (m). Use appropriate arithmetic operators.

#### **Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Operator Precedence Definition:** Operator precedence is a set of rules that determines the order in which operators are evaluated in an expression. Operators with higher precedence are evaluated before operators with lower precedence. When operators of the same precedence appear, **associativity** (left-to-right or right-to-left) decides the evaluation order.

**Precedence Table:**

| Precedence | Operator(s) | Description | Associativity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `**` | Exponentiation | Right-to-left |
| 2 | `+x`, `-x`, `~x` | Unary | Right-to-left |
| 3 | `*`, `/`, `//`, `%` | Multiplicative | Left-to-right |
| 4 | `+`, `-` | Additive | Left-to-right |

**Evaluation of** `result = 5 + 2 * 3 ** 2 - 8 / 4`:

**Step 1:** `**` highest
$$3 \ ** \ 2 = 9$$

**Step 2:** `*` and `/` next (left-to-right)
$$2 \times 9 = 18$$
$$8 / 4 = 2.0 \text{ (float)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute
$$\text{result} = 5 + 18 - 2.0$$

**Step 4:** `+` and `-` (left-to-right)
$$5 + 18 = 23$$
$$23 - 2.0 = 21.0$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{result} = 21.0$ (type `float` due to `2.0`)

#### **Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Formulas:**

$$\text{Fahrenheit} = \left(\text{Celsius} \times \frac{9}{5}\right) + 32$$

$$\text{BMI} = \frac{\text{weight (kg)}}{\text{height (m)}^2}$$

```python
# Celsius to Fahrenheit conversion
celsius = float(input("Enter temperature in Celsius: "))
fahrenheit = (celsius * 9 / 5) + 32
print(f"Temperature in Fahrenheit: {fahrenheit:.2f} F")

# BMI calculation
weight = float(input("\nEnter weight in kg: "))
height = float(input("Enter height in meters: "))
bmi = weight / (height ** 2)
print(f"Body Mass Index (BMI): {bmi:.2f}")
```

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter temperature in Celsius: 37
Temperature in Fahrenheit: 98.60 F

Enter weight in kg: 70
Enter height in meters: 1.75
Body Mass Index (BMI): 22.86
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:**
> Students write `(celsius * 9) / 5 + 32` correctly but then forget the parentheses in the formula, writing `celsius * 9 / 5 + 32`. While this gives the *same* answer due to left-to-right associativity, it is **mathematically ambiguous**. Always parenthesize `9/5` for clarity: `(celsius * 9 / 5) + 32`.
>
> **Common Mistake #2:**
> Using integer division `//` in BMI formula: `weight // (height ** 2)` would give a **wrong truncated integer** answer. Use `/` (true division) for all real-world measurements.
>
> **Common Mistake #3:**
> Forgetting to import `math` when using `math.sqrt()` or `math.pi`. Even if you don't use `math`, declaring `import math` unnecessarily won't lose marks — but failing to import it when needed **will** cost you 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 2: Arithmetic Calculations**

### 🔹 Core Operator Facts
- **`/`** (true division) → **always** returns `float`, never truncated.
- **`//`** (floor division) → returns $\lfloor a/b \rfloor$, rounds toward **negative infinity**.
- **`%`** (modulo) → sign of result matches the **divisor**, NOT the dividend.
- **`**`** (exponentiation) → **right-associative** (the only right-associative binary operator).
- **Division Algorithm Identity:** $a = (a \ // \ b) \times b + (a \ \% \ b)$ — *always holds*.

### 🔹 Type Promotion Rules
- `int + int` → `int`
- `int + float` → `float` (int is "promoted" to float)
- `int + complex` → `complex`
- `float + complex` → `complex`
- **Hierarchy:** `int` ⊂ `float` ⊂ `complex`

### 🔹 Function Distinctions
- `pow(a, b)` ≡ `a ** b` (returns `int` if both `int`)
- `pow(a, b, m)` → computes $a^b \ \% \ m$ **efficiently** (used in RSA cryptography)
- `math.sqrt(x)` → returns `float`, requires $x \geq 0$
- `math.pow(a, b)` → **always returns `float`** (different from `**`!)
- `round(x, n)` → uses **banker's rounding** (rounds half to even)
- `divmod(a, b)` → returns tuple `(quotient, remainder)` in one operation

### 🔹 Critical Python vs Other Languages
- Python `int` has **arbitrary precision** — no overflow.
- Python `//` floors toward $-\infty$, unlike C/Java's truncation toward $0$.
- Python `%` follows divisor's sign, unlike C where `%` follows dividend's sign.

### 🔹 Common Module 2 Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here)
- Forgetting parentheses when combining `**` with unary `-`: `-3 ** 2` = `-(3**2)` = `-9`, not `9`.
- Using `//` when `/` is intended (or vice versa) in formulas.
- Forgetting to import `math` before calling its functions.
- Mixing up `math.pow()` (always float) with `**` (preserves int).
- Treating `round(2.5)` as `3` — banker's rounding gives `2`.

### 🔹 Memorize This Identity (Repeatedly Tested)
$$a = (a \ // \ b) \times b + (a \ \% \ b) \quad \text{for all } b \neq 0$$

### 🔹 KTU 2024 Likely Question Types
1. **Define & differentiate** `/` vs `//` (3 marks) — asked in almost every paper.
2. **Evaluate complex expression** with precedence (7 marks).
3. **Write a program** using arithmetic operators for a scientific formula (7 marks) — compound interest, kinetic energy, BMI, quadratic formula, etc.
4. **Explain `math` module** functions (3 marks).
5. **Trace output** of a snippet with negative modulo or floor division (3-7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/Output Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Input/Output Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Python programming** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, an **Input/Output (I/O) Operation** refers to the mechanism by which a program exchanges data with the external environment — typically the **standard input device (keyboard)**, the **standard output device (monitor/console)**, secondary storage (**files**), or the network. In Python, I/O is handled through a unified abstraction of **streams** (objects of type `TextIOBase` / `IOBase`) that conform to the **PEP 249** and **Python I/O hierarchy** standards.

The three canonical I/O channels defined by Python are:
- **`stdin`** — Standard input (default: keyboard)
- **`stdout`** — Standard output (default: monitor)
- **`stderr`** — Standard error (default: monitor, but unbuffered)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (UCEST105 – Module 2):** Students must master the built-in `input()` and `print()` functions, type conversion (casting), and formatted output using **f-strings (Python 3.6+)**, the `.format()` method, and the classical `%` operator. File handling (`open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `close()`, the `with` statement) is also a high-weightage area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen**:
- **Input** is like a **waiter taking your order** at the table. The chef (your program) cannot start cooking until the order (data) arrives through the waiter (`input()`).
- **Output** is like the **dish served back to you on a plate**. The chef must present the result (`print()`) in a readable, presentable format — otherwise the customer (user) cannot interpret it.
- **Type conversion** is the kitchen translating your order ("I'd like *two* of those") into a precise quantity the chef can measure — turning the *word* "two" into the *number* `2`.
- **File I/O** is the **pantry** — a long-term storage room where the chef stores leftovers (writes data) and retrieves ingredients (reads data) as needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Golden Rules of Python I/O:**
> 1. `input()` **always returns a `str`** — even if the user types `42`, Python receives the string `"42"`. You must explicitly cast it for arithmetic.
> 2. `print()` writes to `stdout` by default, appending a newline character `\n` automatically.
> 3. Files must be **opened** before use and **closed** after — preferably using the `with` context manager to prevent resource leaks.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol / Value | Meaning |
|------|----------------|---------|
| Default end-of-line character | `\n` | Newline (LF on Unix, CRLF translation on Windows) |
| Tab character | `\t` | Horizontal tab |
| Default `print` separator | ` ` | Single space (overridable via `sep`) |
| Default `print` terminator | `end='\n'` | Newline after each `print` call |
| `input()` prompt default | `None` (no prompt) | Optional string shown before reading |
| File open modes (text) | `'r'`, `'w'`, `'a'`, `'r+'` | Read, Write, Append, Read+Write |
| File open modes (binary) | `'rb'`, `'wb'`, `'ab'` | Same as above, but bytes |
| Standard encoding | `utf-8` | Default text encoding since Python 3 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `input()` Function — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The built-in `input()` function suspends program execution, reads a single line from standard input, strips the trailing newline, and returns the result as a **`str`**. Its signature is:

$$\text{input}(\text{prompt} = \text{None}) \rightarrow \text{str}$$

**Operational flow:**
1. The interpreter evaluates the optional `prompt` argument and writes it to `stdout` (without a trailing newline).
2. Execution **blocks** (pauses) until the user presses the **Enter** key.
3. The full line of text is read from the input buffer.
4. The trailing `\n` is stripped.
5. The remaining string is **returned** to the calling context.

**Why casting is mandatory:**

Because `input()` always returns a string, arithmetic operations on the result raise `TypeError`. Consider the logical flaw:

$$\text{user types } 5 \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{input()} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{returns } \texttt{"5"} \;(\text{str, not int})$$

Therefore, for numeric input, the canonical KTU pattern is:

$$\text{value} = \text{int}(\text{input}(\text{"Enter a number: "}))$$

## 2.2 The `print()` Function — Signature and Parameters

The signature (simplified) is:

$$\text{print}(*\text{objects}, \text{sep} = \text{' '}, \text{end} = \text{'}\backslash\text{n'}, \text{file} = \text{sys.stdout}, \text{flush} = \text{False})$$

- **`*objects`** — variadic; any number of values separated by commas.
- **`sep`** — the string placed *between* objects. Default is a single space.
- **`end`** — the string appended *after* the last object. Default is `\n`.
- **`file`** — any object with a `.write(string)` method. Default is `sys.stdout`.
- **`flush`** — boolean, forces immediate write to the stream (default `False`).

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU question asks: *"What will be the output of `print('a', 'b', sep='-', end='!')`?"*
> The answer is `a-b!` — note the absence of the default newline.

## 2.3 Formatted Output — The Three Methods

Python supports three equivalent ways to construct formatted strings. Each is examinable in the **KTU ESE**.

### Method 1 — f-strings (Recommended, PEP 498)

$$\text{f"Hello, \{name\}! You are \{age\} years old."}$$

**Advantages:** Fastest, most readable, supports expressions inside `{}`.

### Method 2 — `.format()` Method

$$\text{"Hello, \{0\}! You are \{1\} years old.".format(name, age)}$$

**Advantages:** Positional and keyword arguments, reusable templates.

### Method 3 — `%` Operator (C-style, legacy)

$$\text{"Hello, \%s! You are \%d years old." \% (name, age)}$$

**Advantages:** Compact for simple cases; widely seen in older KTU question papers.

## 2.4 Format Specifiers — Mini Language

Inside `{}` of an f-string or `.format()`, you can specify:

$$\{\text{value}:\text{fill} \text{align} \text{sign} \text{width} \text{,} \text{.precision} \text{type}\}$$

| Specifier | Meaning | Example | Result |
|-----------|---------|---------|--------|
| `:d` | Integer (base 10) | `f"{42:d}"` | `42` |
| `:f` | Fixed-point float | `f"{3.14159:.2f}"` | `3.14` |
| `:e` | Scientific notation | `f"{12345.6:.2e}"` | `1.23e+04` |
| `:b` | Binary | `f"{10:b}"` | `1010` |
| `:x` | Hexadecimal | `f"{255:x}"` | `ff` |
| `:o` | Octal | `f"{8:o}"` | `10` |
| `:<10` | Left-align, width 10 | `f"{'hi':<10}"` | `hi` (padded right) |
| `:>10` | Right-align, width 10 | `f"{'hi':>10}"` | `        hi` |
| `:^10` | Center, width 10 | `f"{'hi':^10}"` | `    hi    ` |
| `:_` | Thousands separator | `f"{1000000:_}"` | `1_000_000` |

## 2.5 File I/O — The `open()` Function

The canonical signature is:

$$\text{open}(\text{file}, \text{mode} = \text{'r'}, \text{buffering} = -1, \text{encoding} = \text{None}, \text{errors} = \text{None}, \text{newline} = \text{None})$$

**Mode matrix (high-yield for KTU):**

| Mode | Read | Write | Create | Truncate | Pointer Position |
|------|------|-------|--------|----------|------------------|
| `'r'` | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Beginning |
| `'w'` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Beginning |
| `'a'` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | End |
| `'r+'` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Beginning |
| `'w+'` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Beginning |
| `'a+'` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | End |
| `'x'` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Beginning (fails if exists) |

**The `with` statement (context manager) — KTU favourite:**

```python
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
# File is automatically closed here, even if an exception occurs.
```

## 2.6 Escape Characters and Raw Strings

| Escape | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `\n` | Newline |
| `\t` | Tab |
| `\\` | Backslash |
| `\'` | Single quote |
| `\"` | Double quote |
| `\r` | Carriage return |
| `\0` | Null character |

A **raw string** (prefix `r`) disables escape processing:
$$\texttt{r"C:\textbackslash nUsers\textbackslash nName"} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{C:\textbackslash nUsers\textbackslash nName}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why `input()` Returns a String (with Trace)

Let us trace the execution of a simple program to demonstrate the I/O pipeline.

**Program:**
```python
x = input("Enter a number: ")
y = x * 2
print("Result:", y)
```

**Trace table:**

| Step | Statement | Internal State | stdin | stdout |
|------|-----------|----------------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `input("Enter a number: ")` | Buffer flushed; program blocks | empty | `Enter a number: ` |
| 2 | User types `7` and presses Enter | Line `7\n` enters buffer | `7\n` | — |
| 3 | `\n` stripped | `x = "7"` (str) | — | — |
| 4 | `y = x * 2` | String repetition: `"7" * 2 = "77"` | — | — |
| 5 | `print("Result:", y)` | Writes `Result: 77\n` | — | `Result: 77\n` |

**Conclusion:** The variable `x` is the string `"7"`, not the integer `7`. String multiplication `*` repeats the string, hence `"77"`.

**Corrected version (with cast):**

| Step | Statement | Internal State |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | `x = int(input("Enter a number: "))` | `x = 7` (int) |
| 2 | `y = x * 2` | `y = 14` (int) |
| 3 | `print("Result:", y)` | Output: `Result: 14` |

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Output of `print()` with Custom `sep` and `end`

**Question:** What is the exact output?

```python
print("A", "B", "C", sep="-", end="*\n")
print("D")
```

**Step 1 — Evaluate the first `print`:**
- Objects: `"A"`, `"B"`, `"C"`
- Separator: `"-"` (replaces the default space)
- Terminator: `"*\n"`

Concatenation logic:
$$\text{output} = \text{"A"} \oplus \text{"-"} \oplus \text{"B"} \oplus \text{"-"} \oplus \text{"C"} \oplus \text{"*\textbackslash n"}$$

$$= \text{"A-B-C*\textbackslash n"}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the second `print`:**
- Object: `"D"`
- Default `sep=" "` (irrelevant, only one object)
- Default `end="\n"`

$$\text{output} = \text{"D"} \oplus \text{"\textbackslash n"} = \text{"D\textbackslash n"}$$

**Final combined output (rendered):**
```
A-B-C*
D
```

## 3.3 Derivation: f-string Format Specifier Mechanics

**Problem:** Format the value `123.456789` as a currency display with 2 decimal places, comma separators, and a leading `$` sign.

**Code:**
```python
amount = 1234567.891
print(f"Total: ${amount:,.2f}")
```

**Step-by-step resolution of the specifier `:,.2f`:**

1. **`f`** — fixed-point decimal notation. Required for floats.
2. **`.2`** — precision: round to 2 digits after the decimal point.
   $$\text{amount} = 1234567.891 \;\longrightarrow\; 1234567.89$$
3. **`,`** — thousands separator: insert `,` every three digits to the left of the decimal.
   $$1234567.89 \;\longrightarrow\; 1{,}234{,}567.89$$
4. **No width/padding** — the field occupies exactly the number of characters needed.

**Final output:**
$$\texttt{Total: \$1,234,567.89}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Reading a File Line-by-Line

**Problem:** Count the number of lines in `log.txt` that contain the word "ERROR".

**Algorithmic derivation:**

Let the file contain $n$ lines. We define a counter:

$$c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(\text{"ERROR"} \in \text{line}_i)$$

where $\mathbb{1}$ is the indicator function (1 if condition true, 0 otherwise).

**Python implementation:**

```python
count = 0
with open("log.txt", "r") as f:
    for line in f:
        if "ERROR" in line:
            count += 1
print(f"Error lines: {count}")
```

**Trace (assuming `log.txt` contains):**
```
INFO: System started
ERROR: Disk full
WARNING: Low memory
ERROR: Timeout
```

| Iteration | `line` (after `.strip()`) | Contains "ERROR"? | `count` |
|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | `INFO: System started` | No | 0 |
| 2 | `ERROR: Disk full` | Yes | 1 |
| 3 | `WARNING: Low memory` | No | 1 |
| 4 | `ERROR: Timeout` | Yes | 2 |

**Final output:** `Error lines: 2`

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: I/O Method Selection

| Scenario | Best Method | Reason |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Quick debug output | `print()` | Default to stdout, newline-terminated |
| Tabular data with alignment | f-string + `:<10`, `:>10` | Visual alignment in console |
| Reading entire small file | `f.read()` | Simple, loads all into memory |
| Reading large file line-by-line | `for line in f:` | Memory-efficient (iterator) |
| Writing log that must persist on crash | `f.flush()` or open with `buffering=1` | Forces write to disk |
| Avoiding resource leaks | `with open(...) as f:` | Auto-close on exit/exception |
| Capturing output of a function | `io.StringIO()` | In-memory text stream |
| Binary data (images, audio) | Open with `'rb'` or `'wb'` | Returns `bytes`, not `str` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Python I/O Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Keyboard] -->|"Types '42' + Enter"| B(stdin Buffer)
    B --> C["input(prompt) function"]
    C -->|"Returns '42' as str"| D[Variable Assignment]
    D --> E["int() / float() / Casting"]
    E --> F[Arithmetic / Logic]
    F --> G["print() function"]
    G -->|"sep, end params"| H(stdout Buffer)
    H --> I[Monitor Display]
    J[Secondary Storage - File] -->|"open file 'r'"| K[File Object]
    K --> L["read() / readline() / for-loop"]
    L --> F
    F --> M["write() to file 'w' / 'a'"]
    M --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — File Open Modes and Pointer Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> ReadMode: open file 'r'
    Closed --> WriteMode: open file 'w' (truncates)
    Closed --> AppendMode: open file 'a'
    ReadMode --> ReadWriteMode: open file 'r+'
    WriteMode --> Closed: close()
    AppendMode --> Closed: close()
    ReadWriteMode --> Closed: close()
    WriteMode --> Empty: pointer at byte 0
    AppendMode --> EOF: pointer at end
    ReadMode --> Beg: pointer at byte 0
    ReadMode --> Closed: close()
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct I/O Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Need to Read or Write Data?]) --> Q1{Source/Destination?}
    Q1 -->|Keyboard/Console Input| Q2{Need numeric processing?}
    Q1 -->|File| FileQ{Read or Write?}
    Q1 -->|In-memory| MemUse[StringIO / BytesIO]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Cast["input() → int() / float()"]
    Q2 -->|No| Direct["input() → str variable"]
    Cast --> Print[Use print to display]
    Direct --> Print
    FileQ -->|Read| ReadMode[open with 'r']
    FileQ -->|Write| WriteMode{Overwrite or Append?}
    WriteMode -->|Overwrite| WMode[open with 'w']
    WriteMode -->|Append| AMode[open with 'a']
    ReadMode --> ContextMgr[Use 'with' block]
    WMode --> ContextMgr
    AMode --> ContextMgr
    ContextMgr --> Done([I/O Complete])
    Print --> Done
    MemUse --> Done
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Data Flow During a `print()` Call

```
   +------------------+
   |  Python Program  |
   |                  |
   |  print("Hi", 42) |
   +--------+---------+
            |
            v   (str(42) implicit conversion)
   +------------------+
   |  Internal buffer |
   |  "Hi 42\n"       |
   +--------+---------+
            |
            v   (write() to file=sys.stdout)
   +------------------+
   |   sys.stdout     |
   |   (TextIOWrapper)|
   +--------+---------+
            |
            v   (OS-level system call)
   +------------------+
   |  Terminal / TTY  |
   |  "Hi 42"         |
   +------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between the `input()` and `raw_input()` functions in Python. Why does `input()` always return a string, and how would you convert the string `"123.45"` to a floating-point number?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **Python 2**, `raw_input()` read a line from the console and returned it as a `str`, while `input()` evaluated the entered text as a Python expression (a security risk). In **Python 3** (used in KTU 2024), `raw_input()` was **removed**, and `input()` behaves like the old `raw_input()` — it always returns a `str`.

The `input()` function reads a line of text from `stdin` and strips the trailing newline. It does not perform any automatic type inference because the underlying input device delivers **characters**, not typed values. Therefore, even if the user types `123`, the result is the three-character string `"123"`.

To convert `"123.45"` to a float:

```python
value = float("123.45")   # value = 123.45 (type: float)
```

The `float()` constructor parses the string according to Python's literal-float grammar and raises `ValueError` if parsing fails.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the role of the `sep` and `end` parameters of the `print()` function. What will be the output of the following code?**

```python
print("Python", "is", "awesome", sep="_", end="!!\n")
print("Welcome")
```

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`sep`** (separator): specifies the string inserted **between** multiple objects passed to `print()`. Default is a single space `' '`.
- **`end`** (terminator): specifies the string appended **after** the last object. Default is the newline `'\n'`.

In the given code, the first `print` joins `"Python"`, `"is"`, `"awesome"` with `"_"` and terminates with `"!!\n"`. The second `print` outputs `"Welcome"` followed by the default newline.

**Output:**
```
Python_is_awesome!!
Welcome
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students answer **one** full question (either OR choice).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Write a Python program that:
1. Accepts a student's name, roll number, and marks in three subjects from the user.
2. Computes the total and average.
3. Displays the result in a neatly formatted table using f-strings, with the average shown to 2 decimal places.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the differences between the `'r'`, `'w'`, and `'a'` file modes in Python. Write a program that reads a text file `input.txt`, counts the number of words in it, and writes the count to another file `output.txt`.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

```python
# Step 1: Read inputs with type casting where necessary
name = input("Enter student name: ")
roll = input("Enter roll number: ")
m1 = int(input("Marks in Subject 1: "))
m2 = int(input("Marks in Subject 2: "))
m3 = int(input("Marks in Subject 3: "))

# Step 2: Compute total and average
total = m1 + m2 + m3
average = total / 3

# Step 3: Display formatted output using f-strings
print("+" + "-" * 40 + "+")
print(f"| {'Student Report':^38} |")
print("+" + "-" * 40 + "+")
print(f"| {'Name':<12}: {name:<24} |")
print(f"| {'Roll No':<12}: {roll:<24} |")
print(f"| {'Subject 1':<12}: {m1:<24} |")
print(f"| {'Subject 2':<12}: {m2:<24} |")
print(f"| {'Subject 3':<12}: {m3:<24} |")
print(f"| {'Total':<12}: {total:<24} |")
print(f"| {'Average':<12}: {average:<24.2f} |")
print("+" + "-" * 40 + "+")
```

**Sample run:**
```
Enter student name: Anu
Enter roll number: R101
Marks in Subject 1: 85
Marks in Subject 2: 90
Marks in Subject 3: 78
+----------------------------------------+
|             Student Report             |
+----------------------------------------+
| Name        : Anu                      |
| Roll No     : R101                     |
| Subject 1   : 85                       |
| Subject 2   : 90                       |
| Subject 3   : 78                       |
| Total       : 253                      |
| Average     : 84.33                    |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**Mark allocation (a):**
- Correct input statements with casting: **2 marks**
- Correct computation of total and average: **1 mark**
- Formatted output using f-strings: **3 marks**
- Neat alignment and presentation: **1 mark**

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Theoretical comparison:**

| Mode | File Existence | Behaviour on Open | Pointer Position |
|------|----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| `'r'` | **Required** | Error if file missing | Beginning (byte 0) |
| `'w'` | Optional | Creates if missing; **truncates** (erases) if exists | Beginning (byte 0) |
| `'a'` | Optional | Creates if missing; **preserves** existing content | End-of-file |

`'r'` is **read-only**; `'w'` is **write-only** and destroys prior content; `'a'` is **write-only** and **appends** new content at the end.

**Program:**

```python
# Read input.txt, count words, write count to output.txt
with open("input.txt", "r") as src:
    content = src.read()                # reads entire file as a single string

words = content.split()                 # splits on any whitespace
word_count = len(words)

with open("output.txt", "w") as dst:
    dst.write(f"Total words: {word_count}\n")

print(f"Counted {word_count} words. Result written to output.txt")
```

**Explanation of key lines:**
- `with open("input.txt", "r") as src:` — opens `input.txt` in read mode; the `with` block guarantees the file closes automatically even if an error occurs.
- `content = src.read()` — reads the entire file into the string `content`.
- `content.split()` — splits the string on whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) and returns a list of word strings.
- `len(words)` — returns the integer count.
- The second `with` block opens `output.txt` in write mode and writes the formatted count.

**Mark allocation (b):**
- Clear explanation of three modes with a comparison: **3 marks**
- Correct use of `open()` and `with` statement: **2 marks**
- Correct word-counting logic: **1 mark**
- Writing result to file: **1 mark**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the three methods of string formatting in Python (f-strings, `.format()`, and `%` operator) with one example each. State two advantages of f-strings over the other two methods.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a Python program that:
1. Opens a file `data.txt` in append mode.
2. Accepts lines of text from the user until the user types `"STOP"`.
3. Writes each line to the file (excluding `"STOP"`).
4. After exiting the loop, reads the entire file and prints its contents with line numbers.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Method 1 — f-strings (PEP 498, Python 3.6+):**

```python
name = "Arun"
age = 20
print(f"My name is {name} and I am {age} years old.")
# Output: My name is Arun and I am 20 years old.
```

**Method 2 — `.format()` method:**

```python
print("My name is {} and I am {} years old.".format(name, age))
# Output: My name is Arun and I am 20 years old.
```

Positional and keyword variants are also allowed:
```python
print("{1} is {0} years old.".format(age, name))
```

**Method 3 — `%` operator (printf-style):**

| Specifier | Type |
|-----------|------|
| `%s` | String |
| `%d` | Integer |
| `%f` | Float |
| `%.2f` | Float, 2 decimals |

```python
print("My name is %s and I am %d years old." % (name, age))
# Output: My name is Arun and I am 20 years old.
```

**Two advantages of f-strings:**
1. **Better readability** — variables and expressions appear inline, making the code easier to write and maintain.
2. **Faster execution** — f-strings are evaluated at runtime *without* an intermediate format-string parsing step, so they are the fastest of the three methods (verified by Python's `timeit` benchmarks).

A third bonus advantage is that f-strings support **arbitrary expressions** inside `{}`, e.g., `f"{2 + 3 * 4}"` yields `14`.

**Mark allocation (a):**
- Correct example for each of the three methods: **3 marks (1 each)**
- Two valid advantages of f-strings: **2 marks**
- Clarity and additional notes (specifiers, types): **2 marks**

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

```python
# (1) Open data.txt in append mode
f = open("data.txt", "a")

# (2) Accept lines until "STOP"
print("Enter text lines (type STOP to finish):")
while True:
    line = input("> ")
    if line == "STOP":
        break
    f.write(line + "\n")     # (3) write each line, with newline

f.close()    # close the append-mode file

# (4) Re-open in read mode and print with line numbers
print("\n--- Contents of data.txt ---")
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    for i, line in enumerate(f, start=1):
        print(f"{i}: {line.rstrip()}")
```

**Sample run (assuming `data.txt` is initially empty):**
```
Enter text lines (type STOP to finish):
> Hello World
> Python is fun
> STOP

--- Contents of data.txt ---
1: Hello World
2: Python is fun
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- `open("data.txt", "a")` — append mode. If the file does not exist, it is created. If it exists, new data is added at the end without erasing existing content.
- The `while True` loop repeatedly prompts the user. The `break` statement exits when the user types `"STOP"`.
- `f.write(line + "\n")` writes each line followed by a newline so the file remains human-readable.
- `f.close()` releases the file handle. (Alternatively, the `with` statement could be used.)
- In the read phase, `enumerate(f, start=1)` yields `(line_number, line_content)` pairs starting from 1.
- `line.rstrip()` removes the trailing newline before printing to avoid double-spacing.

**Mark allocation (b):**
- Correct opening in append mode: **1 mark**
- Correct loop and `STOP` sentinel logic: **2 marks**
- Writing lines correctly: **1 mark**
- Reading and displaying with line numbers: **2 marks**
- Proper file closing / use of `with`: **1 mark**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cast `input()` to `int` / `float`** before arithmetic → causes `TypeError` at runtime. KTU deducts **2 marks** for this in Part B coding questions.
> 2. **Confusing `print` default `end='\n'`** with `input` — students often write `input("Enter: ")` expecting a newline after the prompt.
> 3. **Using `'w'` mode when `'a'` is required** (or vice versa) — both compile and run, but produce **wrong output**, costing full marks on the I/O sub-question.
> 4. **Forgetting to close the file** in programs that do *not* use `with` — partial deduction (**1 mark**) for resource-leak style errors.
> 5. **Mixing string and numeric types in f-strings** — `f"{x}"` where `x` is a number works, but writing `f"x = x + 1"` (without braces) is a *very common* silent failure.
> 6. **In format specifiers, swapping `:` and `,`** — e.g., writing `f"{x:,2f}"` (missing dot) raises `ValueError`; practice the exact grammar `{:,.2f}`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorise for KTU ESE):**
> - `input()` **always returns `str`**. Always cast: `int(input(...))`, `float(input(...))`.
> - `print()` defaults: `sep=' '`, `end='\n'`, `file=sys.stdout`.
> - Three formatting methods: **f-strings**, **`.format()`**, **`%` operator** — know all three; expect any in the paper.
> - Format specifier grammar: `{:fill align sign width , .precision type}`.
> - `:,.2f` = thousands separator + 2 decimal places (common exam question).
> - File modes to remember cold: `'r'` (read), `'w'` (write + truncate), `'a'` (append), `'r+'` (read+write, no truncate).
> - **Always** prefer `with open(...) as f:` to auto-close files.
> - `'w'` mode **erases** the existing file. `'a'` mode **preserves** it.
> - Binary files use `'b'` suffix (`'rb'`, `'wb'`) and return `bytes`, not `str`.
> - Raw strings `r"..."` disable escape-character processing.
> - `split()` with no argument splits on **any whitespace** and ignores empties — perfect for word counts.
> - `enumerate(iterable, start=n)` is the idiomatic way to get line numbers when reading a file.
> - `sys.stdin`, `sys.stdout`, `sys.stderr` are the three standard streams — `print(...)` and `input()` are thin wrappers around them.
> - The `flush=True` argument in `print()` forces immediate output, useful in progress bars and logging.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision (Selection) Symbol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision (Selection) Symbol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Decision (Selection) Symbol** in flowchart design is a standardized geometric shape — specifically a **rhombus (diamond)** — used to represent a conditional branch point in an algorithm. According to the **ISO 5807:1985** and **ANSI/ISO 5807-1985** standards for program flowcharts, the decision symbol denotes an operation that determines which of two or more alternative paths the program flow will follow based on a logical (Boolean) condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (UCEST105 - Module 2):**
> The decision symbol is the visual embodiment of **conditional logic** in algorithmic thinking. Every `if`, `if-else`, `elif`, nested `if`, and `switch-case` construct in Python ultimately maps back to one or more decision symbols in its flowchart representation.

### Key Properties of the Decision Symbol

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Geometric Shape** | Rhombus / Diamond |
| **Number of Entry Arrows** | Exactly **1** (one) |
| **Number of Exit Arrows** | **2** (binary) or **3+** (multi-way) |
| **Label Inside** | A Boolean condition or relational expression |
| **Exit Labels** | `True` / `False` (or `Yes` / `No`, or specific values) |
| **Standard Reference** | ISO 5807:1985, ANSI X3.5-1970 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Boolean Condition:**
> A Boolean condition is any expression that evaluates to exactly one of two values: `TRUE` (1) or `FALSE` (0). Examples include `x > 10`, `age >= 18`, `marks >= 50 AND marks < 60`.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Signal Junction

Imagine you are driving a train along a single railway track. As you approach a **signal junction (a railway switch)**, the signal operator checks one critical question: *"Is the main line clear of traffic?"*

- If the answer is **YES (TRUE)**, the switch opens toward the **mainline track** — your train continues on its primary route.
- If the answer is **NO (FALSE)**, the switch diverts you onto a **siding (loop track)** — an alternative path that may be a detour, a station stop, or an error-handling route.

The **railway switch (junction)** is your **Decision Symbol**. The **operator's question** is your **Boolean condition**. The **two possible tracks emerging from the switch** are your **True/False exit arrows**. The train itself is the **program flow / control flow** that takes exactly one path based on the decision.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works for KTU exams:**
> Examiners often award marks to answers that link abstract logic to real-world scenarios. The railway-signal metaphor demonstrates *Apply* (Bloom Level 3) understanding, which is heavily tested in KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions.

---

## 1.3 Why the Diamond Shape? A Design Rationale

The diamond is chosen deliberately because:
1. It is **visually distinct** from rectangles (process) and ovals (terminal), preventing ambiguity in large flowcharts.
2. Its **four corners** naturally suggest a *branching* structure.
3. The **top and bottom corners** act as natural entry/exit points, leaving the **left and right corners** for the two outgoing paths.

```
        Entry (one arrow in)
            ▲
           ╱ ╲
          ╱   ╲
         ╱ C ? ╲
          ╲   ╱
           ╲ ╱
            ▼
   ┌──── False (left)    True (right) ────┐
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake (Loses 1–2 Marks):**
> Students often draw **two entry arrows** into a decision symbol or label the diamond with *actions* (e.g., "Calculate Sum") instead of *conditions* (e.g., "Sum > 100?"). The diamond is **strictly for conditions**, not for processing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Decision Symbol — Structured Logic Breakdown

A decision symbol performs a **three-stage logical operation** every time program flow reaches it:

1. **Evaluation Stage** — The Boolean condition written inside the diamond is evaluated using the current values of the variables.
2. **Branching Stage** — Based on the result (`TRUE` or `FALSE`), the control flow is directed to **exactly one** of the outgoing arrows.
3. **Re-convergence Stage** — Both outgoing paths (and any sub-flows) typically re-merge at a later **connector** or **process** symbol, after which execution continues normally.

### The Three Logical Forms of Decision Symbols

**Form 1 — Binary (Two-Way) Decision**
The most common form. Evaluates a single Boolean expression and offers two exit paths.

```
        ╱╲
       ╱  ╲
      ╱ a  ╲──── TRUE ──► [Path A]
      ╲ > b╱
       ╲  ╱
        ╲╱
         │
         └── FALSE ──► [Path B]
```

**Form 2 — Multi-Way (Case-Type) Decision**
A generalized decision symbol that tests a single variable against multiple possible values, producing *n* outgoing arrows. In Python, this is implemented using `if-elif-else` chains.

**Form 3 — Nested Decision**
A decision symbol whose outgoing paths themselves contain additional decision symbols, creating a decision tree of arbitrary depth.

---

## 2.2 Boolean Logic Foundation — The Engine Inside the Diamond

The decision symbol is a *visual representation* of Boolean algebra. Every condition inside a diamond is built from:

| Operator | Symbol (Math) | Python Equivalent | Truth Value Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AND (Conjunction)** | $\land$ or $\cdot$ | `and` | TRUE only if **both** operands are TRUE |
| **OR (Disjunction)** | $\lor$ or $+$ | `or` | TRUE if **at least one** operand is TRUE |
| **NOT (Negation)** | $\lnot$ or $\overline{x}$ | `not` | Inverts the Boolean value |
| **XOR (Exclusive OR)** | $\oplus$ | `^` (bitwise) or `!=` (logical) | TRUE if operands differ |
| **Equality** | $=$ | `==` | TRUE if both operands are identical |
| **Inequality** | $\neq$ | `!=` | TRUE if operands differ |
| **Less Than** | $<$ | `<` | Standard numeric comparison |
| **Greater Than** | $>$ | `>` | Standard numeric comparison |
| **Less Than or Equal** | $\le$ | `<=` | Standard numeric comparison |
| **Greater Than or Equal** | $\ge$ | `>=` | Standard numeric comparison |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the logical equivalences — KTU frequently asks "Simplify the following Boolean expression" questions worth 3–7 marks.

| # | Law / Identity | Logical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identity Law** | $A \lor 0 = A$ \quad $A \land 1 = A$ | Simplifying conditional checks |
| 2 | **Domination Law** | $A \lor 1 = 1$ \quad $A \land 0 = 0$ | Short-circuit reasoning |
| 3 | **Idempotent Law** | $A \lor A = A$ \quad $A \land A = A$ | Removing duplicate tests |
| 4 | **Complement Law** | $A \lor \lnot A = 1$ \quad $A \land \lnot A = 0$ | Branching certainty proofs |
| 5 | **Double Negation** | $\lnot(\lnot A) = A$ | Inverting conditions |
| 6 | **De Morgan's Law 1** | $\lnot(A \land B) = \lnot A \lor \lnot B$ | `not(a and b)` $\equiv$ `not a or not b` |
| 7 | **De Morgan's Law 2** | $\lnot(A \lor B) = \lnot A \land \lnot B$ | `not(a or b)` $\equiv$ `not a and not b` |
| 8 | **Commutative Law** | $A \lor B = B \lor A$ \quad $A \land B = B \land A$ | Order-independent decisions |
| 9 | **Associative Law** | $(A \lor B) \lor C = A \lor (B \lor C)$ | Grouping nested decisions |
| 10 | **Distributive Law** | $A \land (B \lor C) = (A \land B) \lor (A \land C)$ | Expanding compound conditions |
| 11 | **Absorption Law** | $A \lor (A \land B) = A$ | Eliminating redundant checks |
| 12 | **Conditional Identity** | $A \to B = \lnot A \lor B$ | Translating `if A: B` to Boolean |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Boolean logic embedded in decision symbols underpins every digital circuit, microcontroller instruction, network routing protocol, database query optimizer, and machine-learning classifier. The decision symbol in a flowchart is essentially a **software abstraction of a hardware multiplexer (MUX)** — an $n$-to-1 digital switch.

---

## 2.4 The Decision Symbol as a Multiplexer Analogy

In digital electronics, a **2-to-1 Multiplexer (MUX)** selects one of two input data lines and forwards it to a single output, governed by a *select line* (which is essentially a 1-bit decision).

| Multiplexer Concept | Decision Symbol Concept |
|---|---|
| Select Line (S) | Boolean Condition |
| Input 0 (D0) | FALSE branch path |
| Input 1 (D1) | TRUE branch path |
| Output (Y) | Next executed process |

This is why mastering the decision symbol is a **gateway skill** — the same logical thinking transfers directly into digital logic design, embedded systems programming, and even AI decision trees.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Truth Table Derivation for a Composite Decision Condition

Consider the decision condition most commonly tested in KTU exams:

$$C = (A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C)$$

We will derive the complete truth table **exhaustively** — every row, every step.

### Step 1 — Identify the Number of Variables

The condition has three Boolean variables: $A$, $B$, and $C$.

The total number of rows in the truth table is:

$$N_{rows} = 2^{n} = 2^{3} = 8$$

### Step 2 — Construct the Truth Table Header

| Row | A | B | C | A ∧ B | ¬A | ¬A ∧ C | (A ∧ B) ∨ (¬A ∧ C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | ? | ? | ? | ? |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ? | ? | ? | ? |

### Step 3 — Evaluate Row by Row

**Row 1:** $A=0, B=0, C=0$

- $A \land B = 0 \land 0 = 0$
- $\lnot A = \lnot 0 = 1$
- $\lnot A \land C = 1 \land 0 = 0$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 0 \lor 0 = 0$

**Row 2:** $A=0, B=0, C=1$

- $A \land B = 0 \land 0 = 0$
- $\lnot A = \lnot 0 = 1$
- $\lnot A \land C = 1 \land 1 = 1$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 0 \lor 1 = 1$

**Row 3:** $A=0, B=1, C=0$

- $A \land B = 0 \land 1 = 0$
- $\lnot A = \lnot 0 = 1$
- $\lnot A \land C = 1 \land 0 = 0$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 0 \lor 0 = 0$

**Row 4:** $A=0, B=1, C=1$

- $A \land B = 0 \land 1 = 0$
- $\lnot A = 1$
- $\lnot A \land C = 1 \land 1 = 1$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 0 \lor 1 = 1$

**Row 5:** $A=1, B=0, C=0$

- $A \land B = 1 \land 0 = 0$
- $\lnot A = 0$
- $\lnot A \land C = 0 \land 0 = 0$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 0 \lor 0 = 0$

**Row 6:** $A=1, B=0, C=1$

- $A \land B = 1 \land 0 = 0$
- $\lnot A = 0$
- $\lnot A \land C = 0 \land 1 = 0$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 0 \lor 0 = 0$

**Row 7:** $A=1, B=1, C=0$

- $A \land B = 1 \land 1 = 1$
- $\lnot A = 0$
- $\lnot A \land C = 0 \land 0 = 0$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 1 \lor 0 = 1$

**Row 8:** $A=1, B=1, C=1$

- $A \land B = 1 \land 1 = 1$
- $\lnot A = 0$
- $\lnot A \land C = 0 \land 1 = 0$
- $(A \land B) \lor (\lnot A \land C) = 1 \lor 0 = 1$

### Step 4 — Compile the Final Truth Table

| Row | A | B | C | A ∧ B | ¬A | ¬A ∧ C | **C Output** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **0** |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **1** |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **0** |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **1** |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| 7 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **1** |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **1** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This truth table can be **synthesized into a digital logic circuit** using a Karnaugh map (K-map) and implemented with three AND gates, one OR gate, and one NOT gate — a topic revisited in *Digital Electronics* (KTU course EST130).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Symbol Mapping to Python Constructs

| Flowchart Decision Symbol | Python Equivalent Construct | Number of Decision Symbols |
|---|---|---|
| Single binary decision | `if condition:` | **1** |
| If-Else | `if condition: ... else: ...` | **1** |
| If-Elif-Else chain (n branches) | `if/elif/elif/.../else` | **n** |
| Nested If (depth 2) | `if outer: if inner: ...` | **2** |
| Switch-like (Python 3.10+) | `match-case` | **n** (cases) + 1 default |
| Ternary operator | `x = A if cond else B` | **1** |
| Logical AND short-circuit | `if A and B:` | **2** (one per operand) |
| Logical OR short-circuit | `if A or B:` | **2** (one per operand) |

### Worked Example — Mapping a Nested Decision to Python

Consider this KTU-exam-style nested decision: *"Find the largest of three numbers a, b, c."*

The flowchart will contain **two decision symbols** in a nested structure. Let us trace the logic mathematically.

$$
L = \begin{cases}
a & \text{if } a \ge b \text{ AND } a \ge c \\
b & \text{if } b > a \text{ AND } b \ge c \\
c & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Derivation of the second condition:**

The condition "$b$ is the largest" is logically:

$$b > a \;\land\; b \ge c$$

**Proof that the three conditions are mutually exclusive:**

- If $a$ is largest, then $a \ge b$ and $a \ge c$, so $b > a$ is FALSE — the second condition fails.
- If $b$ is largest, then $b > a$ is TRUE, but the first condition requires $a \ge b$, which is FALSE.
- Therefore, the two conditions cannot both be TRUE simultaneously. $\blacksquare$

**Proof that the three conditions are collectively exhaustive:**

Any input where neither $a$ nor $b$ is largest must mean $c$ is largest (or tied for largest). The `else` branch in Python handles this automatically. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Decision Complexity Analysis

For an algorithm with $k$ sequential decision symbols, each binary, the total number of possible execution paths is:

$$P_{total} = 2^{k}$$

| Number of Binary Decisions (k) | Total Execution Paths |
|---|---|
| 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 8 |
| 5 | 32 |
| 10 | **1,024** |
| 20 | **1,048,576** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *"determine the number of test cases"* for a flowchart, count the decision symbols $k$ and compute $2^{k}$ (or more for multi-way decisions). This is the foundation of **basis path testing** in software engineering.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Single Decision (If-Else) Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read value of x/]
    B --> C{x > 50?}
    C -- TRUE --> D[/Print "Pass"/]
    C -- FALSE --> E[/Print "Fail"/]
    D --> F([End])
    E --> F
```

### Diagram Interpretation Table

| Node ID | Visual Shape | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| A | Stadium/Terminal (rounded) | START of program flow |
| B | Parallelogram | INPUT operation (Read x) |
| C | Diamond (Rhombus) | **DECISION SYMBOL** — Boolean test |
| D | Parallelogram | OUTPUT on TRUE branch |
| E | Parallelogram | OUTPUT on FALSE branch |
| F | Stadium/Terminal | END of program flow |

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Multi-Way Decision (If-Elif-Else Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read marks/]
    B --> C{marks >= 90?}
    C -- TRUE --> D[/Print Grade A/]
    C -- FALSE --> E{marks >= 75?}
    E -- TRUE --> F[/Print Grade B/]
    E -- FALSE --> G{marks >= 50?}
    G -- TRUE --> H[/Print Grade C/]
    G -- FALSE --> I[/Print Grade D/]
    D --> J([End])
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Above Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER [Input Layer]
        R1[/Read marks from user/]
    end

    subgraph DECISION_TREE [Decision Tree Layer - 3 Decision Symbols]
        D1{marks >= 90?}
        D2{marks >= 75?}
        D3{marks >= 50?}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER [Output Layer]
        O1[/Display Grade A/]
        O2[/Display Grade B/]
        O3[/Display Grade C/]
        O4[/Display Grade D/]
    end

    R1 --> D1
    D1 -- TRUE --> O1
    D1 -- FALSE --> D2
    D2 -- TRUE --> O2
    D2 -- FALSE --> D3
    D3 -- TRUE --> O3
    D3 -- FALSE --> O4
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Nested Decision (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read a, b, c/]
    B --> C{a >= b?}
    C -- TRUE --> D{a >= c?}
    D -- TRUE --> E[/Print 'a is largest'/]
    D -- FALSE --> F[/Print 'c is largest'/]
    C -- FALSE --> G{b >= c?}
    G -- TRUE --> H[/Print 'b is largest'/]
    G -- FALSE --> I[/Print 'c is largest'/]
    E --> J([End])
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** Notice how the **outer decision** (C: a >= b?) partitions the search space into two halves. The **inner decisions** (D and G) further refine the result. This is identical to the structure of a **binary search algorithm**, where each decision symbol halves the remaining possibilities. For $n$ elements, this gives a time complexity of $O(\log_2 n)$.

---

## 4.4 Decision Symbol Anatomy — Close-Up Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DECISION_ANATOMY [Anatomy of a Decision Symbol]
        TOP[Top Corner - Single Entry Point]
        LEFT[Left Corner - FALSE Exit Arrow]
        RIGHT[Right Corner - TRUE Exit Arrow]
        BOTTOM[Bottom Corner - Auxiliary or Continuation]
        CORE[Inside: Boolean Condition Expression]
    end

    TOP --> CORE
    CORE --> LEFT
    CORE --> RIGHT
    CORE -.Optional.-> BOTTOM
```

### Standardized Labeling Conventions (KTU-Compliant)

| Exit Direction | Acceptable Labels |
|---|---|
| Right / Down-Right | `TRUE`, `YES`, `Y`, `1` |
| Left / Down-Left | `FALSE`, `NO`, `N`, `0` |
| Multi-way exits | `= value1`, `= value2`, `= value3` |
| Default branch | `default` or `else` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfall:**
> If a student writes a decision symbol **without labeling the exit arrows** (True/False), they lose at least **1 mark** in the 7-mark flowchart-drawing sub-question. The examiner's official key explicitly checks for arrow labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
