
### 4.3 Top-3 Functional Decomposition Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeInput["Input List L of n integers"]
    nodeInit["Initialize first minus infinity second minus infinity third minus infinity"]
    nodeLoop["FOR each element x in L"]
    nodeCheck1{"x greater than first?"}
    nodeShiftA["third = second second = first first = x"]
    nodeCheck2{"x greater than second?"}
    nodeShiftB["third = second second = x"]
    nodeCheck3{"x greater than third?"}
    nodeShiftC["third = x"]
    nodeNext["Continue loop"]
    nodeOutput["Output first second third as Top 3"]

    nodeInput --> nodeInit
    nodeInit --> nodeLoop
    nodeLoop --> nodeCheck1
    nodeCheck1 -- YES --> nodeShiftA
    nodeCheck1 -- NO --> nodeCheck2
    nodeShiftA --> nodeNext
    nodeCheck2 -- YES --> nodeShiftB
    nodeCheck2 -- NO --> nodeCheck3
    nodeShiftB --> nodeNext
    nodeCheck3 -- YES --> nodeShiftC
    nodeCheck3 -- NO --> nodeNext
    nodeShiftC --> nodeNext
    nodeNext --> nodeLoop
    nodeLoop --> nodeOutput

    style nodeInput fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeOutput fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeInit fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Python Reference Implementation (KTU Lab-Ready)

```python
def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1:
        return arr
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    left = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    right = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    return merge(left, right)


def merge(left, right):
    result = []
    i = j = 0
    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
        if left[i] <= right[j]:
            result.append(left[i])
            i += 1
        else:
            result.append(right[j])
            j += 1
    result.extend(left[i:])
    result.extend(right[j:])
    return result


def top_three(arr):
    first = second = third = float('-inf')
    for x in arr:
        if x > first:
            third = second
            second = first
            first = x
        elif x > second:
            third = second
            second = x
        elif x > third:
            third = x
    return first, second, third


# Demo
data = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]
print("Sorted:", merge_sort(data))
print("Top 3:", top_three(data))
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Viva Note:** Always remember to handle the **base case** `len(arr) <= 1` in `merge_sort`. Forgetting it causes infinite recursion and a stack overflow. This is a frequently asked viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define decomposition in the context of computational thinking. Differentiate between recursive and functional decomposition with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

Decomposition is the process of breaking a complex problem into smaller, independent sub-problems. **Recursive decomposition** splits a problem into smaller instances of *itself* — for example, Merge Sort decomposes the sorting of n elements into sorting of two halves of size n/2. **Functional decomposition** splits a problem into *different* sub-tasks, each performing a distinct function — for example, finding the Top 3 integers is decomposed into three find-max sub-tasks, each excluding the previous winners. (3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for distinction with examples)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Why is Merge Sort preferred over Bubble Sort when sorting large datasets stored on disk?

**Model Answer:**

Merge Sort exhibits a **sequential access pattern** during the merge phase, making it ideal for disk-based (external) sorting because sequential reads/writes are orders of magnitude faster than random access on magnetic storage. Additionally, Merge Sort guarantees **Θ(n log n)** worst-case performance, whereas Bubble Sort degrades to **Θ(n²)**. For terabyte-scale data, the gap between Θ(n log n) and Θ(n²) is the difference between hours and weeks of computation. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**(a)** Write the recurrence relation for Merge Sort and solve it using the recursion tree method to show that $T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Trace Merge Sort on the list **[40, 10, 30, 20, 50, 5, 25]** showing all divide and merge steps. Count the total number of comparisons. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The recurrence relation for Merge Sort is:

$$
T(n) = 2 \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n), \quad T(1) = \Theta(1)
$$

The Θ(n) term represents the cost of merging two sorted sub-arrays of total size n.

**Recursion Tree:**

| Level i | Number of sub-problems | Size of each sub-problem | Work per level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | n | n |
| 1 | 2 | n/2 | n |
| 2 | 4 | n/4 | n |
| … | … | … | … |
| i | $2^i$ | $n / 2^i$ | n |
| log₂ n | n | 1 | n |

**Derivation of work at level i:**

$$
W_i = 2^i \cdot \frac{n}{2^i} = n
$$

**Total work (summing all levels from 0 to log₂ n):**

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n} W_i = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n} n = n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = n \log_2 n + n
$$

Since $n \log_2 n$ dominates asymptotically:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)
$$

Hence proved. (7 marks: 1 recurrence + 4 tree analysis + 2 final conclusion)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Divide phase (top-down):**

- [40, 10, 30, 20, 50, 5, 25] → [40, 10, 30, 20] | [50, 5, 25]
- [40, 10, 30, 20] → [40, 10] | [30, 20]
- [40, 10] → [40] | [10]
- [30, 20] → [30] | [20]
- [50, 5, 25] → [50, 5] | [25]
- [50, 5] → [50] | [5]

**Merge phase (bottom-up):**

- Merge [40] and [10] → [10, 40] (1 comparison)
- Merge [30] and [20] → [20, 30] (1 comparison)
- Merge [50] and [5] → [5, 50] (1 comparison)
- Merge [10, 40] and [20, 30] → [10, 20, 30, 40] (3 comparisons)
- Merge [5, 50] and [25] → [5, 25, 50] (2 comparisons)
- Merge [10, 20, 30, 40] and [5, 25, 50] → [5, 10, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50] (6 comparisons)

**Total comparisons:** 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 2 + 6 = **14 comparisons**

**Final sorted list:** [5, 10, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50] (7 marks: 4 for correct trace + 1 for comparison count + 2 for final answer)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the functional decomposition approach to find the top three largest integers in a list of *n* integers. Write the algorithm in Python. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the input list **[15, 7, 23, 9, 31, 4, 18, 23, 11]**, trace the single-pass top-3 algorithm showing the state of all three trackers after each element. State the time and space complexity. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Functional Decomposition Explanation:**

The problem "find top 3 largest" is decomposed into three **functional sub-tasks**, each performing the same primitive operation (find-max) but on a reduced problem space that excludes the previous winners. This is **functional decomposition** because the sub-tasks are *distinct in scope* (each excludes prior answers) yet follow a *common pattern*. The optimized single-pass version avoids the O(n log n) cost of sorting by maintaining only three variables (`first`, `second`, `third`) and updating them in O(1) per element.

**Python Algorithm:**

```python
def top_three(arr):
    if len(arr) < 3:
        return sorted(arr, reverse=True)
    first = second = third = float('-inf')
    for x in arr:
        if x > first:
            third = second
            second = first
            first = x
        elif x > second:
            third = second
            second = x
        elif x > third:
            third = x
    return first, second, third
```

**Working principle:** Each `if-elif` chain ensures that at most one of the three trackers is updated per element. The cascade (third ← second ← first ← x) is the *shift-down* operation that maintains the invariant: `first ≥ second ≥ third` after every iteration. (7 marks: 3 for explanation + 4 for algorithm)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Initial state:** `first = -∞`, `second = -∞`, `third = -∞`

| Step | Element x | first | second | third | Action Triggered |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | 15 | -∞ | -∞ | x > first → shift first down |
| 2 | 7 | 15 | 7 | -∞ | x > second → update second |
| 3 | 23 | 23 | 15 | 7 | x > first → shift all down |
| 4 | 9 | 23 | 15 | 9 | x > third → update third |
| 5 | 31 | 31 | 23 | 15 | x > first → shift all down |
| 6 | 4 | 31 | 23 | 15 | no update |
| 7 | 18 | 31 | 23 | 18 | x > third → update third |
| 8 | 23 | 31 | 23 | 18 | x > second → update second (shift third ← old second = 15, then third ← old second wait) — re-trace: first=31, second was 23, third was 18; x=23 > second(23)? No, equal — using strict `>` no update. With `>=` second would update. Standard KTU answer: **no update** (x not greater than second) |
| 9 | 11 | 31 | 23 | 18 | no update |

**Final Top 3:** first = **31**, second = **23**, third = **18**

**Time complexity:** $T(n) = \Theta(n)$ — one pass, O(1) work per element.

**Space complexity:** $S(n) = \Theta(1)$ — only three tracker variables, no extra data structures.

(7 marks: 5 for trace table + 1 for time + 1 for space)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** `if len(arr) <= 1: return arr` in Merge Sort → causes infinite recursion → **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Writing O(n)** instead of **O(n log n)** for Merge Sort → this is a **3-mark deduction** error, as the recurrence clearly shows the extra log factor.
> 3. **Off-by-one in merge** — copying remaining elements after the loop is mandatory; forgetting `result.extend(left[i:])` and `result.extend(right[j:])` causes lost elements.
> 4. **Tie-breaking ambiguity** in Top-3 — students often get confused when equal elements appear. The KTU convention is to use **strict `>`** so that an equal value does not displace a current tracker; mention this explicitly in your answer.
> 5. **Confusing "Top 3" with "3 largest distinct"** — clarify in your answer whether duplicates count. Default KTU assumption: duplicates are counted (you can have two 22s as 1st and 2nd).
> 6. **Slicing cost** in `arr[:mid]` and `arr[mid:]` — although Python list slicing is O(k), in algorithmic analysis we treat it as a separate cost. Mention that optimized versions use index ranges to avoid the O(n) slicing cost per recursion level.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Decomposition** = breaking a problem into smaller sub-problems. Two flavours: **Recursive** (self-similar, e.g., Merge Sort) and **Functional** (distinct sub-tasks, e.g., Top-3).
- **Merge Sort** uses the **Divide and Conquer** paradigm: Divide → Recurse → Merge. It is a **stable**, **comparison-based**, **out-of-place** sort.
- The recurrence is $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$, solved by the **Master Theorem Case 2** to give $T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$.
- The recursion tree has $\log_2 n$ levels, each contributing $n$ work → total $n \log_2 n$.
- The **merge** sub-routine runs in linear time using the **two-pointer technique**; both pointers advance monotonically.
- **Merge Sort space** is $\Theta(n)$ due to the auxiliary array. It is NOT in-place.
- **Top-3 selection** using a single pass with three trackers is $\Theta(n)$ time and $\Theta(1)$ space — strictly better than sort-and-slice for k = 3.
- The three trackers must be updated in **cascading** order: `third ← second ← first ← x` to preserve the invariant `first ≥ second ≥ third`.
- **Tie-breaking** in Top-3: use strict `>` to avoid displacing equal trackers; this preserves stability of first-occurrence.
- For large datasets on disk, Merge Sort is preferred due to **sequential access pattern**.
- The **decomposition** lesson from these two problems: *a well-decomposed problem* has sub-problems whose partial solutions combine *easily* (Merge Sort: trivial merge; Top-3: just read trackers).
- Always include the **base case** in recursive algorithms; always **state complexities** in Big-Θ notation in KTU answers; always **show work in the recursion tree** for full marks.
- KTU expects **closed-form time complexity** in the form $T(n) = n \log_2 n + n$, not just $\Theta(n \log_2 n)$, when deriving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursion: Definition and Reasons for Using Recursion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recursion: Definition and Reasons for Using Recursion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recursion** is a problem-solving technique in computer science and algorithmic thinking in which a **function calls itself**, either directly or indirectly, to solve a smaller instance of the same problem until a terminating condition (called the **base case**) is reached.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (UCEST105 – Algorithmic Thinking with Python), recursion is treated as one of the foundational paradigms of algorithm design, sitting alongside *iteration*, *divide-and-conquer*, and *greedy* approaches. It is formally classified under **inductive problem solving**, where the solution to a problem of size $n$ is expressed in terms of the solution to a problem of size $n-1$, $n/2$, or some reduced form.

A recursive algorithm is defined mathematically as a **piecewise function** of the form:

$$
T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Base Case (Direct Answer)} & \text{if } n \leq \text{threshold} \\
\text{Recursive Case (Function on smaller input)} & \text{if } n > \text{threshold}
\end{cases}
$$

Here, $T(n)$ denotes the work performed by the algorithm on input of size $n$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of recursion as nested Russian Dolls (Matryoshka dolls).**

Imagine you are holding a large painted wooden doll. To find the smallest doll inside, you do the following:

1. You **open the current doll** (this is the **recursive call** — you are now handling a *smaller* doll).
2. You repeat step 1 on the newly revealed doll.
3. The moment you find a doll that **cannot be opened** (a solid, tiny doll), you **stop** (this is the **base case**).
4. Then you begin **closing back** the dolls in reverse order (this is the **return phase** or *unwinding the stack*).

The same mental model applies to recursion in Python:
- The **call** = opening a doll.
- The **base case** = the smallest, unopenable doll.
- The **return** = putting the dolls back together.

Another classic analogy is **two mirrors facing each other** — each reflection is a smaller version of the original scene, eventually fading to a vanishing point (the base case).

---

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the following terms — they are **guaranteed** to appear in Part A (3-mark) questions:

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Base Case** | The terminating condition that stops further recursive calls. |
| **Recursive Case** | The branch where the function invokes itself with a smaller input. |
| **Call Stack** | The runtime data structure that stores active function invocations. |
| **Stack Overflow** | Runtime error when recursion depth exceeds the call-stack limit (default ≈ **1000 frames** in CPython). |
| **Direct Recursion** | Function $A()$ calls $A()$ itself. |
| **Indirect Recursion** | Function $A()$ calls $B()$, which in turn calls $A()$. |
| **Tail Recursion** | The recursive call is the **last** operation in the function. |
| **Head Recursion** | The recursive call occurs **before** any other processing. |
| **Recursion Depth** | The total number of pending (unresolved) recursive calls at any moment. |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

This topic is the **gateway** to Module 3, which covers recursion, searching, and sorting. Almost every advanced algorithm taught later — *Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Tree Traversals, Backtracking* — relies on the principles defined here. KTU examiners routinely test this topic for **6 to 14 marks** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Recursive Function

Every well-formed recursive function in Python contains **three mandatory components**:

1. **Base Case (Termination Condition)**
   - The simplest sub-problem that can be solved *without* further recursion.
   - Without it, the function will recurse infinitely and crash with a `RecursionError`.

2. **Recursive Case (Reduction Step)**
   - The branch that transforms the current problem into a **smaller, similar** problem.
   - Each call must strictly *progress toward* the base case (e.g., $n \to n-1$ or $n \to n/2$).

3. **Return Statement**
   - Either returns the **base case value directly**, or combines the result of the recursive call with the current frame's work.

### Canonical Python Template

```python
def recursive_function(n):
    # --- BASE CASE ---
    if n <= 1:               # Stopping condition
        return 1             # Direct answer
    
    # --- RECURSIVE CASE ---
    return n * recursive_function(n - 1)   # Calls itself on smaller input
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "smaller" matters:** If the recursive call does not shrink the input, the function never reaches the base case. The parameter must converge toward the base-case condition mathematically.

---

## 2.2 The Two Phases of Recursion

Recursive execution always proceeds in **two distinct phases**:

| Phase | Direction | What Happens |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Winding (Calling Phase)** | Top → Bottom | Function calls push new frames onto the call stack. |
| **Unwinding (Returning Phase)** | Bottom → Top | Base case triggers; results propagate back through pending frames. |

This is the **single most important conceptual point** for KTU board exams. Many 7-mark questions ask students to *trace* the call stack for a given recursive function.

---

## 2.3 Mathematical Representation: The Recurrence Relation

A recursive algorithm's **time complexity** is expressed as a **recurrence relation** $T(n)$.

### General Form

$$
T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)
$$

Where:
- $a$ = number of sub-problems generated per call.
- $b$ = factor by which the input shrinks.
- $f(n)$ = cost of work done *outside* the recursive calls (dividing, combining, etc.).

### Common Patterns You Must Memorize

| Recurrence Form | Algorithm Example | Closed-Form Solution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | Linear Recursion (e.g., factorial) | $T(n) = O(n)$ |
| $T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n/2) + c$ | Binary Recursion (e.g., tree height) | $T(n) = O(n)$ |
| $T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n/2) + n$ | Merge Sort | $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ | Binary Search | $T(n) = O(\log n)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ | Fibonacci (naive) | $T(n) = O(2^n)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above table, $c$ represents a **constant-time operation** (e.g., one multiplication, one comparison). The symbol $\log$ without a base implies base 2 in algorithmic analysis.

---

## 2.4 The 7 Core Reasons for Using Recursion (KTU High-Yield List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"List any four reasons for using recursion."* Memorize all seven — they map directly to the syllabus outcomes.

1. **Natural Fit for Self-Similar Problems** — Problems like fractals, trees, and grammar parsing have a structure that *is* recursive.

2. **Elegant and Concise Code** — A 2-line recursive function replaces a 10-line iterative loop (compare factorial implementations below).

3. **Backtracking Made Easy** — Algorithms exploring all possibilities (N-Queens, Sudoku, maze solving) rely on recursion for clean *try → recurse → undo* logic.

4. **Inherent to Certain Data Structures** — Trees, linked lists, and graphs are defined recursively; their traversals (in-order, pre-order, post-order, DFS) are inherently recursive.

5. **Divide and Conquer** — Algorithms that split a problem (e.g., Merge Sort, Quick Sort) are most naturally expressed recursively.

6. **Mathematical Induction Alignment** — Recursive code mirrors the inductive proof structure: assume true for $n-1$, prove for $n$.

7. **Dynamic Programming Foundations** — Memoized and tabulated DP solutions are built on top of recursive sub-problem decomposition.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Recursive Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Compiler Design** | Parsing expression trees, recursive-descent parsers. |
| **Computer Graphics** | Fractal generation (Mandelbrot, Sierpinski triangle). |
| **Artificial Intelligence** | Game-tree search (Minimax, Alpha-Beta Pruning). |
| **Operating Systems** | Recursive directory traversal, process forking trees. |
| **Bioinformatics** | Phylogenetic tree construction, sequence alignment. |
| **Network Routing** | Recursive DNS resolution, hierarchical routing protocols. |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Pin this table. Every entry is a high-yield exam item for KTU 2024 ESE.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | Linear recursion complexity | Factorial, sum-of-n |
| $T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n/2) + n$ | Divide-and-conquer complexity | Merge Sort |
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ | Halving recursion | Binary Search |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ | Branching recursion | Naive Fibonacci |
| $S(n) = O(n)$ | Stack space used | Any depth-$n$ recursion |
| $\text{Master Theorem case 1}$ | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ |
| $\text{Master Theorem case 2}$ | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log n)$ |
| $\text{Master Theorem case 3}$ | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Time Complexity of Factorial

**Problem:** Compute the time complexity $T(n)$ of the following recursive factorial function.

```python
def factorial(n):
    if n <= 1:                # Line 2: Base case check
        return 1              # Line 3: Constant time
    return n * factorial(n-1) # Line 4: One multiplication + one recursive call
```

### Step-by-Step Construction of the Recurrence

**Step 1 — Identify the base case cost.**
When $n \leq 1$, the function performs exactly **one comparison** and **one return**, both of which are $O(1)$.

$$
T(1) = c_1
$$

where $c_1$ is a small constant (e.g., $c_1 = 2$ clock cycles in a real CPU).

**Step 2 — Identify the recursive case cost.**
For $n > 1$, the function performs:
- One comparison (`n <= 1`) → $O(1)$
- One multiplication (`n * ...`) → $O(1)$
- One recursive call to `factorial(n-1)` → $T(n-1)$

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + c_2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the recurrence by repeated substitution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c_2 \\
     &= [T(n-2) + c_2] + c_2 = T(n-2) + 2c_2 \\
     &= [T(n-3) + c_2] + 2c_2 = T(n-3) + 3c_2 \\
     &= \;\;\vdots \\
     &= T(n-k) + k \cdot c_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The substitution continues until the base case $T(1)$ is reached, which happens when $k = n-1$:

$$
T(n) = T(1) + (n-1) \cdot c_2 = c_1 + (n-1) \cdot c_2
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Big-O notation.**

Since $c_1$ and $c_2$ are constants, the dominant term is linear in $n$:

$$
T(n) = c_1 + (n-1) \cdot c_2 = \Theta(n)
$$

Therefore, the **time complexity of recursive factorial is $O(n)$**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Stack Space of Recursive Factorial

Each recursive call pushes a new **stack frame** onto Python's call stack. For `factorial(n)`, the call chain is:

$$
\text{fact}(n) \to \text{fact}(n-1) \to \text{fact}(n-2) \to \cdots \to \text{fact}(1)
$$

This is a **chain of $n$ pending frames**. The space occupied is:

$$
S(n) = n \cdot \text{sizeof(Frame)} = O(n)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This $O(n)$ auxiliary space is the **principal drawback** of recursion compared to an iterative loop, which uses $O(1)$ space. KTU examiners often contrast these two implementations for full 14 marks.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Fibonacci Naive Recurrence

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ with $T(0) = T(1) = 1$.

**Step 1 — Recognize the closed form.** This recurrence corresponds to the Fibonacci numbers, so $T(n) = F_{n+1}$.

**Step 2 — Use the characteristic equation.** Assume a solution of the form $T(n) = r^n$:

$$
r^n = r^{n-1} + r^{n-2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r^2 - r - 1 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the quadratic.**

$$
r = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 4}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

So the two roots are the **golden ratio** $\varphi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ and $\hat{\varphi} = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx -0.618$.

**Step 4 — General solution.**

$$
T(n) = A \cdot \varphi^n + B \cdot \hat{\varphi}^n
$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial conditions** $T(0) = T(1) = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(0) = A + B &= 1 \\
T(1) = A\varphi + B\hat{\varphi} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving yields:

$$
A = \frac{\varphi}{\sqrt{5}}, \quad B = -\frac{\hat{\varphi}}{\sqrt{5}}
$$

**Step 6 — Asymptotic simplification.** Since $\mid \hat{\varphi} \mid < 1$, the second term vanishes for large $n$:

$$
T(n) = \frac{\varphi^{n+1}}{\sqrt{5}} \quad \text{(Binet's Formula)}
$$

In Big-O terms:

$$
T(n) = O(\varphi^n) = O(2^n) \quad \text{(since } \varphi < 2\text{)}
$$

This **exponential** complexity is the canonical example of *why* naive recursion is dangerous for KTU board problems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Recursion vs Iteration

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks comparison questions. Memorize this matrix verbatim.

| Parameter | Recursion | Iteration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Function calls itself | Loop repeats a block of code |
| **Termination** | Base case condition | Loop condition becomes false |
| **Memory** | $O(n)$ call-stack frames | $O(1)$ auxiliary variables |
| **Speed** | Slower (function-call overhead) | Faster (no overhead) |
| **Code Size** | Shorter, more elegant | Often longer |
| **Risk** | Stack overflow on deep recursion | Infinite loop on bad condition |
| **Best For** | Trees, graphs, divide-and-conquer | Linear scans, accumulators |
| **Readability** | High for recursive structures | High for linear processes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursive Call Stack — Factorial of 5

This diagram traces the **winding** and **unwinding** phases when computing `factorial(5)`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["fact 5  n=5  return 5 * fact(4)"] --> B["fact 4  n=4  return 4 * fact(3)"]
    B --> C["fact 3  n=3  return 3 * fact(2)"]
    C --> D["fact 2  n=2  return 2 * fact(1)"]
    D --> E["fact 1  n=1  BASE CASE  return 1"]
    E --> D2["fact 2 returns 2 * 1 = 2"]
    D2 --> C2["fact 3 returns 3 * 2 = 6"]
    C2 --> B2["fact 4 returns 4 * 6 = 24"]
    B2 --> A2["fact 5 returns 5 * 24 = 120"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style D2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style C2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style B2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Orange frames** = Winding phase (calls being pushed onto the stack).
- **Green frame** = Base case (the termination point).
- **Blue frames** = Unwinding phase (values being returned upward).
- **Pink frame** = Final result delivered to the original caller.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Recursion Works

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        P["Problem of size n"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["RECURSIVE ENGINE"]
        BC{"Base Case\nReached?"}
        DC["Direct Answer\nreturn constant"]
        RC["Recursive Call\nsolve sub-problem n-1"]
        COMBINE["Combine sub-result\nwith current frame"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        RES["Final Result"]
    end

    P --> BC
    BC -- "Yes n le 1" --> DC --> RES
    BC -- "No n gt 1" --> RC --> COMBINE --> RES
    COMBINE -. "feeds back as smaller n" .-> BC

    style BC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style DC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style RC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style COMBINE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style RES fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:3px
```

**Interpretation:**
1. The **Input Layer** supplies a problem of size $n$.
2. The **Recursive Engine** checks the base case.
3. If satisfied → **Direct Answer** path executes (constant time).
4. If not → **Recursive Call** reduces the problem to size $n-1$ and loops back.
5. The **Combine** stage fuses the sub-result with the current frame's contribution.
6. The **Output Layer** yields the final answer.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact mental model KTU examiners expect when asking *"Explain the working of recursion with a block diagram."*

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Choose Recursion vs Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Is the problem\nself-similar?"] -->|"No"| USEIT["Use Iteration"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2["Is the depth\npredictable and small?"]
    Q2 -->|"No n greater than ~1000"| WARN["Stack Overflow Risk\nuse Iteration + explicit stack"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| Q3["Does an iterative\nsolution exist?"]
    Q3 -->|"No natural loop"| USEREC["Use Recursion"]
    Q3 -->|"Yes but uglier"| Q4["Is code clarity\nthe priority?"]
    Q4 -->|"Yes"| USEREC
    Q4 -->|"No performance critical"| USEIT

    style WARN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style USEREC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style USEIT fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

This is the **practical decision tree** a Python developer follows — and a fantastic diagram to reproduce in a 14-mark KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define recursion. State any two reasons for using recursion.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Recursion is a programming technique in which a function solves a problem by **calling itself** with a smaller or simpler input until it reaches a **base case** that can be solved directly without further recursion.

**Any two reasons (1 mark total, 0.5 each):**
1. Recursion provides an **elegant, concise** solution for problems that are naturally self-similar (e.g., tree traversals, factorial, Fibonacci).
2. It is the **natural paradigm for divide-and-conquer** algorithms such as Merge Sort, Quick Sort, and Binary Search, where the problem is split into sub-problems of the same form.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a base case in recursion? Why is it essential?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> [!NOTE]
> A **base case** is the terminating condition of a recursive function — the simplest sub-problem whose answer is known *without* requiring any further recursive call (e.g., `if n <= 1: return 1` in factorial).

**Why essential (2 marks):**
- Without a base case, the function would call itself **infinitely**, eventually exhausting the call stack and raising a `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded`.
- It provides the **anchor point** that allows the recursion to terminate and the **unwinding phase** to begin propagating results back to the original caller.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering 14-mark questions, students commonly lose marks by: (a) forgetting to **trace the call stack diagram**, (b) not mentioning **both time AND space complexity**, (c) writing code **without explaining the base case and recursive case separately**, and (d) confusing **direct vs indirect recursion**. Address all four points explicitly to secure full marks.

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Define recursion. Explain the **two phases** of recursive execution with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python function to compute the **factorial of a number using recursion**. Trace the call stack for `factorial(4)` and determine its **time and space complexity**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Recursion is a method of solving a problem where the solution depends on **solutions to smaller instances of the same problem**. A recursive function is one that **calls itself** during its execution, with each call reducing the problem size, until a **base case** is hit.

**Two Phases (4 marks):**

| Phase | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Winding (Calling Phase)** | The function makes successive recursive calls, each pushing a new **stack frame** onto the call stack. The argument $n$ shrinks at each step (e.g., $n \to n-1$). |
| **Unwinding (Returning Phase)** | When the base case is reached, control returns to the previous frame, which then computes its final value and returns to *its* caller, and so on, until the original call is resolved. |

**Neat Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid call-stack diagram in **Section 4.1** above. Draw a vertical stack showing `fact(3) → fact(2) → fact(1) → return 1 → return 2 → return 6`.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Python Code (3 marks):**

```python
def factorial(n):
    # Base case
    if n <= 1:
        return 1
    # Recursive case
    return n * factorial(n - 1)

print(factorial(4))   # Output: 24
```

**Call-Stack Trace for `factorial(4)` (3 marks):**

| Call # | Function Call | Action | Returns To |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `factorial(4)` | Sees `4 > 1`, calls `factorial(3)` | Pending |
| 2 | `factorial(3)` | Sees `3 > 1`, calls `factorial(2)` | Pending |
| 3 | `factorial(2)` | Sees `2 > 1`, calls `factorial(1)` | Pending |
| 4 | `factorial(1)` | **Base case hit** → returns `1` | Call 3 |
| 5 | Back in `factorial(2)` | Computes `2 * 1 = 2` → returns `2` | Call 2 |
| 6 | Back in `factorial(3)` | Computes `3 * 2 = 6` → returns `6` | Call 1 |
| 7 | Back in `factorial(4)` | Computes `4 * 6 = 24` → returns `24` | Original caller |

**Complexity Analysis (1 mark):**

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T(n) = O(n) \text{ time},\quad S(n) = O(n) \text{ space}
$$

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain **any four reasons** for using recursion in algorithm design. Compare recursion with iteration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a recursive Python function to compute the **n-th Fibonacci number**. Show the **recurrence relation** and derive its **time complexity** using repeated substitution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Four Reasons for Using Recursion (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Natural Fit for Self-Similar Problems:** Tree traversals, fractals, and grammar parsers have an inherently recursive structure. Writing them iteratively would require manually managing an explicit stack and is far more error-prone.

2. **Concise and Readable Code:** A recursive factorial fits in 3 lines, while its iterative counterpart needs 4–5 lines with explicit loop variables. For complex algorithms like Quick Sort, the recursive form is dramatically shorter.

3. **Backtracking:** Problems requiring *try → recurse → undo* logic (N-Queens, Sudoku, mazes) are cleanest in recursion because the call stack automatically handles the "undo" on return.

4. **Divide and Conquer:** Algorithms like Merge Sort and Binary Search divide the problem into sub-problems, solve them recursively, and combine the results — recursion is the natural paradigm.

**Recursion vs Iteration (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Recursion | Iteration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Memory | $O(n)$ stack frames | $O(1)$ |
| Speed | Slower (call overhead) | Faster |
| Termination | Base case | Loop condition |
| Best Suited For | Trees, divide-and-conquer | Linear sequences |

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Python Code (2 marks):**

```python
def fibonacci(n):
    if n <= 1:                  # Base cases: F(0)=0, F(1)=1
        return n
    return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)
```

**Recurrence Relation (2 marks):**

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c, \quad T(0) = T(1) = c
$$

**Derivation by Repeated Substitution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c \\
     &= [T(n-2) + T(n-3) + c] + [T(n-3) + T(n-4) + c] + c \\
     &= T(n-2) + 2T(n-3) + T(n-4) + 3c \\
     &= \;\;\vdots
\end{aligned}
$$

The recurrence $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2)$ corresponds to Fibonacci growth, which solves via the **characteristic equation** $r^2 = r + 1$, yielding $r = \varphi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$. Thus:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(\varphi^n) = O(2^n)
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The naive recursive Fibonacci has **exponential time complexity**, which is why dynamic programming (memoization) is needed in practice.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — read this three times before the exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Recursion = a function calling itself with a smaller input until a **base case** is reached.
- ✅ **Two mandatory parts of any recursive function:** **Base case** (stopping) and **Recursive case** (self-call with reduced input).
- ✅ **Two phases:** **Winding** (pushing frames) and **Unwinding** (returning values).
- ✅ **Default Python recursion limit:** approximately **1000 frames** — beyond this, a `RecursionError` occurs.
- ✅ **Stack space** for a recursive function of depth $n$ is always $O(n)$, regardless of how efficient the work is per frame.
- ✅ **Master the time complexities:** Factorial = $O(n)$, Merge Sort = $O(n \log n)$, Binary Search = $O(\log n)$, Naive Fibonacci = $O(2^n)$.
- ✅ **Seven reasons to use recursion:** self-similarity, code elegance, backtracking, recursive data structures, divide-and-conquer, mathematical induction alignment, DP foundation.
- ✅ **Direct recursion** = $A \to A$; **Indirect recursion** = $A \to B \to A$.
- ✅ **Tail recursion** is *not* optimized in Python (unlike Scheme or Haskell) — do not assume tail-call optimization when explaining performance.
- ✅ **Always draw the call-stack trace** in 14-mark answers — it is the single fastest way to earn the "diagram" mark.
- ✅ **Compare recursion with iteration** whenever asked — examiners love this and it costs you nothing to add.
- ✅ **Memorize the Master Theorem cases** — they appear in nearly every KTU algorithm complexity question.
- ✅ **Use Binet's formula** $F_n = \frac{\varphi^n - \hat{\varphi}^n}{\sqrt{5}}$ as the closed-form derivation if asked to "solve" the Fibonacci recurrence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning:** Many students write recursive code in exams but **forget to handle the case $n = 0$ or $n = 1$** in Fibonacci. The base cases are `F(0) = 0` AND `F(1) = 1` — both must be present, or you lose 1 mark immediately. Also, never write `if n == 1: return 1` *without* handling $n = 0$ — it is a **recurring** (pun intended) mistake in KTU answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Call Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Call Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Call Stack (also known as Execution Stack, Runtime Stack, or Machine Stack):**
> A *Last-In, First-Out (LIFO)* data structure maintained by the Python interpreter (CPython) that tracks the active function invocations of a running program. Each time a function is called, a new **Stack Frame (Activation Record)** is *pushed* onto the call stack; when the function returns, its frame is *popped* off.

In the context of **Algorithmic Thinking with Python**, the call stack is the backbone that enables:
- **Function composition** (calling functions from within functions)
- **Recursion** (a function calling itself)
- **Exception propagation** (errors bubble up the stack)
- **Local scope management** (each frame has its own namespace)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cafeteria Tray" Model

Imagine a **spring-loaded cafeteria tray dispenser**:
- A new tray is placed **on top** when you arrive (a function is called).
- You can only take food from the **top tray** (the most recently added one).
- When you finish with a tray, you remove it from the top (function returns).

> [!NOTE]
> **LIFO Principle in Plain English:** The last function that gets called is the **first** one to finish. This is why deeply nested calls unwind in reverse order — exactly like a stack of books where you can only pick up the topmost book.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Python Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Stack Frame** | A single "block" storing one function's state | Created automatically per call |
| **Push Operation** | Adding a new frame to the top | Function invocation |
| **Pop Operation** | Removing the top frame | `return` statement / end of function |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | Pointer to the topmost frame | Maintained by interpreter |
| **Base Pointer (BP)** | Pointer to the current frame's base | Used for local variable access |
| **Stack Overflow** | Stack exceeds memory limit | `RecursionError` in Python |
| **Default Python Recursion Limit** | **1000** frames | `sys.getrecursionlimit()` |

> [!TIP]
> Python's default recursion limit is **1000** calls. You can check it with `import sys; print(sys.getrecursionlimit())`. This is a **board-favorite** fact — remember it for the exam!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Stack Frame

Every time Python calls a function, it allocates a new **frame object** containing the following:

1. **Function Reference** — pointer to the function's code object.
2. **Local Namespace (`locals()`)** — a mapping of local variable names to values.
3. **Arguments (Parameters)** — values passed to the function.
4. **Return Address** — where to resume execution after the function completes.
5. **Instruction Pointer (IP)** — the next bytecode line to execute.
6. **Cell/Closure Variables** — for nested functions (free variables).

## 2.2 Operational Flow — The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The call stack operates on a strict **discipline of three phases** for every function call:

**Phase 1 — Push (Call)** 🔽
- The interpreter suspends the caller.
- It allocates a new frame in memory.
- Parameters are bound, and the IP is set to the callee's first instruction.

**Phase 2 — Execute (Active)** ⚙️
- The callee runs, reading/writing only to **its own** frame's locals.
- If the callee calls *another* function, a new frame is pushed **on top**, blocking the callee.

**Phase 3 — Pop (Return)** 🔼
- A `return` statement produces a value.
- The frame is destroyed, and its locals are freed.
- Control returns to the caller's saved IP, with the returned value in hand.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Complexity | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stack Push | $T_{\text{push}} = O(1)$ | Constant time | Amortized |
| Stack Pop | $T_{\text{pop}} = O(1)$ | Constant time | Constant time |
| Stack Peek (Top) | $T_{\text{peek}} = O(1)$ | Constant time | Read-only |
| Recursion Depth | $D = n$ for n nested calls | Linear | Frames in memory |
| Total Stack Space | $S_{\text{stack}} = O(n)$ | Linear | Worst case |
| Time per Frame | $\Theta(1)$ overhead | Constant | For push + pop |
| Python Recursion Limit | $L_{\max} \approx 1000$ | Hard limit | Adjustable via `sys.setrecursionlimit()` |
| Fibonacci Recursive Calls | $C(n) = 2 \cdot C(n-1) + O(1)$ | $O(2^n)$ | Exponential blow-up |
| Factorial Stack Depth | $D_{\text{fact}}(n) = n$ | Linear | Tail recursion *not* optimized in Python |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Math:** Each stack frame in CPython occupies roughly **1–8 KB** depending on local variables. So $D = 1000$ frames can consume **8 MB** of stack memory — a major reason recursion has a hard limit.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Symbol tables and scope resolution use a stack-based approach (the very same principle).
- **Operating Systems:** Context switching saves CPU state onto a kernel stack.
- **Debugging Tools:** `pdb` (Python Debugger) and IDEs visualize the call stack as a "backtrace."
- **Browser Engines:** JavaScript engines (V8, SpiderMonkey) use a call stack for event loop handling.
- **Embedded Systems:** RTOS task stacks must be sized carefully to avoid overflow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Call-Stack Trace — The `factorial()` Function

Let's derive the complete call-stack behavior for `factorial(3)` step by step.

### 3.1.1 The Python Code Under Analysis

```python
def factorial(n):
    if n == 0:        # Base case
        return 1
    else:             # Recursive case
        return n * factorial(n - 1)

result = factorial(3)
```

### 3.1.2 The Derivation — Frame-by-Frame Build-up

We trace what happens at each bytecode step. Let $\mathcal{F}(n)$ denote the frame created for `factorial(n)`.

**Step 1: Initial Call**

The global scope pushes a *module-level* frame. Then `factorial(3)` is invoked.

$$
\text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}, \mathcal{F}(3)]
$$

**Step 2: Inside `factorial(3)`**

The function checks `n == 0`. Since $3 \neq 0$, it executes the recursive call `factorial(2)`. A new frame is **pushed**.

$$
\text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}, \mathcal{F}(3), \mathcal{F}(2)]
$$

**Step 3: Inside `factorial(2)`**

Again, $2 \neq 0$, so `factorial(1)` is called. Push.

$$
\text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}, \mathcal{F}(3), \mathcal{F}(2), \mathcal{F}(1)]
$$

**Step 4: Inside `factorial(1)`**

Again, $1 \neq 0$, so `factorial(0)` is called. Push.

$$
\text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}, \mathcal{F}(3), \mathcal{F}(2), \mathcal{F}(1), \mathcal{F}(0)]
$$

**Step 5: Base Case Hit**

Inside `factorial(0)`, the condition `n == 0` is **True**. The function returns `1`. The frame is **popped**.

$$
\text{Return Value: } 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}, \mathcal{F}(3), \mathcal{F}(2), \mathcal{F}(1)]
$$

**Step 6: Unwinding `factorial(1)`**

The expression `1 * factorial(0)` is now `1 * 1 = 1`. Returns `1`. Pop.

$$
\text{Return Value: } 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}, \mathcal{F}(3), \mathcal{F}(2)]
$$

**Step 7: Unwinding `factorial(2)`**

The expression `2 * factorial(1)` is now `2 * 1 = 2`. Returns `2`. Pop.

$$
\text{Return Value: } 2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}, \mathcal{F}(3)]
$$

**Step 8: Unwinding `factorial(3)`**

The expression `3 * factorial(2)` is now `3 * 2 = 6`. Returns `6`. Pop.

$$
\text{Return Value: } 6 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{CallStack} = [\text{GLOBAL}]
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\text{result} = 6
$$

### 3.1.3 Stack Depth Equation

For any input $n \geq 0$, the maximum stack depth is given by:

$$
D_{\text{max}}(n) = n + 1
$$

This is because we hold the current frame plus the base-case frame at the deepest point. The **space complexity** of recursive `factorial` is therefore:

$$
S_{\text{recursion}} = \Theta(n)
$$

## 3.2 The "Two-Stack" Mental Model (Analytical Matrix)

| Phase | Action | Top of Stack | Stack Size | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Call `factorial(3)` | Push $\mathcal{F}(3)$ | $\mathcal{F}(3)$ | 1 | ⬆ Growing |
| Call `factorial(2)` | Push $\mathcal{F}(2)$ | $\mathcal{F}(2)$ | 2 | ⬆ Growing |
| Call `factorial(1)` | Push $\mathcal{F}(1)$ | $\mathcal{F}(1)$ | 3 | ⬆ Growing |
| Call `factorial(0)` | Push $\mathcal{F}(0)$ | $\mathcal{F}(0)$ | 4 (Peak) | ⬆ Peak |
| Return from `factorial(0)` | Pop, return 1 | $\mathcal{F}(1)$ | 3 | ⬇ Shrinking |
| Return from `factorial(1)` | Pop, return 1 | $\mathcal{F}(2)$ | 2 | ⬇ Shrinking |
| Return from `factorial(2)` | Pop, return 2 | $\mathcal{F}(3)$ | 1 | ⬇ Shrinking |
| Return from `factorial(3)` | Pop, return 6 | GLOBAL | 0 | ⬇ Empty |

## 3.3 Stack Overflow — Formal Derivation

The maximum recursion depth $D_{\max}$ in CPython is bounded by the operating system's allocated thread stack size $M_{\text{stack}}$ and per-frame size $F_{\text{frame}}$:

$$
D_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{\text{stack}}}{F_{\text{frame}}} \right\rfloor
$$

For Python's default (where $M_{\text{stack}} \approx 8\,\text{MB}$ and $F_{\text{frame}} \approx 8\,\text{KB}$):

$$
D_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{8 \times 1024}{8} \right\rfloor = 1024 \approx 1000
$$

When $D > D_{\max}$, Python raises:

$$
\text{RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Call Stack Build-up and Unwinding (Factorial of 3)

The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the **push** and **pop** operations of the call stack as Python executes `factorial(3)`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Program Start]) --> pushGlobal[Push GLOBAL Frame]
    pushGlobal --> callF3[Call factorial 3]
    callF3 --> pushF3[Push Frame F3 with n=3]
    pushF3 --> check3{n equals 0?}
    check3 -- No --> callF2[Call factorial 2]
    callF2 --> pushF2[Push Frame F2 with n=2]
    pushF2 --> check2{n equals 0?}
    check2 -- No --> callF1[Call factorial 1]
    callF1 --> pushF1[Push Frame F1 with n=1]
    pushF1 --> check1{n equals 0?}
    check1 -- No --> callF0[Call factorial 0]
    callF0 --> pushF0[Push Frame F0 with n=0]
    pushF0 --> check0{n equals 0?}
    check0 -- Yes --> ret0[Return 1 and Pop F0]
    ret0 --> unwind1[Compute 1 times 1 equals 1]
    unwind1 --> ret1[Return 1 and Pop F1]
    ret1 --> unwind2[Compute 2 times 1 equals 2]
    unwind2 --> ret2[Return 2 and Pop F2]
    ret2 --> unwind3[Compute 3 times 2 equals 6]
    unwind3 --> ret3[Return 6 and Pop F3]
    ret3 --> doneA([Result equals 6])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stack Frame Architecture

The following block diagram details the **internal structure of a single stack frame** as managed by the CPython interpreter.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRAME["STACK FRAME for factorial n"]
        direction TB
        A1["Function Code Pointer"]
        A2["Return Address to Caller"]
        A3["Global Namespace Reference"]
        A4["Local Variables dict: locals of n, return value"]
        A5["Instruction Pointer IP next bytecode"]
        A6["Cell and Free Variables for closures"]
    end
    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A6
    A6 --- BELOW["Points to NEXT FRAME below in stack"]
    BELOW --- BASE["Stack Pointer SP at TOP of stack"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Call Stack Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> Pushing: Function Called
    Pushing --> Active: Frame on Top
    Active --> Pushing: Inner Function Called
    Pushing --> Active: Frame on Top
    Active --> Popping: Return Statement Hit
    Popping --> Active: Previous Frame Resumes
    Popping --> Empty: All Frames Returned
    Empty --> Overflow: Depth exceeds 1000
    Overflow --> [*]: RecursionError Raised
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1: Define the Call Stack. Mention the LIFO principle.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **call stack** is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure maintained by the Python interpreter to track active function calls. When a function is called, a **stack frame** containing parameters, local variables, and the return address is *pushed* onto the top. When the function completes, the frame is *popped* and control returns to the caller.
>
> The **LIFO principle** states that the *last* function pushed is the *first* to be popped — analogous to a stack of plates. (3 marks)

---

### ❓ Question 2: What is Stack Overflow? How does Python handle it?
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Stack Overflow** occurs when the call stack exceeds its allocated memory limit, typically due to unbounded recursion. (1 mark)
>
> In Python, the interpreter enforces a default recursion limit of **1000 frames** to prevent infinite recursion. (1 mark)
>
> When exceeded, Python raises a `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded` exception. This can be inspected or modified using `sys.getrecursionlimit()` and `sys.setrecursionlimit()`. (1 mark)

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** confuse the *call stack* with a *general-purpose* data structure — it is managed by the interpreter, not by you.
> - **Do not** forget to draw the **stack frame contents** in the diagram; merely listing "push/pop" loses **2–3 marks**.
> - **Always** state the **base case** explicitly when tracing recursion. Examiners deduct marks for assuming it.
> - For Part B questions, **trace each step** (frame creation, return value, pop). Skipping steps is the #1 reason for losing the **7 marks** allocated to the trace sub-part.

---

### ✏️ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Adapted from KTU Dec 2022 Pattern]**

#### (a) Explain the structure of a stack frame with a neat diagram. List the components stored in it. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **stack frame** (also called an *activation record*) is the unit of data pushed onto the call stack for each function call. In CPython, it is implemented as a `PyFrameObject`.

**Components stored in a stack frame (7 marks for complete listing + diagram):**

1. **Function Code Object** — pointer to the compiled bytecode of the function. (1 mark)
2. **Return Address** — instruction pointer of the caller, so execution can resume correctly. (1 mark)
3. **Local Namespace (`f_locals`)** — a dictionary storing local variables. (1 mark)
4. **Global Namespace Reference (`f_globals`)** — pointer to the module's globals, for variable lookup. (1 mark)
5. **Built-in Namespace Reference (`f_builtins`)** — for accessing `print`, `len`, etc. (1 mark)
6. **Instruction Pointer (`f_lasti`)** — index of the next bytecode instruction. (1 mark)
7. **Cell and Free Variables** — for closures and nested functions. (1 mark)

**Neat Diagram (auto-credited with textual layout):**

```
+-------------------------------------+
|        STACK FRAME (PyFrame)        |
+-------------------------------------+
| 1. Code Object (function pointer)   |
| 2. Return Address                   |
| 3. f_locals (local variables)       |
| 4. f_globals (module globals)       |
| 5. f_builtins (built-in names)      |
| 6. f_lasti (next instruction)       |
| 7. Cell/Free vars (closures)        |
+-------------------------------------+
        ▲
        │  Stack Pointer (SP) points HERE
        │
   [Previous Frame Below]
```

---

#### (b) Write a Python recursive function to compute the sum of the first N natural numbers. Trace the call stack for `sum_n(4)` showing all push and pop operations. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

```python
def sum_n(n):
    if n == 0:          # Base case
        return 0
    else:
        return n + sum_n(n - 1)   # Recursive case

print(sum_n(4))
```

**Step-by-step Call Stack Trace for `sum_n(4)`:**

| Step | Operation | Stack (top → right) | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Call `sum_n(4)` | `[GLOBAL, sum_n(4)]` | — |
| 2 | Call `sum_n(3)` | `[GLOBAL, sum_n(4), sum_n(3)]` | — |
| 3 | Call `sum_n(2)` | `[GLOBAL, sum_n(4), sum_n(3), sum_n(2)]` | — |
| 4 | Call `sum_n(1)` | `[GLOBAL, sum_n(4), sum_n(3), sum_n(2), sum_n(1)]` | — |
| 5 | Call `sum_n(0)` | `[..., sum_n(1), sum_n(0)]` ← peak | — |
| 6 | Base case hit | Pop `sum_n(0)` | 0 |
| 7 | Compute `1 + 0` | Pop `sum_n(1)` | 1 |
| 8 | Compute `2 + 1` | Pop `sum_n(2)` | 3 |
| 9 | Compute `3 + 3` | Pop `sum_n(3)` | 6 |
| 10 | Compute `4 + 6` | Pop `sum_n(4)` | **10** |

(7 marks — 1 for code, 4 for trace steps, 1 for peak identification, 1 for final answer)

---

### ✏️ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) Differentiate between recursion and iteration. Explain how the call stack supports recursion with an example. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Recursion | Iteration |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Function calls itself | Loop constructs (`for`, `while`) |
| State Storage | **Call Stack** (implicit) | Loop variables (explicit) |
| Termination | Base case | Loop condition fails |
| Memory | $O(n)$ stack frames | $O(1)$ space |
| Speed | Slower (function call overhead) | Faster |
| Risk | **Stack Overflow** | Infinite loop |
| Readability | Elegant for tree/graph problems | Clear for linear tasks |

(4 marks for table)

**Call Stack Support for Recursion — Example (3 marks):**

For `factorial(3)`, the call stack holds partial computations (`n * factorial(n-1)`) on each frame. When `factorial(0)` returns 1, each frame multiplies by its stored `n` value as it pops. This **"deferred multiplication"** is exactly what makes recursion possible — without the stack, the partial result would be lost between calls.

---

#### (b) Consider the following Python code. Predict the output and trace the call stack step-by-step. (7 Marks)

```python
def mystery(x):
    if x < 1:
        return
    print(x)
    mystery(x - 1)
    print("Done", x)

mystery(3)
```

**Model Solution:**

**Output Prediction (1 mark):**

```
3
2
1
Done 1
Done 2
Done 3
```

**Call Stack Trace (6 marks):**

| Step | Call | Stack (top on right) | Print Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `mystery(3)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3)]` | — |
| 2 | `print(3)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3)]` | `3` |
| 3 | `mystery(2)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3), M(2)]` | — |
| 4 | `print(2)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3), M(2)]` | `2` |
| 5 | `mystery(1)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3), M(2), M(1)]` | — |
| 6 | `print(1)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3), M(2), M(1)]` | `1` |
| 7 | `mystery(0)` | `[..., M(1), M(0)]` | — (base case: returns immediately) |
| 8 | Pop `M(0)` | `[..., M(1)]` | — |
| 9 | `print("Done", 1)` | `[..., M(1)]` | `Done 1` |
| 10 | Pop `M(1)` | `[..., M(2)]` | — |
| 11 | `print("Done", 2)` | `[..., M(2)]` | `Done 2` |
| 12 | Pop `M(2)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3)]` | — |
| 13 | `print("Done", 3)` | `[GLOBAL, M(3)]` | `Done 3` |
| 14 | Pop `M(3)` | `[GLOBAL]` | — |

This is a classic **"print before and after recursion"** pattern, where the `print` after the recursive call only fires during the **unwinding** phase of the stack.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **call stack** is a **LIFO** structure managed by the Python interpreter — *not* the programmer.
- ✅ Each function call **pushes a stack frame**; each return **pops it**.
- ✅ A frame contains: **code pointer, return address, locals, globals reference, builtins, IP, and closures**.
- ✅ The default **recursion limit in Python is 1000** — exceeding it raises `RecursionError`.
- ✅ Recursion uses **O(n)** stack space; iteration uses **O(1)**.
- ✅ **Base case** is mandatory in recursion — it is what stops the stack from growing forever.
- ✅ **Tail recursion is NOT optimized in Python** (unlike Scheme/OCaml) — every call adds a frame.
- ✅ Recursion unwinding happens in **reverse order** of the calls (LIFO).
- ✅ The stack pointer (**SP**) always points to the **topmost active frame**.
- ✅ The `print` statements *after* a recursive call execute during the **pop** phase, not the push phase.
- ✅ Python's `traceback` module visualizes the call stack at the time of an exception — invaluable for debugging.
- ✅ Common exam trap: confusing the *call stack* with the *heap* (heap stores objects, stack stores frames).
- ✅ For iterative conversion of recursion, use an **explicit stack data structure** (manual simulation).
- ✅ Python does not support tail-call optimization due to frame preservation for tracebacks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursion and the Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recursion and the Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Recursion** is a computational problem-solving technique in which a function invokes a copy of itself, either directly or indirectly, in order to solve a smaller instance of the same problem. The recursion terminates when it reaches a **base case** (a trivially solvable condition) and unwinds through a series of **recursive cases** (self-referential calls).

In the context of **computer memory architecture**, every recursive invocation pushes a new **stack frame** (also called an *activation record*) onto the program's **call stack** — a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure maintained by the runtime environment. Each frame holds the function's local variables, return address, and parameters for that specific invocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Recursion is to be studied in conjunction with the **call stack** mechanism. Students must understand *what is stored*, *when it is pushed/popped*, and *how stack overflow occurs*. This combination is a high-weightage area in KTU End Semester Examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long line of **identical Russian nesting dolls (Matryoshka dolls)**. To solve the problem of "counting all the dolls," you do not count them all at once. Instead, you:

1. **Open the outermost doll** (this is a recursive call — a new sub-problem).
2. Check: *Is there a doll inside?* If not, return **1** (this is the **base case**).
3. If yes, you **set the current doll aside** and ask the next inner doll: "How many dolls from here onwards?" (this is the **recursive call**).
4. When the innermost doll finally answers, you **add 1** (for the doll you set aside) to its answer and pass the result back outward.

Each doll you open corresponds to a **stack frame** being **pushed** onto the call stack. When a doll finishes its job and reports back, that frame is **popped** off the stack. If there are too many dolls (e.g., an infinitely deep nesting), the stack runs out of physical space — this is **stack overflow**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **Base Case** | The terminating condition that does NOT trigger a recursive call. | Essential to prevent infinite recursion. |
| **Recursive Case** | The branch where the function calls itself with a smaller sub-problem. | Drives progress toward the base case. |
| **Stack Frame** | A memory block containing local variables, parameters, and return address for one function call. | Pushed on call, popped on return. |
| **Call Stack** | The runtime LIFO structure tracking active function invocations. | Grows downward in most architectures. |
| **Stack Overflow** | Runtime error when the call stack exceeds its allocated memory. | Caused by deep/missing-base-case recursion. |
| **Unwinding** | The phase where recursive calls return and stack frames are popped. | Where post-recursion logic executes. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Bolded for Emphasis):** The default call stack size in CPython (the reference Python interpreter) is **approximately 1000 frames** before a `RecursionError` is raised. This is a *runtime constant* enforced by the interpreter, not a hardware limit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Recursive Function — Three Logical Pillars

Every well-formed recursive function in Python (and in algorithmic thinking generally) consists of **three mandatory pillars**. Missing any one of them produces either an incorrect program or an infinite loop.

1. **Base Case (Termination Condition):**
   - The condition under which the function returns a hardcoded, known value.
   - *Why it matters:* Without it, the function would recurse infinitely and crash with `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded`.
   - *How to design:* Identify the smallest possible input for which the answer is trivially known (e.g., `0! = 1`, `sum of empty list = 0`).

2. **Recursive Case (Reduction Step):**
   - The branch where the function calls itself with a *strictly smaller* or *simpler* input.
   - *Why it matters:* This is the mechanism by which the problem shrinks toward the base case.
   - *How to design:* Express the answer for input `n` in terms of the answer for input `n-1`, `n/2`, or some other reduced form.

3. **Progress Guarantee (Convergence):**
   - A logical argument that the recursive call moves *closer* to the base case on every invocation.
   - *Why it matters:* KTU examiners frequently award marks for this implicit reasoning even when code is not required.

### 2.2 The Stack: A LIFO Discipline

The **call stack** operates on the **Last-In, First-Out (LIFO)** principle — the last frame pushed is the first frame popped. This mirrors the physical behavior of a stack of plates.

**Operational Rules of the Call Stack:**
- **PUSH:** On every function call (including recursive ones), a new stack frame is allocated and pushed onto the top of the stack.
- **POP:** When a function executes `return`, its frame is popped, and control returns to the **caller's frame** at the saved return address.
- **PEEK (Top of Stack):** The currently executing function is always the top frame.
- **GROWTH DIRECTION:** In most architectures (x86-64, ARM, and the CPython VM), the stack grows from **high memory addresses toward low memory addresses**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Applicable Context |
|---------|---------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Number of stack frames at depth d** | $\text{Frames}(d) = d + 1$ | $d$ = recursion depth | Direct recursion |
| **Time Complexity (single call, constant work)** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | $c$ = constant | Linear recursion (e.g., factorial) |
| **Solving the recurrence** | $T(n) = c \cdot n$ | — | Yields $O(n)$ |
| **Time Complexity (two recursive calls)** | $T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n/2) + c$ | Master Theorem case 1 | Divide-and-conquer (e.g., Fibonacci naive) |
| **Solving the recurrence** | $T(n) = O(n)$ | — | Linear time |
| **Space Complexity of recursion** | $S(n) = O(d)$ | $d$ = max depth | Equals maximum stack size |
| **Stack Overflow Condition** | $\text{depth} > \text{stack\_limit}$ | `stack_limit ≈ 1000` in CPython | Triggered by missing base case or excessive depth |
| **Tail Recursion Space (theoretical)** | $S(n) = O(1)$ | If compiler optimizes | CPython **does not** optimize tail calls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Space vs. Time Complexity:** Many students confuse the *time* complexity (how many operations) with the *space* complexity (how much memory). For recursion, **time complexity counts total calls**, but **space complexity counts the maximum depth of the call stack at any one moment**. This distinction is a frequent 2-mark question in Part A.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Recursive descent parsers use recursion to traverse nested grammar productions; the call stack naturally mirrors parse-tree depth.
- **Operating Systems:** Kernel stack management for interrupts and context switches is implemented via stack frames analogous to function calls.
- **Artificial Intelligence:** Depth-First Search (DFS), backtracking algorithms (N-Queens, Sudoku solver), and minimax tree search all rely on the recursion + stack paradigm.
- **File Systems:** Recursive directory traversal (e.g., `os.walk` in Python) uses the call stack implicitly to backtrack from deep subdirectories.
- **Embedded Systems:** Recursion is generally *avoided* in deeply embedded firmware because stack memory is severely limited (often 512 bytes to 4 KB), making stack overflow a critical concern.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Factorial: $n!$

**Problem Statement:** Compute $n! = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \dots \times 1$ using recursion.

**Recursive Definition (Mathematical Foundation):**
$$n! = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \quad \text{(base case)} \\ n \times (n-1)! & \text{if } n > 0 \quad \text{(recursive case)} \end{cases}$$

**Python Implementation:**

```python
def factorial(n):
    # Base case: the smallest input with a known answer
    if n == 0:
        return 1
    # Recursive case: express n! in terms of (n-1)!
    return n * factorial(n - 1)
```

**Stack Frame Trace for `factorial(3)`:**

We trace the call stack step by step. The arrow $\rightarrow$ indicates the *direction of execution*, and the stack frames grow from top (most recent) to bottom (oldest).

| Step | Action | Call Stack (top → bottom) | Return Value |
|------|--------|--------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Call `factorial(3)` | `[factorial(3)]` | — |
| 2 | Inside, call `factorial(2)` | `[factorial(3), factorial(2)]` | — |
| 3 | Inside, call `factorial(1)` | `[factorial(3), factorial(2), factorial(1)]` | — |
| 4 | Inside, call `factorial(0)` | `[factorial(3), factorial(2), factorial(1), factorial(0)]` | — |
| 5 | Base case: return 1 | — | `1` (pops factorial(0)) |
| 6 | Compute $1 \times 1$ | `[factorial(3), factorial(2)]` | `1` (pops factorial(1)) |
| 7 | Compute $2 \times 1$ | `[factorial(3)]` | `2` (pops factorial(2)) |
| 8 | Compute $3 \times 2$ | `[]` | `6` (pops factorial(3)) |

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

The recurrence relation for factorial is:

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + c$$

where $c$ represents the constant work done at each level (one multiplication, one comparison). We solve it by **repeated substitution (unrolling)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c \\
     &= [T(n-2) + c] + c = T(n-2) + 2c \\
     &= [T(n-3) + c] + 2c = T(n-3) + 3c \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= T(n-k) + k \cdot c
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $k = n$ (reaching the base case $T(0) = c_0$):

$$
T(n) = T(0) + n \cdot c = c_0 + n \cdot c
$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = O(n)$ — linear time. There are exactly $n+1$ stack frames at maximum depth, so **space complexity is also $O(n)$**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fibonacci: $F(n)$

**Problem Statement:** Compute the $n$-th Fibonacci number, defined as $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)$, with $F(0) = 0$ and $F(1) = 1$.

**Python Implementation:**

```python
def fibonacci(n):
    # Two base cases
    if n == 0:
        return 0
    if n == 1:
        return 1
    # Recursive case: two self-calls
    return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2)
```

**Recurrence Relation:**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c$$

This is a **non-homogeneous linear recurrence**. The dominant term grows as the golden ratio $\varphi$ to the $n$-th power:

$$
T(n) = O(\varphi^n), \quad \text{where} \quad \varphi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap — The Fibonacci Space Complexity Surprise:** Although the *time* complexity is exponential, the *space* complexity is only $O(n)$, not $O(2^n)$. Reason: at any given moment, the call stack only holds one *path* from root to a leaf in the recursion tree. The maximum depth is $n$, never $2^n$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Recursion vs. Iteration

| Property | Recursion | Iteration |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Termination** | Base case | Loop condition (e.g., `while n > 0`) |
| **Memory** | Uses call stack ($O(d)$) | Uses loop variables ($O(1)$) |
| **Readability** | Naturally expresses divide-and-conquer | Often more verbose for tree/graph problems |
| **Speed** | Slower (function call overhead) | Faster (no call overhead) |
| **Stack Overflow Risk** | **Yes** (deep recursion) | **No** (bounded by loop counter) |
| **Mutual Recursion** | Supported (A calls B calls A) | Requires explicit state machine |
| **KTU Preference** | For tree/graph/divide-and-conquer | For simple accumulations |

### 3.4 Stack Overflow — Formal Derivation of the Failure Mode

Let $S_{\text{max}}$ denote the maximum stack size allocated by the OS/interpreter. The default CPython limit is approximately **1000 frames**. A stack overflow occurs when:

$$
\text{Recursion Depth} > S_{\text{max}}
$$

For factorial, the depth equals $n$, so overflow occurs at $n \approx 1000$. For an exponentially branching recursion (e.g., Ackermann function), the depth can reach $S_{\text{max}}$ with very small inputs (e.g., `Ackermann(3, 6)` is enough to overflow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Note (Exhaustive Step):** In the recurrence $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$, the "+$c$" represents the **constant-time work** done in the current frame (one multiplication, one comparison, one subtraction). It is *not* dependent on $n$. This is why unrolling yields a *linear* sum $c + c + c + \dots$ ($n$ times), giving $O(n)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Call Stack Lifecycle for `factorial(3)`

```mermaid
    callMain --> push3["PUSH: factorial(3), n=3, return_addr=main"]

    eval3 --> push2["PUSH: factorial(2), n=2, return_addr=line_in_f3"]
    push2 --> eval2["n==0? NO -> recursive call"]
    eval2 --> push1["PUSH: factorial(1), n=1, return_addr=line_in_f2"]
    push1 --> eval1["n==0? NO -> recursive call"]
    eval1 --> push0["PUSH: factorial(0), n=0, return_addr=line_in_f1"]
    push0 --> baseHit{"n==0? YES"}
    baseHit --> ret0["RETURN 1 -> POP frame factorial(0)"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Stack Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph memSpace["Virtual Memory Layout (High -> Low Addresses)"]
        direction TB

    end

    frameN -. "enlarged view" .-> frameDetail

    gapA ~~~ frameN
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Recursion vs. Iteration State Machine

```mermaid

        r1(["input n"]) --> r2{"base case?"}
        r2 -- "yes" --> r3["return hardcoded value"]
        r2 -- "no" --> r4["PUSH frame onto call stack"]
        r4 --> r5["recursive call with smaller input"]
        r5 --> r2
        r3 --> r6(["final answer"])
    end

    subgraph iterSide["ITERATION PATH"]
        i1(["input n"]) --> i2["initialize accumulator = identity"]
        i2 --> i3{"loop condition?"}

        i3 -- "false" --> i5(["final answer = accumulator"])
```
