<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between recursion and dynamic programming. When would you prefer dynamic programming over plain recursion?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Recursion | Dynamic Programming |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| Definition | Function calling itself to solve smaller sub-problems. | Optimised recursion that stores results of overlapping sub-problems. |
| Sub-problems | Typically disjoint (independent). | Overlapping (repeated). |
| Time complexity | Often exponential, e.g., $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$. | Polynomial, e.g., $\mathcal{O}(n)$ or $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$. |
| Memory | Only call stack. | Stack + cache/table. |

DP is preferred when the problem exhibits **both** optimal substructure and overlapping sub-problems, e.g., Fibonacci, Binomial Coefficient, 0/1 Knapsack, and Longest Common Subsequence. **(1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark for conditions, 1 mark for examples.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is memoisation? How does it differ from tabulation in dynamic programming?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Memoisation** is a top-down approach to DP where a recursive function stores the result of each sub-problem in a cache (dictionary/array) the first time it is computed. Subsequent calls simply return the cached value.

**Tabulation** is a bottom-up iterative approach where a table is filled starting from the smallest base case up to the required solution, without using recursion.

| Feature | Memoisation | Tabulation |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Direction | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
| Style | Recursive | Iterative |
| Stack usage | Yes | No |
| Sub-problems solved | Only those required | All sub-problems in the table |
| Constant factor | Slightly higher (function-call overhead) | Slightly lower (loop overhead) |

**(1 mark definition, 1 mark tabulation definition, 1 mark comparison table.)**

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions.

---

#### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks) — *FIBONACCI DEEP DIVE* `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Write a Python function to compute the $n$-th Fibonacci number using naive recursion. Show the recursion tree for $F(4)$ and derive its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Rewrite the same function using dynamic programming (tabulation approach). Derive the new time and space complexity, and explain why this is more efficient. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Naive Recursive Fibonacci — 7 marks**

**Python Code (3 marks):**

```python
def fib_naive(n):
    if n <= 1:                # Base case
        return n
    return fib_naive(n-1) + fib_naive(n-2)
```

**Recursion Tree for $F(4)$ (2 marks):**

```
                  F(4)
                /      \
            F(3)        F(2)
           /    \       /   \
        F(2)  F(1)   F(1)  F(0)
        /  \
     F(1) F(0)
```

Total calls = **9** recursive invocations for $F(4)$.

**Time Complexity Derivation (2 marks):**

The recurrence relation is:
$$T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + c, \qquad T(0) = T(1) = 1$$

Solving via the characteristic equation $x^2 = x + 1$ gives roots $\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ and $\hat{\phi} \approx -0.618$. The dominant root determines the growth:

$$T(n) = \mathcal{O}(\phi^n) = \mathcal{O}(1.618^n) \approx \mathcal{O}(2^n)$$

---

**(b) Tabulation-Based Fibonacci — 7 marks**

**Python Code (3 marks):**

```python
def fib_dp(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)        # Allocate table
    dp[0], dp[1] = 0, 1       # Initialise base cases
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
    return dp[n]
```

**Complexity Derivation (2 marks):**

The for-loop runs $n-1$ times, each iteration performs $\mathcal{O}(1)$ work:
$$T_{\text{DP}}(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)$$

Auxiliary space for the table:
$$S_{\text{DP}}(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)$$

**Efficiency Explanation (2 marks):**

In the naive version, $F(k)$ is recomputed once for every distinct path leading to it — leading to exponential redundancy. In the DP version, each $F(k)$ is computed **exactly once** and stored. We trade a small $\mathcal{O}(n)$ memory overhead for a massive reduction from $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time, satisfying the *overlapping sub-problems* property of dynamic programming.

---

#### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) — *BINOMIAL COEFFICIENT & DECISION SKILL* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the two essential properties a problem must satisfy to be solved using dynamic programming. Give two examples of problems that satisfy these properties and two that do not. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python function to compute the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ using dynamic programming. Derive its time and space complexity and compare it with the naive recursive version. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Two Pillars of DP — 7 marks**

**Property 1: Optimal Substructure (3 marks)**

A problem has *optimal substructure* if the globally optimal solution can be constructed by combining the optimal solutions of its sub-problems. Formally, if $\text{OPT}(P)$ denotes the optimum of problem $P$ decomposed into sub-problems $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_m$:

$$\text{OPT}(P) = \text{combine}\bigl(\text{OPT}(P_1), \text{OPT}(P_2), \ldots, \text{OPT}(P_m)\bigr)$$

**Property 2: Overlapping Sub-problems (2 marks)**

The recursive formulation must solve the *same* sub-problem multiple times. The total number of **distinct** sub-problems $C(n)$ must be much smaller than the total number of **total** recursive calls $B(n)$ in the naive approach.

**Examples (2 marks):**

| ✅ Satisfies both (use DP) | ❌ Does not satisfy (use plain recursion) |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Fibonacci $F(n) = F(n-1)+F(n-2)$ | Tower of Hanoi (disjoint sub-problems) |
| Binomial $\binom{n}{k}$ | Factorial $n!$ (no overlap) |
| 0/1 Knapsack | Linear Search |
| Longest Common Subsequence | Merge Sort (Divide & Conquer, disjoint) |

---

**(b) DP Binomial Coefficient — 7 marks**

**Python Code (3 marks):**

```python
def binom_dp(n, k):
    # Create Pascal's triangle as a 2D table
    C = [[0] * (k + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
    for i in range(n + 1):
        for j in range(min(i, k)):
            if j == 0 or j == i:
                C[i][j] = 1          # Boundary condition
            else:
                C[i][j] = C[i-1][j-1] + C[i-1][j]   # Pascal's rule
    return C[n][k]
```

**Complexity Derivation (2 marks):**

- Outer loop runs $n+1$ times, inner loop up to $k+1$ times.
- Total cell operations: $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot k)$.

$$T_{\text{DP}}(n,k) = \mathcal{O}(n \cdot k), \qquad S_{\text{DP}}(n,k) = \mathcal{O}(n \cdot k)$$

**Comparison with Naive Recursion (2 marks):**

| Metric | Naive Recursion | DP Tabulation |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Time | $\mathcal{O}\!\bigl(2^n\bigr)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot k)$ |
| Space (stack) | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ stack + $\mathcal{O}(nk)$ table |
| Stack overflow | Yes, for $n > 1000$ | No |
| Memo of Pascal | None | Every cell stored once |

The DP version is exponentially faster for large $n$, and is the only feasible option when $n > 30$ or so.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting base case:** Writing `return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)` without `if n <= 1: return n` causes infinite recursion and **zero marks** for the function.
> 2. **Confusing memoisation with caching the *final* answer only:** Examiners want you to cache **every** sub-problem, not just the last one. Always show the cache being populated.
> 3. **Writing the wrong complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ for naive Fibonacci is correct, but writing $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for naive recursion will cost 2 marks. Read carefully.
> 4. **Forgetting boundary conditions in DP tables:** Off-by-one errors in `dp[0]` and `dp[1]` initialisation are the #1 reason students lose marks.
> 5. **Using `|` inside table cells:** It breaks the markdown table. Always use `\vert` for absolute value.
> 6. **Calling it "Dynamic Programming Language":** It is an *algorithmic technique*, not a language. This mistake alone can cost 1 mark.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ **Recursion =** function calls itself, terminates via **base case**.
- ✅ **DP =** recursion + **memory** (cache or table) for overlapping sub-problems.
- ✅ **DP needs BOTH:** *Optimal substructure* AND *overlapping sub-problems*.
- ✅ **Memoisation = Top-Down** (recursive + cache). **Tabulation = Bottom-Up** (iterative + table).
- ✅ **Naive Fibonacci:** $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(2^n)$, dominated by golden ratio $\phi \approx 1.618$.
- ✅ **DP Fibonacci:** $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)$, $S(n) = \mathcal{O}(1)$ with space optimisation.
- ✅ **Binomial $\binom{n}{k}$:** Naive $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ vs DP $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot k)$.
- ✅ **Tower of Hanoi, Factorial, Merge Sort** → pure recursion (no overlap, no DP needed).
- ✅ **LCS, Knapsack, Edit Distance, Matrix Chain Multiplication, Coin Change** → classic DP.
- ✅ **Mnemonic "OOPS":** **O**ptimal substructure + **O**verlapping sub-**P**roblem**S** → DP.
- ✅ **Examiner's mantra:** Always mention *both* properties, *both* complexities (time + space), and provide a **recursion tree or DP table** diagram to score full marks.
- ✅ **Space optimisation trick:** For Fibonacci-style recurrences, only the last two values are needed — use two variables instead of an array.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greedy Algorithm Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST105 - ALGORITHMIC THINKING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Greedy Algorithm Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greedy Algorithm:** A **Greedy Algorithm** is an algorithmic paradigm that follows the problem-solving heuristic of making the **locally optimal choice at each stage** with the hope of finding a **global optimum**. At every decision point, the algorithm greedily selects the best immediate (local) solution without reconsidering previous choices, thereby constructing the solution incrementally.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome mapping for **CO4 – Design algorithms using suitable algorithmic strategies**), a greedy algorithm is formally defined as one that satisfies two key mathematical properties:

1. **Greedy-Choice Property**: A globally optimal solution can be arrived at by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice.
2. **Optimal Substructure**: An optimal solution to the problem contains within it optimal solutions to sub-problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard 2-mark answer):** *"A greedy algorithm is a method for solving optimization problems by making the best possible choice at each step, hoping these local optima lead to a global optimum. It never reconsiders its choices."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🍔 The Burger Counter Analogy:**
> Imagine you are very hungry and you have **₹500** to spend at a food court. You walk past counters in a single pass (no going back). At every counter, you buy the *largest, most filling item* that fits your remaining budget. You never return to a previous counter, even if a later purchase makes you realize that an earlier item was unnecessary. This *short-sighted, locally maximal* decision-making is exactly how a **Greedy Algorithm** works. Sometimes you end up with a full stomach (optimal), but sometimes you leave a tiny bit of money unused because an earlier greedy purchase blocked a better combination (suboptimal).

**A more formal real-world mapping:**

| Real-World Scenario | Greedy Algorithm Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Choosing the **shortest** checkout line at a supermarket | Locally optimal choice at each stage |
| Giving **change** using the largest possible denominations first | Coin Change (Canonical Systems) |
| Selecting the **earliest finishing** meeting that fits your calendar | Activity Selection Problem |
| Always picking the **cheapest** item that still solves your problem | Minimum Spanning Tree (Kruskal's) |

## 1.3 Classification Within Algorithmic Paradigms

```
Algorithm Design Paradigms
├── Brute Force
├── Divide and Conquer
├── Dynamic Programming
├── Greedy Algorithms   ◄── THIS MODULE
├── Backtracking
└── Branch and Bound
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Dynamic Programming (DP):** Greedy algorithms are *top-down* and *one-pass*; they never revisit decisions. Dynamic Programming is *bottom-up* (or memoized top-down) and explores *all* sub-problems. Greedy is faster but applicable to a **strictly smaller** class of problems.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> In greedy algorithm analysis, the following standard complexity metrics are routinely used (KTU Expected Knowledge):
> - **Time Complexity (T(n))**: The number of elementary operations as a function of input size *n*.
> - **Space Complexity (S(n))**: Auxiliary memory consumed (excluding input storage).
> - **Approximation Ratio (ρ)**: For NP-hard problems, the worst-case ratio between greedy's output and the true optimum.
> - **Amortized Cost**: Average cost per operation over a sequence of greedy steps.
> - **Greedy Factor / Competitive Ratio**: Used in online algorithms.
> - Standard sorting bound: **O(n log n)** (Merge Sort, Heap Sort).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Structure of a Greedy Algorithm

A greedy algorithm can be decomposed into **five logical phases**:

1. **Problem Formulation as an Optimization Problem:**  
   Define the objective function $f(x)$ to be *minimized* (or *maximized*) and the constraint set $\mathcal{C}$ of feasible solutions.

2. **Identification of Candidate Set (Universe of Elements):**  
   Let $\mathcal{U} = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n\}$ be the set of all candidate elements (edges, activities, denominations, jobs, etc.).

3. **Selection Function (Greedy Choice Rule):**  
   Define a *selection function* $S: 2^{\mathcal{U}} \rightarrow \mathcal{U}$ that picks the most "promising" element according to a *local* criterion (smallest weight, largest profit, earliest finish time, etc.).

4. **Feasibility Check:**  
   A *feasibility function* $F$ verifies whether adding the selected element keeps the partial solution within the constraints $\mathcal{C}$.

5. **Objective Function Evaluation & Solution Assembly:**  
   The *objective function* $z$ computes the value of the assembled solution. The algorithm terminates when no more candidates can be added feasibly.

## 2.2 The Two Mathematical Pillars of Greedy Correctness

### 2.2.1 Greedy-Choice Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Greedy-Choice Property):**  
> A problem exhibits the *greedy-choice property* if a globally optimal solution can be constructed by making a sequence of locally optimal choices, **without** needing to reconsider any prior decision.

**Mathematical Statement:**  
Let $\mathcal{O}$ be a globally optimal solution for instance $I$. There exists an optimal solution $\mathcal{O}'$ that contains the greedy choice $c_1$ as its first element.

$$
\exists \, \mathcal{O}' : \quad \text{profit}(\mathcal{O}') = \text{profit}(\mathcal{O}) \;\land\; c_1 \in \mathcal{O}'
$$

### 2.2.2 Optimal Substructure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Optimal Substructure):**  
> A problem has *optimal substructure* if the optimal solution to the whole problem can be expressed as a combination of (a) one greedy choice $c_1$, and (b) an optimal solution to the sub-problem that results after removing $c_1$.

$$
\text{OPT}(I) = \text{combine}\bigl(c_1, \; \text{OPT}(I \setminus c_1)\bigr)
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **canonical greedy algorithms**, their time complexities, greedy choice rules, and applications. This is *high-yield* for KTU Part A and Part B questions.

| Algorithm | Greedy Choice Rule | Time Complexity | Optimal Substructure Type | KTU Expected Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Activity Selection** | Earliest finish time | $O(n \log n)$ | Maximize count of non-overlapping | 7–14 |
| **Fractional Knapsack** | Highest value/weight ratio | $O(n \log n)$ | Maximize fractional value | 7–14 |
| **0/1 Knapsack** | Highest value/weight ratio | Not optimal! | Use DP instead | Conceptual |
| **Huffman Coding** | Lowest frequency merge | $O(n \log n)$ | Minimize weighted path length | 7 |
| **Dijkstra (SSSP)** | Closest unvisited vertex | $O((V+E)\log V)$ with heap | Minimize distance | 7–14 |
| **Prim's (MST)** | Minimum weight edge from tree | $O(E \log V)$ | Minimize total weight | 7–14 |
| **Kruskal's (MST)** | Minimum weight edge globally | $O(E \log E)$ | Minimize total weight | 7–14 |
| **Job Sequencing with Deadlines** | Highest profit, earliest deadline | $O(n^2)$ or $O(n \log n)$ | Maximize profit | 7 |
| **Coin Change (Canonical)** | Largest denomination ≤ remainder | $O(n)$ | Minimize coin count | 3–7 |
| **Minimum Coins (Indian/Canonical)** | Largest coin ≤ amount | $O(n)$ | Minimize number of coins | 3–7 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Time Complexity Bound for Greedy Algorithms:**  
> Most greedy algorithms reduce to sorting or priority-queue operations, leading to a standard bound of:
> $$T(n) = O(n \log n)$$
> Where **$n$** = number of candidate elements.

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions and Engineering Utility

- **Boundary Condition 1 (Empty Input):** The algorithm must return a valid (possibly empty) solution when $n = 0$.
- **Boundary Condition 2 (Single Element):** When $n = 1$, the greedy choice is trivially optimal.
- **Boundary Condition 3 (No Feasible Solution):** The feasibility function must return $\bot$ (failure) rather than crashing.
- **Boundary Condition 4 (Tie-Breaking):** When two candidates are equal under the greedy criterion, a deterministic tie-break rule must be defined (e.g., lower index wins).

**Real-World Engineering Applications:**

| Field | Application | Algorithm Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Routing** | OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) | Dijkstra's |
| **Telecommunications** | Minimum-cost network layout | Kruskal's / Prim's |
| **Data Compression** | ZIP, JPEG entropy coding | Huffman Coding |
| **CPU Scheduling** | SJF (Shortest Job First) scheduling | Greedy by burst time |
| **Database Query Optimization** | Join order selection | Greedy heuristics |
| **AI Game Playing** | Chess evaluation heuristics | Greedy evaluation |
| **VLSI Circuit Design** | Wire-length minimization | MST-based routing |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Proof of Optimality for the Activity Selection Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Given $n$ activities with start times $s_i$ and finish times $f_i$, select the **maximum number** of mutually non-overlapping activities that can be performed by a single resource.

### 3.1.1 Greedy Strategy

**Sort** all activities by their **finish time** in ascending order. Then iteratively select the next activity whose start time is $\geq$ the finish time of the last selected activity.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Proof of Correctness (Exchange Argument)

Let the greedy algorithm produce the set:
$$G = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k\}$$

And let an arbitrary optimal solution be:
$$O = \{b_1, b_2, \dots, b_m\}$$

**Step 1 (Base Case):**  
Both $G$ and $O$ are sorted by finish time. The first activity in $G$ is $a_1$, which has the *earliest finish time* among all activities.

**Step 2 (Exchange Lemma):**  
Consider $b_1$, the first activity in $O$. Since $a_1$ finishes *no later* than $b_1$:

$$
f(a_1) \leq f(b_1)
$$

**Step 3 (Replace $b_1$ with $a_1$ in $O$):**  
Construct a new optimal solution $O'$ by replacing $b_1$ with $a_1$:

$$
O' = \{a_1, b_2, b_3, \dots, b_m\}
$$

This replacement is **valid** because every activity in $O'$ that starts after $a_1$ also started after $b_1$ (since $f(a_1) \leq f(b_1)$).

**Step 4 (Inductive Step):**  
The sub-problem of selecting activities from the remaining set (those starting $\geq f(a_1)$) is identical to the original problem. By induction, the greedy choice remains optimal for this sub-problem.

**Step 5 (Conclusion):**  
By induction on the number of iterations:

$$
\boxed{|G| = |O| = m = k}
$$

That is, the greedy solution is **cardinality-optimal** for the Activity Selection Problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fractional Knapsack — Step-by-Step Value Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given $n$ items, each with weight $w_i$ and value $v_i$, and a knapsack of capacity $W$, maximize the total value. Unlike the 0/1 knapsack, items may be **fractionally** taken.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Algorithm

**Step 1 — Compute Value-to-Weight Ratio:**  
For each item $i$:

$$
r_i = \frac{v_i}{w_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Sort in Descending Order of $r_i$:**  
Rearrange items such that:

$$
r_1 \geq r_2 \geq r_3 \geq \dots \geq r_n
$$

**Step 3 — Iteratively Fill the Knapsack:**  
Initialize: $\text{remaining} \leftarrow W, \; \text{total\_value} \leftarrow 0$

For each item $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:

$$
\text{take}_i = \min(w_i, \; \text{remaining})
$$

$$
\text{total\_value} \leftarrow \text{total\_value} + \text{take}_i \cdot r_i
$$

$$
\text{remaining} \leftarrow \text{remaining} - \text{take}_i
$$

Stop when $\text{remaining} = 0$.

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**  
- Capacity $W = 50$ kg
- Item A: $w = 10$, $v = 60$
- Item B: $w = 20$, $v = 100$
- Item C: $w = 30$, $v = 120$

**Step 1 — Ratios:**

$$
r_A = \frac{60}{10} = 6.0
$$
$$
r_B = \frac{100}{20} = 5.0
$$
$$
r_C = \frac{120}{30} = 4.0
$$

**Step 2 — Sort (A, B, C):**

**Step 3 — Fill:**

| Step | Item | Weight Available | Weight Taken | Value Added | Remaining Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 50 | 10 | $10 \times 6 = 60$ | 40 |
| 2 | B | 40 | 20 | $20 \times 5 = 100$ | 20 |
| 3 | C | 20 | 20 | $20 \times 4 = 80$ | 0 |

**Final Maximum Value:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Value} = 60 + 100 + 80 = 240}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Huffman Coding Tree Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given a set of $n$ characters with frequencies $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n$, construct a **binary prefix code** with minimum expected codeword length.

**Step 1:** Create a leaf node for each character with weight = frequency. Insert all into a **min-heap** (priority queue) $\mathcal{H}$.

**Step 2:** While $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert \geq 2$:

$$
\text{Extract } x = \min(\mathcal{H})
$$
$$
\text{Extract } y = \min(\mathcal{H})
$$
$$
z.\text{weight} = x.\text{weight} + y.\text{weight}
$$
$$
z.\text{left} = x, \quad z.\text{right} = y
$$
$$
\text{Insert } z \text{ into } \mathcal{H}
$$

**Step 3:** The remaining node in $\mathcal{H}$ is the root of the Huffman tree.

**Optimal Code Length (Shannon Entropy Bound):**  
The expected codeword length $L$ of any prefix code satisfies:

$$
H(S) \leq L < H(S) + 1
$$

Where $H(S)$ is the **Shannon Entropy**:

$$
H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Greedy vs. Dynamic Programming

| Property | Greedy Algorithm | Dynamic Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Strategy** | Local optimum at each step | Global optimum over all sub-problems |
| **Reversibility** | Choices are *final* (no backtrack) | Choices can be revised via sub-problems |
| **Direction** | Top-down, one-pass | Bottom-up or top-down with memoization |
| **When to Use** | Greedy-choice property + optimal substructure | Only optimal substructure |
| **Time** | Generally $O(n \log n)$ | Generally $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ |
| **Space** | $O(1)$ to $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ (table) |
| **Example – 0/1 Knapsack** | ❌ Fails | ✅ Works |
| **Example – Fractional Knapsack** | ✅ Works | ✅ Works |
| **Example – Shortest Path (DAG)** | ✅ Dijkstra | ✅ Bellman-Ford |

## 3.5 Counter-Example: Why Greedy Fails for 0/1 Knapsack

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** The 0/1 Knapsack problem does *not* satisfy the **greedy-choice property** (it only has optimal substructure). Therefore, greedy by value/weight ratio produces **suboptimal** results.

**Numerical Counter-Example:**  
- Capacity $W = 10$
- Item 1: $w = 6, v = 8$ → $r = 1.33$
- Item 2: $w = 5, v = 5$ → $r = 1.00$
- Item 3: $w = 5, v = 5$ → $r = 1.00$

**Greedy Choice (by ratio):**  
Picks Item 1 first ($r = 1.33$). Then capacity left = 4. Cannot fit Items 2 or 3.

$$
\text{Greedy Total Value} = 8
$$

**Optimal (DP) Choice:**  
Picks Items 2 + 3. Total weight = 10 ≤ capacity.

$$
\text{Optimal Total Value} = 10
$$

**Conclusion:** Greedy *failed* because it failed to anticipate the combinatorial value of smaller items.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: General Greedy Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Input Set of Candidates U]) --> initA[Initialize Empty Solution Set S]
    initA --> sortA[Sort U by Greedy Criterion]
    sortA --> loopStart{For each candidate x in U}
    loopStart -- Yes --> feasCheck{Is S union x Feasible?}
    feasCheck -- Yes --> addCand[Add x to S]
    addCand --> loopStart
    feasCheck -- No --> skipCand[Skip / Reject x]
    skipCand --> loopStart
    loopStart -- No --> outputA[Output Solution Set S]
    outputA --> endA([END])
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Greedy Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer
        candSet[Candidate Set U]
        constSet[Constraint Set C]
        objFunc[Objective Function f]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer
        selectMod[Selection Function S]
        feasMod[Feasibility Checker F]
        greedyLoop[Greedy Decision Engine]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer
        partialSol[Partial Solution]
        finalSol[Final Optimal Solution]
    end

    candSet --> selectMod
    constSet --> feasMod
    objFunc --> greedyLoop
    selectMod --> greedyLoop
    feasMod --> greedyLoop
    greedyLoop --> partialSol
    partialSol --> greedyLoop
    greedyLoop --> finalSol
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Decision Tree — Greedy Choice at Each Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Stage 0: Full Candidate Set U] --> node2[Apply Greedy Selection Function S]
    node2 --> node3[Selected Element c1 with Best Local Score]
    node3 --> node4{Feasibility Check F: Does c1 satisfy constraints?}
    node4 -- Yes --> node5[Add c1 to Solution]
    node4 -- No --> node6[Discard c1]
    node5 --> node7[Remaining Set U minus c1]
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8{Is U empty OR No feasible element?}
    node8 -- No --> node2
    node8 -- Yes --> node9[Output Assembled Solution]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Greedy Algorithm — Problem Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Greedy Algorithms] --> br1[Optimization on Intervals]
    rootA --> br2[Graph Algorithms]
    rootA --> br3[Scheduling]
    rootA --> br4[Encoding]

    br1 --> leaf1[Activity Selection]
    br1 --> leaf2[Interval Scheduling Max]

    br2 --> leaf3[Prim MST]
    br2 --> leaf4[Kruskal MST]
    br2 --> leaf5[Dijkstra SSSP]

    br3 --> leaf6[Job Sequencing with Deadlines]
    br3 --> leaf7[Coin Change Canonical]
    br3 --> leaf8[Fractional Knapsack]

    br4 --> leaf9[Huffman Coding]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Greedy Property Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    propStart([Verify Greedy Applicability]) --> chk1{Greedy-Choice Property?}
    chk1 -- No --> rejectProp[Use Dynamic Programming Instead]
    chk1 -- Yes --> chk2{Optimal Substructure?}
    chk2 -- No --> rejectProp
    chk2 -- Yes --> approveProp[Proceed with Greedy Strategy]
    approveProp --> designAlg[Design Selection Function S]
    designAlg --> proveAlg[Prove Correctness via Exchange Argument or Induction]
    proveAlg --> doneProp([Algorithm Validated])
```
